111110.title: Soybean Farming
111110.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~280K soybean farms, but top quartile produces ~80% of output (commodity scale). Subsidy-dependent (ARC/PLC), weather/price volatile. Precision-ag AI offers <2pp margin lift on seed/chem optimization (USDA ERS 2023). Land-rich, labor-light businesses don't fit roll-up labor-arbitrage thesis.

111120.title: Oilseed (except Soybean) Farming
111120.rationale: Canola/sunflower/flax — niche commodity, ~12K farms (USDA NASS). Same dynamics as soybeans: subsidy + weather + commodity pricing. No exit market beyond strategic ag (Bunge/ADM/Cargill don't buy producer farms; they buy elevators/crushers).

111130.title: Dry Pea and Bean Farming
111130.rationale: USDA Census: ~6K farms concentrated in MT/ND/ID. Pulse crop demand growing (plant protein), but margins still commodity-thin. Small enough sector that PE doesn't bother; strategic acquirers are pulse processors not farms.

111140.title: Wheat Farming
111140.rationale: USDA: ~110K wheat farms, ARC/PLC subsidies critical (avg ~$40K/farm). Margin compression severe — 2024 USDA ERS net cash income forecast down 25% YoY. Precision-ag adoption already ~60% on large farms (NASS Farm Computer Usage 2023). No PE roll-up logic.

111150.title: Corn Farming
111150.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~300K corn farms, largest crop by acreage (~90M acres). Highest precision-ag adoption (John Deere See & Spray, Climate FieldView ~70% large-farm penetration). Still: commodity, subsidy-dependent (~$5B/yr ARC/PLC), weather binary. Producer-level roll-ups historically failed (UAP/Pinnacle examples).

111160.title: Rice Farming
111160.rationale: USDA: ~5,500 rice farms, concentrated in AR/CA/LA/MS/TX/MO — relatively concentrated already. Heavy capital (laser-leveling, water infra). Subsidy-critical, water-rights constrained. USRice/Riceland (co-op) dominates aggregation; PE roll-up entry blocked by cooperative structure.

111191.title: Oilseed and Grain Combination Farming
111191.rationale: Mixed-rotation farms (corn-soybean primarily), Midwest. ~70K operations per USDA Census 2022. Same commodity dynamics — rotation efficiency already captured by precision-ag platforms. No M&A multiple arbitrage; family-farm succession dominates exits.

111199.title: All Other Grain Farming
111199.rationale: Barley, oats, sorghum, rye, etc. — small acreage crops. ~30K farms collectively. Sub-scale to attract institutional capital; specialty malt-barley shows some premium but volumes tiny.

111211.title: Potato Farming
111211.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~2,400 potato farms (consolidated), concentrated in ID/WA/WI. Capital-intensive storage, processor-contract dominant (Lamb Weston/McCain/Simplot buy ~80%). Some labor for sorting/grading where AI vision helps. Exit market = strategic processors not financial buyers.

111219.title: Other Vegetable (except Potato) and Melon Farming
111219.rationale: ~33K farms (USDA Census 2022), highly labor-intensive (H-2A wages rising 6-8%/yr per BLS). Some AI/robotics opportunity (Carbon Robotics weeding, harvest robots — early stage). Fragmented but seasonal/perishable risk caps multiples; produce M&A typically 4-6x EBITDA (per AgriCapital/Capstone advisory).

111310.title: Orange Groves
111310.rationale: FL citrus collapsed (citrus greening/HLB disease) — production down 90% since 2004 per USDA NASS. CA orange industry stable but mature. Industry shrinkage, not growth. No PE thesis on declining-acreage commodity.

111320.title: Citrus (except Orange) Groves
111320.rationale: Mandarins/lemons/limes — CA-dominated (Wonderful Citrus vertically integrated). Mandarin segment grew with Cuties/Halos branding but consolidated already. Limited fragmented mid-market for PE.

111331.title: Apple Orchards
111331.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~7,800 apple orchards, WA dominant. Robotic harvesting/sorting active R&D (Abundant Robotics, FFRobotics). Labor 40%+ of costs (H-2A). Some PE interest (Permira/Sun Capital looked at packers, not growers). Grower-packer combos more PE-viable than pure orchards.

111332.title: Grape Vineyards
111332.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~8,900 vineyards. Wine-grape land trades on lifestyle premium (Napa $300K+/acre), distorting financial multiples. PE active in wine brands (Duckhorn, Constellation deals) not vineyards specifically. Table/raisin grapes more commodity-like.

111333.title: Strawberry Farming
111333.rationale: USDA Census: ~1,800 strawberry farms, CA dominant (~90% production). Hand-picking labor 50%+ of cost — Tortuga/Advanced.Farm robotics emerging. But Driscoll's licensing model + Reiter Affiliated control varietals & distribution; independent grower roll-up structurally blocked.
sources: USDA ERS Fruit & Tree Nuts Outlook

111334.title: Berry (except Strawberry) Farming
111334.rationale: Blueberries/raspberries/blackberries — high-growth category (USDA blueberry production +60% over decade). ~4,500 farms. Fresh berry demand strong, premium pricing. PE has played packers (Naturipe, Wish Farms) — grower side still co-op/family dominated.

111335.title: Tree Nut Farming
111335.rationale: Almonds/walnuts/pistachios — CA-dominated, capital-intensive (water rights critical). USDA Census: ~17K nut farms. Wonderful Company (pistachios/almonds) vertically integrated; Blue Diamond co-op dominates almonds. Land-value-driven returns, not operating leverage. Water-policy risk severe (SGMA in CA).

111336.title: Fruit and Tree Nut Combination Farming
111336.rationale: Mixed orchards, mostly CA Central Valley. ~2,500 operations. Diversification doesn't add multiple uplift — buyers want pure-play. Same labor/water/regulatory headwinds as parent categories.

111339.title: Other Noncitrus Fruit Farming
111339.rationale: Stone fruit (peaches, plums, cherries), avocados, kiwi, pomegranates etc. Avocado segment hot but Mexico dominates supply. ~12K US farms across categories. Highly fragmented but small individual TAMs limit roll-up scale.

111411.title: Mushroom Production
111411.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~400 farms but Top 5 (Monterey, Phillips, Highline, To-Jo, Modern) ~70% share — already consolidated. Indoor controlled-environment = AI/automation highly applicable (climate, harvest robotics). Premium specialty mushroom segment (lion's mane, oyster) growing 15%+ — fragmented entry. Best V/A/M scores in NAICS 111.

111419.title: Other Food Crops Grown Under Cover
111419.rationale: Greenhouse vegetables (tomatoes, cucumbers, lettuce), vertical farming. ~1,800 operations per USDA Census. CEA (Controlled Environment Ag) is AI-native — Mucci, NatureSweet, AppHarvest (bankrupt), Plenty (struggling). High capex/unit-economics risk. Real consolidation play but execution risk material.

111421.title: Nursery and Tree Production
111421.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~7,500 nurseries. PE active here (Imperial Nurseries, Monrovia private). Landscape supply roll-ups happening (SiteOne acquired ~50+ wholesale distributors 2018-2024). Recurring landscape demand, labor-heavy = AI in inventory/logistics. Best fragmentation + multiple arbitrage in NAICS 111.

111422.title: Floriculture Production
111422.rationale: Cut flowers, potted plants, bedding plants. ~5K farms but US production declining (Colombia/Ecuador dominate cut flowers). Domestic operations focus on potted/bedding (Costa Farms, Altman Plants — large players already). Mature category, limited growth thesis.

111910.title: Tobacco Farming
111910.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~3,500 tobacco farms (down from ~90K in 1997 — buyout program ended quotas 2004). Declining demand (cigarette volumes -5%/yr). ESG-blocked from institutional capital. No PE will touch.

111920.title: Cotton Farming
111920.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~13K cotton farms, concentrated TX/GA/MS. Subsidy-critical (ARC/PLC + STAX), price-volatile (China demand). John Deere autonomous tractors heavily deployed. Commodity producer with no exit market for PE.

111930.title: Sugarcane Farming
111930.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~700 farms, FL/LA/TX. US Sugar Corp + Florida Crystals (Fanjul) dominate FL; LA more fragmented but co-op (Sugar Cane Growers) controlled. Sugar program (USDA loan rates, TRQs) makes economics policy-dependent. Highly concentrated industry already.

111940.title: Hay Farming
111940.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~190K hay farms — extreme fragmentation but tiny businesses (median <$150K rev). Most is feed for own livestock or local market. No PE thesis: no scale, no labor arbitrage, no exit market. Pure family-farm sector.

111991.title: Sugar Beet Farming
111991.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~2,800 farms. Vertically integrated through grower cooperatives (American Crystal, Southern Minn Beet Sugar, Western Sugar) — farmers ARE the co-op shareholders. PE roll-up structurally impossible.

111992.title: Peanut Farming
111992.rationale: USDA Census 2022: ~5,000 peanut farms, GA/AL/FL/TX. Birdsong/Golden Peanut/Olam dominate shelling (downstream). Farm-level: ARC/PLC dependent commodity. Same dynamics as cotton.

111998.title: All Other Miscellaneous Crop Farming
111998.rationale: Catch-all: hops, mint, herbs, ginseng, hemp, etc. Very fragmented but micro-businesses. Hemp post-2018 Farm Bill bubble collapsed (-80% acreage since 2019). Specialty herbs/hops have niche premium but small TAM. Hops consolidated (Hopsteiner, Yakima Chief).

112111.title: Beef Cattle Ranching and Farming
112111.rationale: USDA 2022 Census: 622K beef cow operations, avg 44 cows. JBS/Tyson/Cargill/National Beef control 85% of packing; ranchers are price-takers with cyclical 3-7% margins. Land-heavy asset play, not operating roll-up.

112112.title: Cattle Feedlots
112112.rationale: Top 30 feedlots (Five Rivers/Cactus/Friona) finish ~50% of US cattle; 2K commercial yards remain. Margins squeezed by packer concentration and feed/cattle spread volatility. AI in ration/health useful but ROI thin.

112120.title: Dairy Cattle and Milk Production
112120.rationale: USDA 2022: ~24K dairy farms (down from 70K in 2003), top 3% produce >50% of milk. Cooperative-dominated (DFA, Land O'Lakes) caps pricing. Robotic milking/computer vision adoption rising but capex-heavy.

112130.title: Dual-Purpose Cattle Ranching and Farming
112130.rationale: Tiny niche, <1% of US cattle operations per USDA. Hybrid beef/milk model uncommon in US (mostly small heritage farms). No PE thesis.

112210.title: Hog and Pig Farming
112210.rationale: Smithfield/JBS/Tyson/Seaboard control ~70% packing; 90%+ of hogs raised under packer contracts. NPPC: 60K operations down from 1M in 1967 — already consolidated. Contract growers earn fixed fee, no upside.

112310.title: Chicken Egg Production
112310.rationale: Top 5 (Cal-Maine, Rose Acre, Versova, Hillandale, Daybreak) own ~50%+ of US layer flock per United Egg Producers. HPAI volatility distorts margins. Cage-free conversion capex barrier favors incumbents.

112320.title: Broilers and Other Meat Type Chicken Production
112320.rationale: Tyson/Pilgrim's/Sanderson/Perdue/Wayne-Sanderson control ~60% of US broiler output (US Poultry & Egg Assoc). 95%+ of growers on contract: fixed payment per pound, integrator owns birds/feed. No roll-up window.

112330.title: Turkey Production
112330.rationale: Butterball/Jennie-O (Hormel)/Cargill/Perdue control ~55% of US turkey production per National Turkey Federation. Contract model mirrors broilers; HPAI compounded losses 2022-24.

112340.title: Poultry Hatcheries
112340.rationale: Captive hatcheries of integrators dominate; ~300 commercial hatcheries (USDA NASS). Aviagen/Cobb-Vantress duopoly in genetics. Independent backyard/specialty hatcheries fragmented but tiny TAM.

112390.title: Other Poultry Production
112390.rationale: Ducks (Maple Leaf Farms 50%+), geese, quail, pheasant — small niches. Maple Leaf dominates ducks; rest fragmented small farms. Sub-$500M total category TAM limits PE interest.

112410.title: Sheep Farming
112410.rationale: USDA 2022 Census: ~101K sheep operations, avg flock 49 head; US sheep inventory at historic low (~5M). Wool prices collapsed; lamb imports (Australia/NZ) dominate. No scale economics or roll-up logic.

112420.title: Goat Farming
112420.rationale: USDA 2022: ~150K goat operations, avg <20 head; meat goat 60%, dairy goat 15%, fiber 5%. Hobby/lifestyle farming dominates. No institutional buyer-side liquidity.

112511.title: Finfish Farming and Fish Hatcheries
112511.rationale: USDA 2018 Aquaculture Census: ~1,500 finfish farms; catfish (top 5 ~60% — Catfish Farmers of America), trout, tilapia, salmon (RAS startups). RAS capex burns capital (Atlantic Sapphire); small wild-card thesis for AI feed/water mgmt.

112512.title: Shellfish Farming
112512.rationale: USDA 2018: ~1,300 shellfish farms (oysters/clams/mussels), heavily owner-operator (PNW, Chesapeake, Gulf). Premium DTC pricing and ESG tailwind; permit/lease moats limit greenfield. Possible niche aggregation play.

112519.title: Other Aquaculture
112519.rationale: Alligators (LA), shrimp (indoor RAS startups), ornamental fish, algae. USDA 2018 counts ~800 ops total; fragmented but micro-niches with no thesis convergence.

112910.title: Apiculture
112910.rationale: USDA NASS: ~2.7M honey-producing colonies, ~20K operations >5 colonies. Pollination services (Adee Honey, Browning's) more revenue than honey. Honey commoditized by Chinese/Argentine imports. Hive-monitoring IoT emerging.

112920.title: Horses and Other Equine Production
112920.rationale: USDA 2022 Census: ~63K equine ops; mostly hobby/sport/breeding, not commodity production. Thoroughbred breeding concentrated (Coolmore/Godolphin) but lifestyle asset class, not roll-up target.
sources: American Horse Council Economic Impact Study

112930.title: Fur-Bearing Animal and Rabbit Production
112930.rationale: Mink industry collapsed post-COVID (2020 cull, fashion bans); chinchilla/fox tiny. Rabbit meat <$50M TAM. Anti-fur regulation risk + no scalability = uninvestable.

112990.title: All Other Animal Production
112990.rationale: Bison, deer, elk, alpaca, llama, ostrich, insects — sum of fragmented hobby/specialty niches. USDA 2022 catches ~50K operations. Insect protein (cricket/BSF) only sub-niche with VC interest, not PE roll-up.

113110.title: Timber Tract Operations
113110.rationale: Timberland ownership; revenue from stumpage sales and land lease. High asset value but cash flow lumpy (harvest cycles). TIMOs/REITs (Weyerhaeuser, Rayonier) already consolidated large tracts; sub-$5M owners fragmented but illiquid. AI minimal beyond GIS/inventory.

113210.title: Forest Nurseries and Gathering of Forest Products
113210.rationale: 184 firms, avg 5 employees — too small for roll-up scale. Seedling nurseries serve timber industry; gathering (pine straw, moss, mushrooms) is hyper-local seasonal labor. Low margins, weather-dependent. No meaningful AI lever.
sources: USDA Forest Service State & Private Forestry

113310.title: Logging
113310.rationale: 7,465 firms, avg 6 employees — fragmented but commodity contract logging for mills. Thin margins, capex-heavy (feller-bunchers $500K+), diesel/labor squeezed. Customer concentration (1-2 mills). Telematics/route AI marginal. PE-unfriendly: cyclical, weather, OSHA risk.

114111.title: Finfish Fishing
114111.rationale: Wild capture quota-limited (NOAA Magnuson-Stevens ACLs). Vessel-level ops, no back-office to consolidate. Permits non-transferable across regions.

114112.title: Shellfish Fishing
114112.rationale: Wild shellfish quota/season-limited; most oyster/clam scale is aquaculture (NAICS 1125, not here). Owner-operator boats, limited entry permits.

114119.title: Other Marine Fishing
114119.rationale: Tiny universe (120 firms). Niche species, quota-bound, no roll-up economics.

114210.title: Hunting and Trapping
114210.rationale: Commercial hunting/trapping near-extinct in US; CBP count mostly guide outfitters/fur (declining). State-licensed, seasonal, no platform thesis.

115111.title: Cotton Ginning
115111.rationale: Capital-intensive seasonal asset (gin facility) tied to cotton acreage; ~186 firms but consolidation already underway via grower co-ops (Calcot, PCCA, Staplcotn) controlling majority of US gin throughput per USDA NASS Cotton Ginnings. Physical processing (suction pipes, lint cleaners, bale presses) is not AI-replaceable; arbitrage limited because exit market is co-op or strategic, not PE.
sources: USDA NASS Cotton Ginnings Annual Summary 2024

115112.title: Soil Preparation, Planting, and Cultivating
115112.rationale: Highly fragmented (2,200 firms, 5.4 avg employees) custom operators with seasonal tractor/planter fleets; per USDA Census of Ag 2022 custom work expenditures, work is dispatched locally with razor-thin margins. AI helps in route/equipment scheduling but field operation itself requires owner-operator equipment capex — multiple arbitrage capped at strategic equipment-dealer plays.
sources: USDA Census of Agriculture 2022 Custom Work Expenses

115113.title: Crop Harvesting, Primarily by Machine
115113.rationale: Custom harvesters (wheat crews migrating TX-MT, silage choppers, nut shakers) — 382 firms but seasonal nomadic operators; combine autonomy (John Deere See & Spray, AGCO Fendt IDEAL) raises productivity but is OEM-captured, not roll-up arbitrage. Capex per combine $750K-$1M caps consolidation upside.

115114.title: Postharvest Crop Activities (except Cotton Ginning)
115114.rationale: Drying, shelling, packing, cold storage — 1,101 firms with higher density (27.9 avg employees) and some PE interest (Paine Schwartz in produce packing, Wind Point in nut handling). AI vision sorters (Tomra, Key Technology) improving yield 3-5pp per peer-reviewed Postharvest Biology & Tech 2023. Asset-heavy but real arbitrage in fresh produce regional packers.

115115.title: Farm Labor Contractors and Crew Leaders
115115.rationale: H-2A visa-driven low-margin pass-through (DOL wage bonds, ETA-790 filings); 656 firms but margin compression from $19.97 AEWR 2025 weighted-average and joint-employer liability per FLSA. AI helps in scheduling/compliance but cannot replace fieldworkers; regulatory risk (DOL enforcement, state ag labor laws CA AB-1066) deters PE.

115116.title: Farm Management Services
115116.rationale: Professional services (farmland portfolio management for absentee landowners — Farmers National, Hertz Farm Mgmt, Peoples Company) with 738 firms; recurring fee revenue (5-10% of gross or $/acre). High AI leverage on yield modeling, lease pricing, satellite (Planet Labs, Climate FieldView) — closest 115 sub-industry to true PE roll-up thesis.
sources: Peoples Company TIAA Westchester Group land mgmt AUM

115210.title: Support Activities for Animal Production
115210.rationale: Hyper-fragmented (4,893 firms, 4.7 avg employees) — AI breeders, sheep shearers, hoof trimmers, livestock spraying. Niche local operators with low ticket sizes; bull/semen distribution consolidated at ABS Global/Genus/Select Sires (not roll-up candidates). AI delta minimal — physical animal handling dominant.
sources: USDA Census of Ag 2022 Animal Specialties

115310.title: Support Activities for Forestry
115310.rationale: Logging contractors, timber cruising, reforestation crews — 1,979 firms heavily dependent on TIMO/REIT customer base (Weyerhaeuser, Rayonier, PotlatchDeltic) which dictates pricing. Per Forest Resources Assoc 2024 logger survey, 60% are owner-operators with <10 employees; high workers' comp/insurance and equipment capex ($400K-$800K skidder) constrain margins.
sources: USDA Forest Service Timber Products Output 2023

211120.title: Crude Petroleum Extraction
211120.rationale: Post-Permian consolidation wave (Pioneer-Exxon, Hess-Chevron, Endeavor-Diamondback) left supermajors and large independents (EOG, Devon, ConocoPhillips) dominant. Sub-scale operators face capital intensity, commodity price exposure, mineral rights complexity. PE roll-up thesis broken: scale already concentrated, no fragmentation arbitrage. AI lift marginal (seismic, predictive maintenance).

211130.title: Natural Gas Extraction
211130.rationale: Appalachian/Haynesville dominated by EQT, Chesapeake, Expand Energy post-merger. Henry Hub price volatility crushes sub-scale economics. Capital-intensive, midstream-dependent, regulatory burden. No PE-friendly multiple arbitrage; strategic buyers already rolled up. AI delta limited to drilling optimization.

212114.title: Surface Coal Mining
212114.rationale: ESG-disfavored secular decline: US coal production fell from 1.17B tons (2008) to ~520M tons (2023) per EIA. Consolidated under Peabody, Arch, Alpha Met; PE exit multiples compressed to 2-4x EBITDA due to stranded-asset risk. Not roll-up viable.

212115.title: Underground Coal Mining
212115.rationale: Same ESG decline as surface; MSHA safety burden + Appalachian basin exhaustion. Underground capex/labor intensity limits AI uplift to autonomous longwall (already deployed by majors). PE avoids; only met-coal players (Warrior, Ramaco) attract capital.

212210.title: Iron Ore Mining
212210.rationale: Cleveland-Cliffs + U.S. Steel control ~95% of US iron ore (Mesabi Range); only 26 firms total per CBP. Capital-intensive oligopoly, no PE roll-up path. Autonomous haul trucks (Rio Tinto model) already standard at majors.

212220.title: Gold Ore and Silver Ore Mining
212220.rationale: Newmont + Barrick + Kinross dominate; junior miners exist but are exploration-stage equities, not PE-roll-up targets. Capital intensity and commodity-price exposure make platform building uneconomic vs. public majors.

212230.title: Copper, Nickel, Lead, and Zinc Mining
212230.rationale: Freeport-McMoRan + Southern Copper + Rio Tinto Kennecott control US copper; 59 firms but top-5 do >85% of output. Strategic mineral status (CHIPS/IRA) attracts strategic acquirers, not PE roll-up. Capex per ton >$15K.

212290.title: Other Metal Ore Mining
212290.rationale: Includes uranium, rare earths, lithium, molybdenum — strategic minerals attracting DOE/DOD funding (MP Materials, Energy Fuels). Single-asset junior miners, not fragmented platforms. Roll-up logic doesn't apply.

212311.title: Dimension Stone Mining and Quarrying
212311.rationale: 395 firms, 13 avg employees — highly fragmented (granite/marble/limestone block quarries serving countertops + monuments). Cyclical to housing; small-ticket roll-up possible but TAM modest (~$2B). AI uplift in quote-to-cash and yield optimization.

212312.title: Crushed and Broken Limestone Mining and Quarrying
212312.rationale: 1,388 firms, 18.7 avg employees — classic aggregates fragmentation. Vulcan Materials + Martin Marietta + Heidelberg trade at 14-18x EBITDA; bolt-on small quarries acquired at 6-8x = 2x multiple arbitrage. IIJA infrastructure tailwind through 2028.

212313.title: Crushed and Broken Granite Mining and Quarrying
212313.rationale: 286 firms, 21 avg employees — Southeast US concentrated (NC/GA/VA). Same Vulcan/MMM roll-up thesis as limestone; aggregate haul radius ~50mi makes local density valuable. Quarry permits = scarce moat.

212319.title: Other Crushed and Broken Stone Mining and Quarrying
212319.rationale: 381 firms, 16 avg employees — trap rock, marble, sandstone for construction. Identical roll-up dynamics as 212312/212313: local fragmentation + multiple arbitrage to majors at 14-18x. AI in route optimization and load planning.

212321.title: Construction Sand and Gravel Mining
212321.rationale: 2,227 firms, 11.2 avg employees — most fragmented aggregates segment. Local pit economics (haul radius ~30mi); Cemex/CRH/Heidelberg + PE platforms (Arcosa, Summit) actively rolling up at 6-8x → trading at 12-15x. IIJA + onshoring demand tailwind.

212322.title: Industrial Sand Mining
212322.rationale: 207 firms, 27 avg employees — frac sand (Wisconsin/Texas Permian) + glass/foundry sand. Consolidated post-2014 shale crash (US Silica, Hi-Crush, Covia). PE roll-up window mostly closed; specialty industrial sands (semiconductors) emerging niche.

212323.title: Kaolin, Clay, and Ceramic and Refractory Minerals Mining
212323.rationale: Georgia kaolin belt dominated by Imerys, KaMin, Thiele; bentonite by Minerals Technologies + Wyo-Ben. Industrial specialty (paper, ceramics, oil drilling) — moderate consolidation, limited roll-up TAM.

212390.title: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Mining and Quarrying
212390.rationale: Catch-all: phosphate (Mosaic), potash (Nutrien), gypsum (USG), salt (Compass), boron (Rio Tinto). Each sub-segment is oligopoly with public majors; no fragmented PE roll-up structure. Lithium emerging but single-asset projects.

213111.title: Drilling Oil and Gas Wells
213111.rationale: Contract drilling already consolidated among Patterson-UTI, Helmerich & Payne, Nabors (top-3 ~50% US land rig share per Baker Hughes rig count). Highly cyclical with WTI prices; 2008 firms is misleading — real M&A targets are <100 mid-size drillers. AI helps drilling optimization (Corva, H&P AutoSlide) but capex-heavy (rigs $20-30M each) kills roll-up math.

213112.title: Support Activities for Oil and Gas Operations
213112.rationale: Largest bucket (9,212 firms) but dominated by HAL/SLB/BKR/Liberty in pressure pumping/wireline/cementing; long tail is local service (rental tools, well testing, workover). Liberty Energy consolidated frac (acquired Schlumberger N.A. frac 2020). EBITDA margins 10-15% mid-cycle; AI use cases (predictive maintenance, fleet routing) marginal vs commodity exposure.

213113.title: Support Activities for Coal Mining
213113.rationale: Structural decline — US coal production fell from 1.17B tons (2008) to 578M (2023) per EIA; only 187 firms, employment 3,154. ESG/financing barriers make PE roll-up nearly impossible (most LPs exclude thermal coal). Specialty met-coal services niche but too small ($1-2B TAM). MSHA safety regulation high but no AI uplift thesis.

213114.title: Support Activities for Metal Mining
213114.rationale: Fragmented 236 firms serving copper/gold/lithium mines; tailwind from critical-minerals reshoring (IRA, DPA Title III). Specialty drilling (Major Drilling, Boart Longyear) and assay/exploration services — Boart Longyear restructured 2021 showing distressed entry points. AI in mineral exploration (KoBold Metals, GoldSpot) is real but applies to majors not service contractors.
sources: S&P Global Market Intelligence mining services

213115.title: Support Activities for Nonmetallic Minerals (except Fuels) Mining
213115.rationale: Smallest bucket (204 firms, avg 15 employees) — serves aggregates, frac sand, lithium brine, industrial minerals. Frac sand boom-bust (Hi-Crush bankrupt 2020, Covia restructured) shows cyclicality; aggregates customers (Vulcan, Martin Marietta, CRH) self-perform. TAM <$2B, EBITDA single-digit margins, no AI thesis beyond logistics.

221111.title: Hydroelectric Power Generation
221111.rationale: FERC-licensed hydro assets (552 firms per CBP) trade as long-duration infrastructure at 12-18x EBITDA; PPA-bound, not roll-up viable. AI confined to flow forecasting and turbine predictive maintenance.

221112.title: Fossil Fuel Electric Power Generation
221112.rationale: 1,799 firms but assets are PUC/FERC-regulated or PPA-merchant; consolidation happens at IPP level (Vistra, NRG), not roll-up. Coal retirement accelerating per EIA AEO 2025; stranded asset risk.

221113.title: Nuclear Electric Power Generation
221113.rationale: Only 164 firms / 54 operating plants; NRC-licensed, owned by Constellation/Duke/Southern. Zero roll-up potential; M&A requires NRC license transfer (12-24 month review).

221114.title: Solar Electric Power Generation
221114.rationale: 621 firms is fragmented but PPA-locked utility-scale assets trade as yieldco infrastructure (8-12x); developer roll-ups exist (Lightsource, Cypress Creek) but capital-intensive. AI in O&M/forecasting modest.

221115.title: Wind Electric Power Generation
221115.rationale: 629 firms but utility-scale wind dominated by NextEra/Avangrid/Berkshire; PPA-bound yieldco assets. AI used for turbine fault detection (Vestas), not roll-up thesis.

221116.title: Geothermal Electric Power Generation
221116.rationale: Only 44 firms; dominated by Ormat, Calpine, Berkshire. Tiny niche, no roll-up runway. Site-specific resource constraints prevent scale-out.

221117.title: Biomass Electric Power Generation
221117.rationale: 113 firms in a declining segment (EIA shows capacity flat-down); feedstock economics weak post-RPS reforms. PPA-bound, not roll-up viable.

221118.title: Other Electric Power Generation
221118.rationale: 160 firms / 952 employees catchall (storage, fuel cells, waste-heat). BESS developers (Plus Power, Esstor) consolidating but capex-heavy infrastructure play, not services roll-up.

221121.title: Electric Bulk Power Transmission and Control
221121.rationale: FERC-regulated transmission owned by ITC/Eversource/AEP; ISO/RTO operators are nonprofits (PJM, MISO). Zero roll-up potential; FERC formula-rate assets trade at infrastructure multiples 15-20x.

221122.title: Electric Power Distribution
221122.rationale: 8,456 firms inflated by 900+ munis and 800+ co-ops (non-acquirable). Investor-owned distribution requires PUC approval per state; consolidation (Exelon/Pepco, Eversource) takes 2-3 years.

221210.title: Natural Gas Distribution
221210.rationale: 2,420 firms but LDCs are state-PUC regulated rate-base monopolies (Atmos, Spire, Southwest Gas). M&A like Sempra/KKR or NextEra/Florida City Gas requires PUC review; not PE roll-up viable.

221310.title: Water Supply and Irrigation Systems
221310.rationale: 4,430 firms but ~85% are municipal/public (per AWWA); only ~2,000 IOUs are acquirable. AWK/WTRG actively roll-up small private/municipal systems at 1.5-2x rate base, but each deal needs PUC approval (12-18 months). Modest but real roll-up segment.

221320.title: Sewage Treatment Facilities
221320.rationale: 562 firms but ~90% municipal-owned (Clean Watersheds Needs Survey); private wastewater consolidates with water systems (Aqua/Essential Utilities). Limited standalone roll-up.

221330.title: Steam and Air-Conditioning Supply
221330.rationale: Only 111 firms (district energy systems: Vicinity, Cordia, ENGIE campuses); tiny niche, geographically locked infrastructure plays. Some consolidation (Cordia/IFM) but capex-heavy, not services roll-up.

236115.title: New Single-Family Housing Construction (except For-Sale Builders)
236115.rationale: Custom/contract single-family builders remain highly fragmented (~59,568 firms, avg 4 employees per CBP); margins thin and cyclical, dependent on local relationships and trades. AI delta limited to estimating/scheduling/permitting software.

236116.title: New Multifamily Housing Construction (except For-Sale Builders)
236116.rationale: Multifamily GCs already moderately consolidated around large REIT/PE-backed developers (Greystar, Lennar Multifamily, Mill Creek); capital-intensive and tied to interest-rate cycles. Limited PE roll-up runway beyond existing platforms.

236117.title: New Housing For-Sale Builders
236117.rationale: Top 10 public for-sale builders (DR Horton, Lennar, NVR, Pulte, KB, Toll, Taylor Morrison, Meritage, MDC, Tri Pointe) captured ~45% of US new home closings in 2024 per SEC 10-Ks; remaining 12K firms compete on land/lots, not platform economics. Roll-up window largely closed.

236118.title: Residential Remodelers
236118.rationale: Extreme fragmentation (~133K firms, avg 3.5 employees, no national share leader per JCHS Harvard) with $500B annual spend per JCHS LIRA 2024; AI/software (estimating, lead-gen, scheduling) could compress quoting cycles. Comparable to HVAC/plumbing roll-up theses but lower repeat-revenue.

236210.title: Industrial Building Construction
236210.rationale: Moderately fragmented (3,173 firms, avg 24 employees) but dominated by specialists (Gray, Clayco, ARCO, Fluor industrial) serving large industrial/data center clients; project-based revenue lumpiness deters roll-up. AI value modest (BIM, scheduling).

236220.title: Commercial and Institutional Building Construction
236220.rationale: Fragmented (~39K firms) but ENR Top 400 GCs (Turner, AECOM, Skanska, Whiting-Turner, Clark) hold large-project share; mid-market GCs face thin 3-5% EBITDA margins and project volatility. AI helps estimating/preconstruction but doesn't fix structural margin compression.

237110.title: Water and Sewer Line and Related Structures Construction
237110.rationale: Highly fragmented (10,818 firms, avg 15 employees); EPA lead-pipe replacement mandate and IIJA $55B water funding driving tailwind, but local DBE/union dynamics and bonding requirements limit roll-up speed. AI applies to bid estimation and GIS routing but field work remains labor-bound.
sources: AWWA State of the Water Industry 2024

237120.title: Oil and Gas Pipeline and Related Structures Construction
237120.rationale: Already consolidated (only 2,166 firms, 81 avg employees) with public majors MasTec Oil & Gas, Quanta, Primoris dominating; cyclical with FERC permitting headwinds and ESG capital flight. Limited sub-$50M EBITDA roll-up runway; AI gains modest (route optimization, weld inspection CV).

237130.title: Power and Communication Line and Related Structures Construction
237130.rationale: Active PE roll-up vertical: Quanta Services, MasTec, MYR Group, Dycom executing aggressive M&A amid data-center power load (+15% by 2030 per EPRI), grid hardening, and BEAD broadband $42B; 6,737 firms still leave acquisition runway below tier-1. AI/CV for drone line inspection, OMS scheduling delivering measurable margin lift.

237210.title: Land Subdivision
237210.rationale: Capital-intensive land banking with cyclical rates exposure; 4,618 firms but business model is balance-sheet (land inventory) not operating-roll-up friendly. Public homebuilders (DR Horton, Lennar) internalize; PE prefers fund-of-lots structures over operating platform. AI helps in entitlement workflow and GIS site selection.

237310.title: Highway, Street, and Bridge Construction
237310.rationale: IIJA $350B highway formula funding through FY2026 sustaining demand; Granite Construction, Sterling Infrastructure, Construction Partners (ROAD) actively rolling up regional asphalt/paving (CPI acquired 30+ firms). 9,202 firms still fragmented at sub-$50M tier but DOT prequalification and aggregates vertical integration create moats. AI for bid estimation, fleet telematics, and pavement defect CV.

237990.title: Other Heavy and Civil Engineering Construction
237990.rationale: Catch-all bucket (marine, dams, rail, athletic fields) with 4,673 heterogeneous firms — hard to articulate a single roll-up thesis. Some sub-niches (marine/dredging via Great Lakes Dredge, rail via RailWorks-style) attract PE but require sub-segment focus. AI gains in scheduling and BIM coordination across multidisciplinary jobs.

238110.title: Poured Concrete Foundation and Structure Contractors
238110.rationale: Heavy field labor (formwork, pours) with minimal AI replaceability; 22,699 firms avg 11 employees show high fragmentation but commercial concrete PE roll-ups (e.g., Brundage-Bone, CRH bolt-ons) trade 5-7x EBITDA, not premium. AI limited to dispatch/takeoff (Togal.AI).

238120.title: Structural Steel and Precast Concrete Contractors
238120.rationale: Only 3,972 firms avg 20 employees — moderate fragmentation, capital-intensive (cranes, fab shops) limits roll-up. BIM/Tekla adoption rising; comparables (Apogee, Insteel) trade 6-8x. AI helps detailing/estimating but field erection is skilled trade.

238130.title: Framing Contractors
238130.rationale: 12,524 firms avg 7 employees — small subs heavily tied to homebuilder cycles, thin margins, minimal recurring revenue. AI penetration near zero; multiples 3-5x. Not a PE roll-up category despite fragmentation.

238140.title: Masonry Contractors
238140.rationale: 18,481 firms avg 7 employees in declining trade (masonry share of new construction falling per Mason Contractors Assoc). Skilled labor shortage worsens economics, AI not applicable to bricklaying. Multiples 3-5x.

238150.title: Glass and Glazing Contractors
238150.rationale: 6,756 firms avg 10 employees — moderate fragmentation; commercial curtainwall consolidating (Harmon, Apogee-LSO buyer). Auto-glass adjacency (Safelite/Belron) shows roll-up viability. Multiples 6-9x for commercial glazing PE.

238160.title: Roofing Contractors
238160.rationale: 24,532 firms avg 8 employees — most active residential PE roll-up in trades (CCMP/Wrench Group, Bain/Apex, Rotunda/AccuRoof, Renovo). Storm/insurance recurring demand, AI drone takeoffs (EagleView, Hover) + CRM (JobNimbus) drive 200-400bps margin lift. Recent exits 10-13x EBITDA.

238170.title: Siding Contractors
238170.rationale: 9,191 firms avg 4.5 employees — very fragmented but project-based with no recurring revenue. Often bundled with roofing in PE rollups (Renuity, Power Home Remodeling) at 6-9x. Standalone siding less attractive.

238190.title: Other Foundation, Structure, and Building Exterior Contractors
238190.rationale: 6,277 firms avg 8 employees — mixed bag (waterproofing, scaffolding, exterior insulation). Niche subsegments (waterproofing, e.g., Groundworks/Cherokee) consolidating at 7-9x but heterogeneous category limits broad thesis.

238210.title: Electrical Contractors and Other Wiring Installation Contractors
238210.rationale: 81,842 firms avg 12 employees — premier home services PE category alongside HVAC (Service Champions, Wrench Group, Authority Brands subsume electric). EV/solar/data center tailwinds, EMCOR/Comfort Systems trade 14-18x. AI dispatch/CSR (ServiceTitan) drives 200-400bps margin uplift.

238220.title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors
238220.rationale: 109,601 firms avg 11 employees — #1 home services PE roll-up. Apex Service Partners ($1B+ rev), Wrench Group, Service Champions, Authority Brands consolidating at 12-16x exit vs 4-6x entry. ServiceTitan AI (call scoring, dispatch, dynamic pricing) drives 300-500bps margin lift; recurring maintenance contracts boost LTV.

238290.title: Other Building Equipment Contractors
238290.rationale: 7,907 firms avg 18 employees — includes elevator (Otis/KONE-dominant), fire suppression (APi Group consolidating), security/low-voltage. Recurring service contracts make APi-like models trade 10-13x. AI for predictive maintenance/dispatch.

238310.title: Drywall and Insulation Contractors
238310.rationale: 20,162 firms avg 13 employees — insulation side actively consolidated (Installed Building Products $2.8B rev, TopBuild $5.3B rev) at 8-11x via builder relationships. Drywall standalone weaker. IRA energy efficiency tailwind.

238320.title: Painting and Wall Covering Contractors
238320.rationale: 37,963 firms avg 5 employees — extremely fragmented. Franchise rollups (CertaPro, Five Star Painting/Neighborly, FreshCoat) viable but project-based limits multiples to 5-8x. AI for quoting (Hover, Paintzen) helps lead conversion.

238330.title: Flooring Contractors
238330.rationale: 18,069 firms avg 5 employees — high fragmentation but commodity install, dealer/big-box (Home Depot/Lowes) compress margins. Some PE activity (Empire Today, 50Floor) at 5-7x. Limited recurring revenue.

238340.title: Tile and Terrazzo Contractors
238340.rationale: 10,710 firms avg 5.5 employees — skilled-labor-dependent install with niche commercial work. Minimal PE activity, multiples 4-6x. AI not material.

238350.title: Finish Carpentry Contractors
238350.rationale: 32,169 firms avg 5 employees — very fragmented craft work (trim, cabinets, doors). Custom nature resists automation; cabinet install bundled with kitchen remodelers. Multiples 4-6x, limited PE thesis.

238390.title: Other Building Finishing Contractors
238390.rationale: 7,960 firms avg 10 employees — heterogeneous (acoustical ceilings, bath enclosures, weather stripping). Niche, no broad PE thesis though acoustical (Performance Contracting) has scale plays.

238910.title: Site Preparation Contractors
238910.rationale: 39,686 firms avg 11 employees — excavation/grading/demolition. Equipment-heavy with cyclical exposure. Some PE (Sun Capital/NorthStar, Ares/Atlas Roofing) at 6-9x. AI helps dispatch/GPS machine control (Trimble/Topcon) modest margin.

238990.title: All Other Specialty Trade Contractors
238990.rationale: 40,302 firms avg 7 employees — catch-all (pools, fences, awnings, sandblasting). Heterogeneity defeats single thesis; pool service (Authority Brands/Pinch A Penny) and fencing roll-ups exist at 7-10x. Subsegment-dependent.

311111.title: Dog and Cat Food Manufacturing
311111.rationale: Industry dominated by Mars Petcare, Nestle Purina, JM Smucker (Big Heart) — top 4 control >70% per APPA/Packaged Facts. Capital-intensive extrusion lines; LLMs limited to formulation R&D and DTC marketing.
sources: Packaged Facts Pet Food in US

311119.title: Other Animal Food Manufacturing
311119.rationale: 1,432 firms avg 22 emp — fragmented livestock feed mills, but dominated by Cargill, ADM, Land O'Lakes Purina at scale. AAFCO regulation moderate; commodity margins thin.

311211.title: Flour Milling
311211.rationale: ADM, Ardent Mills (ConAgra/Cargill JV), Bay State, Grain Craft control ~80% of US wheat flour capacity per North American Millers Association. Commodity spread business — no AI moat.

311212.title: Rice Milling
311212.rationale: Riviana (Ebro Foods), Riceland Foods coop, ADM, Producers Rice Mill dominate. USA Rice Federation reports <90 firms; concentration high.

311213.title: Malt Manufacturing
311213.rationale: Cargill Malt, Malteurop, Rahr, Boortmalt control >85% global capacity. 39 US firms but contracted to brewers — not roll-up territory.

311221.title: Wet Corn Milling and Starch Manufacturing
311221.rationale: ADM, Cargill, Ingredion, Tate & Lyle, Roquette control >90% of US wet milling per Corn Refiners Association. 70 firms but huge scale; HFCS/ethanol cycle-driven.

311224.title: Soybean and Other Oilseed Processing
311224.rationale: ABCD oligopoly (ADM, Bunge, Cargill, Dreyfus) plus AGP coop crush ~85% of US soy per NOPA. Renewable diesel capex wave dominated by majors.

311225.title: Fats and Oils Refining and Blending
311225.rationale: Bunge, Cargill, ADM, Stratas Foods JV dominate edible oils; mid-tier private refiners exist (~118 firms). Some roll-up potential in specialty blends but commodity-priced.
sources: Institute of Shortening and Edible Oils

311230.title: Breakfast Cereal Manufacturing
311230.rationale: Kellogg's, General Mills, Post, Quaker (PepsiCo) — top 4 >85% per IRI scan data. Declining category; private label growing but co-packed by same scale players.

311313.title: Beet Sugar Manufacturing
311313.rationale: American Crystal Sugar, Western Sugar, Southern Minnesota, Michigan Sugar — 4 grower coops process essentially all US beet sugar. USDA sugar program controls pricing.

311314.title: Cane Sugar Manufacturing
311314.rationale: ASR Group (Florida Crystals/Domino), US Sugar, Imperial Sugar control US cane refining. Heavily regulated by USDA sugar program with TRQ allocations.

311340.title: Nonchocolate Confectionery Manufacturing
311340.rationale: Mars Wrigley, Mondelez, Hershey lead but 538 firms with strong craft/gummy/functional segment (Haribo, Ferrara, Jelly Belly, plus many indie). NCA reports growth in functional candy — some roll-up runway.
sources: National Confectioners Association State of Treating

311351.title: Chocolate and Confectionery Manufacturing from Cacao Beans
311351.rationale: Hershey, Mars, Mondelez, Lindt, Ferrero dominate >75%. Craft chocolate from bean ~280 firms but mostly sub-$2M rev; specialty premium niche has multiples but small TAM.

311352.title: Confectionery Manufacturing from Purchased Chocolate
311352.rationale: 998 firms avg 33 emp — genuinely fragmented (regional chocolatiers, gift boxes, private label). Lower capex enables roll-up; e-comm gifting tailwind.

311411.title: Frozen Fruit, Juice, and Vegetable Manufacturing
311411.rationale: Simplot, McCain, Lamb Weston, Conagra (Birds Eye), Pinnacle dominate frozen veg/fries. 214 firms but top 5 ~75% per USDA ERS. Capex-heavy IQF lines.

311412.title: Frozen Specialty Food Manufacturing
311412.rationale: Nestle, Conagra, Schwan's (CJ), Nomad lead frozen meals but 552 firms includes ethnic, better-for-you, foodservice co-packers — meaningful PE roll-up activity (e.g., Sovos Brands).

311421.title: Fruit and Vegetable Canning
311421.rationale: Del Monte, Conagra, Seneca, Bonduelle, Hormel, plus tomato giants (Morning Star, Conagra Hunt's). 811 firms but capex-heavy retort lines; private label co-packers consolidating.

311422.title: Specialty Canning
311422.rationale: Campbell Soup, Conagra (Chef Boyardee), Hormel (Dinty Moore), General Mills (Progresso) dominate soup/baby food. 118 firms — Plum/Beech-Nut/Gerber control baby food.

311423.title: Dried and Dehydrated Food Manufacturing
311423.rationale: Olam, Sensient, Sun-Maid, Graceland Fruit, plus jerky/snacks. 268 firms with growing functional/freeze-dried niche; private label TSP/freeze-dried meals attract PE.

311511.title: Fluid Milk Manufacturing
311511.rationale: DFA, Dean Foods (now DFA), Saputo, HP Hood, Borden control >70% per IDFA. Per-capita fluid milk declining 30% over 20 yrs (USDA). Razor-thin margins; coop dominance.

311512.title: Creamery Butter Manufacturing
311512.rationale: Land O'Lakes, Challenge Dairy, Cabot/Agri-Mark, Darigold, Anchor (Fonterra USA) — major coops dominate. 57 firms; CME-priced commodity.

311513.title: Cheese Manufacturing
311513.rationale: Saputo, Lactalis, Land O'Lakes, Schreiber, Leprino lead but 579 firms with vibrant artisan/specialty layer (1000+ ACS members). Specialty cheese roll-up has precedent (Emmi, Savencia).

311514.title: Dry, Condensed, and Evaporated Dairy Product Manufacturing
311514.rationale: DFA, Glanbia, Fonterra USA, Hilmar, Leprino, Agropur — coops/multinationals dominate NDM/whey/MPC. 175 firms; export-driven commodity.

311520.title: Ice Cream and Frozen Dessert Manufacturing
311520.rationale: Unilever, Nestle/Froneri, Wells Enterprises, Blue Bell lead but 488 firms with strong regional/premium/novelty layer. PE interest (e.g., Wells/Ferrero, Halo Top).

311611.title: Animal (except Poultry) Slaughtering
311611.rationale: Tyson, JBS, Cargill, National Beef control >80% US beef slaughter per USDA GIPSA. 1,311 firms includes small custom processors but Big 4 own pricing power. USDA-FSIS heavy regulation.

311612.title: Meat Processed from Carcasses
311612.rationale: Hormel, Smithfield (WH Group), Tyson, JBS Pilgrim's, Conagra lead value-added but 1,636 firms with regional smokehouses, ethnic processors, deli — meaningful roll-up activity (e.g., Premium Brands).

311613.title: Rendering and Meat Byproduct Processing
311613.rationale: Darling Ingredients, Valley Proteins (now Darling), Sanimax, JBS, Tyson capture >70% per NARA. Renewable diesel feedstock tailwind drove multiples up; less roll-up oxygen left.

311615.title: Poultry Processing
311615.rationale: Tyson, Pilgrim's (JBS), Perdue, Sanderson (Cargill/Continental), Wayne-Sanderson control ~60%+ per USDA. 519 firms avg 442 emp — capital-intensive integrators; no fragmentation play.

311710.title: Seafood Product Preparation and Packaging
311710.rationale: Trident, American Seafoods, Pacific Seafood, Bumble Bee, StarKist (Dongwon), Chicken of the Sea (Thai Union) lead but 528 firms with regional processors. NOAA/FDA traceability emerging — AI moat in cold chain QC.

311811.title: Retail Bakeries
311811.rationale: 8,979 firms avg 10 emp — most fragmented in NAICS 311. Independent neighborhood bakeries, mostly mom-and-pop. AI-augmented ops (scheduling, inventory, SKU optimization, custom-cake config) genuine lever. Multiple arbitrage in roll-up.

311812.title: Commercial Bakeries
311812.rationale: Bimbo Bakeries, Flowers Foods, Campbell (Pepperidge), Aryzta, Hostess (JM Smucker) dominate branded bread. 2,745 firms includes private label co-packers and regional artisan — some PE roll-up (e.g., bagels).

311813.title: Frozen Cakes, Pies, and Other Pastries Manufacturing
311813.rationale: Aryzta, Sara Lee (Tyson), Rich Products, Lawler Foods, Lyons Magnus lead foodservice but 228 firms with regional brands. PE active (CSM Bakery, Dawn Foods).

311821.title: Cookie and Cracker Manufacturing
311821.rationale: Mondelez (Nabisco/Oreo), Kellanova, Campbell (Pepperidge), Ferrero, Utz — top 5 >70%. 439 firms but capex/scale-driven; specialty/better-for-you niche limited (Tate's, HighKey).

311824.title: Dry Pasta, Dough, and Flour Mixes Manufacturing from Purchased Flour
311824.rationale: Barilla, TreeHouse (private label), Conagra (Healthy Choice), Continental Mills (Krusteaz), Pillsbury (Smucker) — branded leads but 390 firms with private label co-packers attractive for PE.

311830.title: Tortilla Manufacturing
311830.rationale: Bimbo (Mission Foods), Gruma (Mission Foods USA — but Gruma owns Mission), Azteca, La Banderita lead but 429 firms include regional Hispanic, organic, low-carb. Growing category 5%+ CAGR.

311911.title: Roasted Nuts and Peanut Butter Manufacturing
311911.rationale: JM Smucker (Jif), Hormel (Skippy), Conagra (Peter Pan), Kraft Heinz (Planters now Hormel), Blue Diamond, Wonderful (Resnick) — top 5 >75%. 267 firms but commodity-priced.

311919.title: Other Snack Food Manufacturing
311919.rationale: Frito-Lay (PepsiCo) ~60% salty snack share, Utz, Campbell (Snyder's-Lance), Hain, Conagra. 484 firms but Frito dominance crushes small-firm pricing power.

311920.title: Coffee and Tea Manufacturing
311920.rationale: Keurig Dr Pepper, JDE Peet's, Nestle, Smucker (Folgers), Starbucks (CPG) dominate mass but 1,090 firms avg 20 emp — genuinely fragmented specialty roasters/private label. SCA reports 4,000+ roasters. AI useful for DTC subscription, demand forecasting.

311930.title: Flavoring Syrup and Concentrate Manufacturing
311930.rationale: Coca-Cola, PepsiCo, Keurig Dr Pepper concentrates own the category; remainder is Monin, Torani, Fontana (Starbucks), DaVinci. 170 firms; high-margin but customer concentration.

311941.title: Mayonnaise, Dressing, and Other Prepared Sauce Manufacturing
311941.rationale: Unilever (Hellmann's), Kraft Heinz, McCormick (French's/Frank's), Clorox (Hidden Valley), Conagra dominate but 402 firms with regional/hot sauce/ethnic active in PE (e.g., Sovos, Yellowbird).

311942.title: Spice and Extract Manufacturing
311942.rationale: McCormick dominates US retail >40% share; Olam, Kerry, IFF, Sensient on B2B flavors. 471 firms includes private label and specialty (organic, ethnic, custom blends).

311991.title: Perishable Prepared Food Manufacturing
311991.rationale: Reser's, Bakkavor, Ready Pac (Bonduelle/Taylor Farms), Taylor Farms, Greencore lead fresh-cut/deli but 955 firms with strong fragmentation in commissary, fresh meals, refrigerated prepared. Retailer fresh-meals push + AI demand forecasting strong roll-up thesis.

311999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Food Manufacturing
311999.rationale: Catch-all bucket: 860 firms avg 37 emp covering tofu, vinegar, baking powder, honey, perishable specialty — naturally fragmented but heterogeneous (hard to roll up coherently).

312111.title: Soft Drink Manufacturing
312111.rationale: Coke/Pepsi/Keurig Dr Pepper control ~85% of US carbonated soft drink volume (Beverage Digest 2024); independent bottlers locked into franchise territories. No PE roll-up arbitrage possible at independent level.

312112.title: Bottled Water Manufacturing
312112.rationale: Nestle/BlueTriton, Niagara, Primo Water, Coke (Dasani), Pepsi (Aquafina) dominate; 293 firms but top 5 hold ~70% share (IBISWorld 2024). Regional spring water has niche PE interest (BlueTriton/Primo merger 2024) but commodity economics.

312113.title: Ice Manufacturing
312113.rationale: Highly fragmented (397 firms, avg 14 employees); Reddy Ice (Centerbridge PE), Arctic Glacier (private equity) consolidating. Hyperlocal routes, freight-protected, but capex-heavy and seasonal. Modest roll-up thesis.

312120.title: Breweries
312120.rationale: 5,165 firms (fragmented) but Brewers Association 2024 reports craft volume DOWN 1% YoY, 9.7% closure rate; AB InBev/Molson Coors/Constellation control ~75% of US beer. Roll-up risk in shrinking craft category; distressed M&A possible.

312130.title: Wineries
312130.rationale: 4,494 firms, highly fragmented; Duckhorn (LVMH/PE acquired 2024 by Butterfly), Vintage Wine Estates, Crimson all active PE platforms. DTC + 3-tier complexity favors operators with scale. Best beverage roll-up target in 312.

312140.title: Distilleries
312140.rationale: Diageo/Brown-Forman/Pernod/Beam Suntory/Sazerac dominate top-shelf shelves and distribution; 1,281 craft distillers face glut and aged-inventory working capital trap (ACSA 2024). Strategic acquisitions go to majors not PE platforms.

312230.title: Tobacco Manufacturing
312230.rationale: Altria/Philip Morris/Reynolds (BAT)/ITG control ~95% of US cigarette market (FTC 2024); declining volumes -5% YoY, ESG-excluded by most institutional LPs. Not investable for PE roll-up fund.

313110.title: Fiber, Yarn, and Thread Mills
313110.rationale: US spinning capacity collapsed ~70% since 2000 per NCTO; commodity yarn is import-dominated (China, India, Vietnam) with thin 5-8% margins. Only specialty/technical yarn (Kevlar-adjacent, conductive) survives, but those are vertically integrated by DuPont/Honeywell - not roll-up candidates.

313210.title: Broadwoven Fabric Mills
313210.rationale: Broadwoven apparel fabric is structurally extinct in US - >95% imported per OTEXA data. Remaining mills (Milliken, Glen Raven) serve defense/industrial niches and won't sell. PE activity essentially zero in past decade; bankruptcies (ITG, Cone Denim) dominate the narrative.

313220.title: Narrow Fabric Mills and Schiffli Machine Embroidery
313220.rationale: Narrow fabrics (webbing, tape, ribbons) have niche survival in safety/medical/military applications - Berry Amendment protects ~$200M of DoD demand. Fragmented (174 firms, avg 30 employees) but specialty pockets have 8-12% margins; some PE interest in defense-spec webbing (e.g., Murdock Webbing precedent).

313230.title: Nonwoven Fabric Mills
313230.rationale: ONLY growing segment in 313 - nonwovens (hygiene, medical PPE, filtration, geotextiles) grew 4-5% CAGR per INDA; COVID accelerated investment. Berry/Kimberly-Clark/Suominen consolidating, but mid-market (Precision Custom Coatings, Pro Med) attracts PE - margins 10-15%. AI helps web inspection and basis-weight control.
sources: INDA North American Nonwovens Industry Outlook 2024

313240.title: Knit Fabric Mills
313240.rationale: Knit fabric production lost to Honduras/CAFTA-DR and Asia; only 109 US firms remain (CBP 2022). Activewear circular knit niche (Eagle Knits, Tukatech-served small mills) survives but margins compressed by polyester import pricing. No notable PE roll-ups in past 10 years.

313310.title: Textile and Fabric Finishing Mills
313310.rationale: Most fragmented in 313 (599 firms, avg 25 employees) - dyeing/printing/finishing job-shops. Environmental compliance (wastewater) is huge moat & cost burden. AI could optimize dye recipes/water use (+3pp), but customer base (apparel mills) is shrinking. Narrow PE thesis around technical finishing (flame-retardant, antimicrobial).

313320.title: Fabric Coating Mills
313320.rationale: Best of 313 after nonwovens - coated fabrics serve automotive (airbags), medical, aerospace, military with technical barriers (Saint-Gobain, Trelleborg, Seaman Corp). Berry Amendment protection, 10-14% margins, some mid-market roll-up activity (e.g., Highland Industries deals). AI for coating uniformity/defect detection.

314110.title: Carpet and Rug Mills
314110.rationale: Mohawk/Shaw/Engineered Floors control ~70% of US market — classic duopoly+1 oligopoly leaves no fragmented mid-market to consolidate. Capital-intensive tufting lines and declining hard-surface substitution kill roll-up math.

314120.title: Curtain and Linen Mills
314120.rationale: 1,146 firms at 18 employees avg looks fragmented but ~75% of US curtains/linens are imported (China/India/Pakistan) — domestic mills compete on custom/hospitality niches with thin margins. AI helps design/inventory but cannot beat import cost basis.

314910.title: Textile Bag and Canvas Mills
314910.rationale: 1,389 firms at 16 employees avg — genuinely fragmented with B2B custom canvas (awnings, industrial bags, military) less import-vulnerable due to spec/lead-time. Best of group but small TAM and labor-intensive cut-and-sew limits AI leverage.

314994.title: Rope, Cordage, Twine, Tire Cord, and Tire Fabric Mills
314994.rationale: Only 130 firms at 49 employees avg — already consolidated around captive tire-cord (Kordsa, Indorama) and industrial rope specialists. Limited targets, commodity exposure, low fragmentation premium.

314999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Textile Product Mills
314999.rationale: 2,316 firms at 12 employees avg — most fragmented in 314 but catch-all bucket (auto trim, medical textiles, industrial felt) means no coherent thesis. Heterogeneity blocks platform synergies; sub-segment pick required.

315120.title: Apparel Knitting Mills
315120.rationale: Near-extinct in US (>97% knit apparel imported per AAFA/Census). CBP shows zero employment — sector hollowed out. No roll-up oxygen; AI cannot reverse offshoring cost gap.

315210.title: Cut and Sew Apparel Contractors
315210.rationale: 2,316 micro-shops, avg 10.6 employees — fragmented but tiny. Berry Amendment military/uniform niches viable; rest squeezed by Asia imports. AI nesting/cutting helps margin, not scale.

315250.title: Cut and Sew Apparel Manufacturing (except Contractors)
315250.rationale: Brand-owning cut-sew survives in defense uniforms, workwear, on-shore fast-fashion micro-runs. Limited targets; CBP suppressed (low firm count). AI demand-forecast adds marginal lift.

315990.title: Apparel Accessories and Other Apparel Manufacturing
315990.rationale: 713 firms, avg 12.8 employees — hats, belts, gloves, ties. Some Berry-protected military gear (gloves, headwear). Fragmented but small TAM; no rollup catalyst, AI uplift modest.

316110.title: Leather and Hide Tanning and Finishing
316110.rationale: Tanning sector collapsed 80%+ since 1990s; remaining 167 firms split between commodity hide processors (export to China/Italy) and tiny specialty/luxury tanners. Capex-heavy, environmental liability (chromium), thin margins, declining demand. No SaaS/AI layer. Not rollable.

316210.title: Footwear Manufacturing
316210.rationale: FDRA: 99% of US footwear imported. Domestic 224 firms = mostly military/work boots (Red Wing, Danner, Thorogood), Berry Amendment compliance niche, and craft/luxury (Allen Edmonds). Roll-up impossible — owners are heritage brands, not financial sellers. Military niche is captive to DoD contracts.

316990.title: Other Leather and Allied Product Manufacturing
316990.rationale: Catchall: handbags, belts, luggage, gloves, saddlery, industrial leather. Mostly micro-businesses and craft/Etsy-tier; large players (Coach, Tumi) own brands and offshore production. Equestrian/saddlery sub-niche exists but <$500M TAM. No data layer, no rollable economics.

321113.title: Sawmills
321113.rationale: Heavily consolidated: Weyerhaeuser, West Fraser, Canfor, Interfor control >40% NA capacity post West Fraser-Norbord merger (2021). Capex-intensive ($50M+ greenfield mill), commodity lumber pricing, top 4 firms operate 100+ mills each — limited PE roll-up runway.

321114.title: Wood Preservation
321114.rationale: 361 firms avg 30 employees, moderately fragmented. Koppers and Stella-Jones dominate treated rail ties/utility poles; residential treaters more fragmented. Chemical treatment is process-driven (ACQ/MCA), low AI replaceability, but B2B sales/dispatch ops have moderate AI lift.

321211.title: Hardwood Veneer and Plywood Manufacturing
321211.rationale: 207 firms, declining segment under pressure from imported Chinese hardwood plywood (AD/CVD duties since 2018). Columbia Forest Products, States Industries are scaled leaders; specialty veneer remains craft-driven. Limited AI lever beyond grading/optimization.

321212.title: Softwood Veneer and Plywood Manufacturing
321212.rationale: Only 75 firms avg 168 employees — highly consolidated by Georgia-Pacific, Weyerhaeuser, Boise Cascade, Roseburg. Capital intensity (>$200M plant) creates moat; AI vision systems for veneer grading already deployed by majors. Not a PE entry point.

321215.title: Engineered Wood Member Manufacturing
321215.rationale: Component plants (I-joists, LVL, roof trusses) — Weyerhaeuser, Boise, LP dominate LVL; truss plants (~500 component manufacturers) remain regional. Builders FirstSource rolled up >50 truss/component plants since 2015. Design automation (MiTek/Simpson software) creates AI design uplift.

321219.title: Reconstituted Wood Product Manufacturing
321219.rationale: OSB/MDF/particleboard: 196 firms but top 5 (LP, West Fraser/Norbord, Georgia-Pacific, Weyerhaeuser, Tolko) hold ~75% NA OSB capacity. $400M+ greenfield mill cost. Process-controlled with mature SCADA/AI vision; no PE roll-up thesis.

321911.title: Wood Window and Door Manufacturing
321911.rationale: 1,093 firms avg 57 employees — fragmented under Andersen/Pella/Marvin/JELD-WEN top tier. Active PE: Cornerstone Building Brands (Clayton Dubilier), MITER Brands (Koch+PGT 2024). Custom millwork shops are classic add-on targets at 5-7x EBITDA. CAD/CPQ automation strong AI lever.

321912.title: Cut Stock, Resawing Lumber, and Planing
321912.rationale: 739 firms avg 29 employees — secondary processors serving cabinet/furniture/molding makers. Regionally fragmented, commodity-adjacent margins. Optimization software (USNR, Autolog) deployed by top tier; smaller shops still manual. Modest AI/scheduling lever; PE interest limited by thin margins.

321918.title: Other Millwork (including Flooring)
321918.rationale: 1,516 firms avg 24 employees — highest fragmentation in NAICS 321. Active PE roll-ups: AHF Products (Paceline+American Industrial), Mohawk's hardwood platform, US LBM millwork add-ons. Custom moldings/stair parts/flooring are CPQ-ready; AI quoting/CAD automation 3-5pp margin lift.

321920.title: Wood Container and Pallet Manufacturing
321920.rationale: 2,767 firms avg 22 employees — most fragmented sub-industry. Active roll-ups: PalletOne (Industrial Opportunity Partners), Kamps Pallets (Freeman Spogli 2021), 48forty Solutions (Audax). NWPCA reports >4,000 firms total. AI for sortation/grading/route optimization (recycled pallet flow) is high-value lever.

321991.title: Manufactured Home (Mobile Home) Manufacturing
321991.rationale: 245 firms avg 131 employees — Clayton Homes (Berkshire), Skyline Champion, Cavco hold >75% units shipped (MHI 2024). HUD-code regulation + factory scale create moat. Limited PE roll-up window beyond niche modular builders.

321992.title: Prefabricated Wood Building Manufacturing
321992.rationale: 752 firms avg 31 employees — modular/panelized builders plus garages/sheds. Fragmented; Champion Home Builders, Westchester Modular, growing offsite construction interest from PE (Lennar invested Veev, Katerra collapse a warning). AI for design/BIM automation, factory scheduling — moderate lift.

321999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Wood Product Manufacturing
321999.rationale: 2,369 firms avg 15 employees — extremely fragmented catch-all (dowels, handles, turnings, specialty wood). Mostly sub-scale lifestyle businesses. Roll-up runway exists but margins thin and segment too heterogeneous to scale a single platform thesis.

322110.title: Pulp Mills
322110.rationale: Only 39 firms with avg 242 employees; commodity pulp dominated by IP, Domtar, Resolute, Georgia-Pacific. CapEx-heavy mills ($500M-$1B), zero PE roll-up logic; AI limited to process optimization in DCS systems.

322120.title: Paper Mills
322120.rationale: Highly consolidated: top 5 (IP, WestRock/Smurfit-Westrock, Domtar, Sylvamo, Packaging Corp) hold ~70% capacity per AF&PA. Secular decline in printing/writing paper -3-5% CAGR; mill closures dominate news, not roll-ups.

322130.title: Paperboard Mills
322130.rationale: 175 establishments but capacity concentrated in Packaging Corp, Smurfit-WestRock, Graphic Packaging, Cascades. Containerboard cyclical (PPW pricing); SBS/CRB stable but capital-intensive ($300M+ machines). Not PE addressable at mill level.

322211.title: Corrugated and Solid Fiber Box Manufacturing
322211.rationale: 1,052 firms but integrated giants (Smurfit-Westrock, IP, Packaging Corp, Pratt Industries) control ~75% volume; independents are sheet plants buying from integrateds. Active consolidation (IP+DS Smith 2025, Smurfit+WestRock 2024) compresses independent multiples 5-7x EBITDA.

322212.title: Folding Paperboard Box Manufacturing
322212.rationale: 567 firms avg 99 employees; Graphic Packaging, WestRock, AR Packaging, Sonoco dominate branded carton work. Independent folding carton converters trade 5-8x; AI useful in CAD nesting, prepress automation (Esko, Kongsberg) but not transformative.

322219.title: Other Paperboard Container Manufacturing
322219.rationale: 266 firms (fiber drums, composite cans, set-up boxes) avg 74 employees. Sonoco dominant in composite cans/tubes; fragmented set-up box niche viable for tuck-ins but small TAM. Multiples 5-7x for sub-$5M EBITDA independents.

322220.title: Paper Bag and Coated and Treated Paper Manufacturing
322220.rationale: 708 firms; bag side consolidating (Novolex, Berry, Mondi, Sappi) post-plastic-ban tailwinds; coated/treated specialty (release liners, labels) is Sappi/Felix Schoeller/Ahlstrom turf. Specialty niches (food-contact coated) 6-9x for $5-15M EBITDA.

322230.title: Stationery Product Manufacturing
322230.rationale: 317 firms avg 49 employees in secular decline (envelopes, tablets, social stationery) -3-6% CAGR per BLS PPI; digital substitution structural. Cenveo bankruptcy 2020 illustrates trap; multiples 3-5x. Not a roll-up thesis.

322291.title: Sanitary Paper Product Manufacturing
322291.rationale: 132 firms but tissue/towel/diaper highly concentrated: Kimberly-Clark, P&G, Georgia-Pacific, Essity, First Quality control ~80% per Euromonitor. Private label tissue (Clearwater, Cascades Tissue) capital-intensive. Limited middle-market arbitrage.

322299.title: All Other Converted Paper Product Manufacturing
322299.rationale: 364 firms avg 43 employees - most fragmented in 322 (paper plates, doilies, filter paper, gift wrap). True middle-market but small TAM per niche; sub-$3M EBITDA targets trade 4-6x. Possible micro-rollup, not core thesis.

323111.title: Commercial Printing (except Screen and Books)
323111.rationale: Secular decline -30% since 2010 (Census ASM). Cimpress/Vistaprint consolidated online; offset/digital local shops fragmented but shrinking. AI helps prepress/quoting but cannot reverse demand loss. Equipment-heavy, low margins, customer attrition to digital media.

323113.title: Commercial Screen Printing
323113.rationale: Apparel/promo niche more resilient than paper but commoditized by Printful/Printify POD platforms. Fragmented (~5,800 firms, avg 12 emp). Some online aggregator plays (Custom Ink) but local roll-up faces POD disruption. AI: design tools, order routing.

323117.title: Books Printing
323117.rationale: Already consolidated (LSC/RRD, Quad, Bertelsmann/CPI). Only 385 firms, capex-intensive web presses. eBook substitution stabilized but print volume flat-to-down. Limited targets for new roll-up; AI marginal (workflow only).

323120.title: Support Activities for Printing
323120.rationale: Prepress/bindery/platemaking — fragmented (1,036 firms), follows host industry decline. AI prepress automation (color, imposition, proofing) meaningful margin lever. But TAM shrinking with parent commercial print. Niche tuck-ins only.

324110.title: Petroleum Refineries
324110.rationale: Supermajor oligopoly (XOM, CVX, MPC, VLO, PSX control ~50%+ of US capacity per EIA). $1B+ capex barriers, ESG headwinds, zero PE roll-up path.

324121.title: Asphalt Paving Mixture and Block Manufacturing
324121.rationale: 1,397 firms, 12 avg employees = highly fragmented. Construction Partners (ROAD) actively rolling up at 8-10x EBITDA; Summit Materials, Colas precedent. Local hauling moats + DOT contract tailwinds.

324122.title: Asphalt Shingle and Coating Materials Manufacturing
324122.rationale: Already consolidated: GAF, Owens Corning, CertainTeed, Atlas dominate. 200 firms but top 4 hold 75%+ share. Capital-intensive (~$50M/plant), no roll-up runway.

324191.title: Petroleum Lubricating Oil and Grease Manufacturing
324191.rationale: Top players (Valvoline, Quaker Houghton, Chevron, Shell) hold majority; ~284 firms leave mid-tier blender targets but EV transition shrinks TAM long-term.

324199.title: All Other Petroleum and Coal Products Manufacturing
324199.rationale: Petroleum coke, briquettes, waxes — only 77 firms, niche end-markets, declining coal-derivative demand. Some specialty wax/petrolatum PE interest but limited universe.

325110.title: Petrochemical Manufacturing
325110.rationale: Dow, ExxonMobil Chemical, LyondellBasell control >70% of US ethylene/propylene capacity per ACC 2024 data; CapEx-heavy crackers ($2-5B each) make roll-up economically irrational.
sources: American Chemistry Council Guide to Business of Chemistry 2024

325120.title: Industrial Gas Manufacturing
325120.rationale: Linde, Air Liquide, Air Products hold ~75% global market (Freedonia 2024); 657 firms is mostly small regional distributors filling cylinders, not producers. Pipeline economics make production consolidation unviable.

325130.title: Synthetic Dye and Pigment Manufacturing
325130.rationale: BASF, Clariant, Huntsman dominate large-volume pigments but specialty/niche dye houses (125 firms, avg 63 emp) show moderate fragmentation. AI helps color-match QC but doesn't replace formulation chemists.

325180.title: Other Basic Inorganic Chemical Manufacturing
325180.rationale: Chlor-alkali, soda ash, TiO2 dominated by Olin, Tronox, Chemours, Westlake (SEC filings); 647 firms includes many captive plants. Energy-intensive, regulated (EPA), low automation upside beyond existing DCS.

325193.title: Ethyl Alcohol Manufacturing
325193.rationale: Fuel ethanol dominated by POET, ADM, Valero, Green Plains; commodity margins tied to corn/RIN prices, not operational efficiency. RFS-policy dependent; AI cannot fix structural margin compression.

325194.title: Cyclic Crude, Intermediate, and Gum and Wood Chemical Manufacturing
325194.rationale: Coal tar, BTX intermediates, rosin chemistry dominated by Koppers, Ingevity, Rain Carbon; only 69 firms but feedstock-integrated to steel/petroleum majors. Niche but capital-intensive.

325199.title: All Other Basic Organic Chemical Manufacturing
325199.rationale: 870 firms catch-all bucket (acetic acid, glycols, specialty intermediates); long tail of mid-size custom synthesis houses with moderate roll-up potential, but most are subsidiaries of majors (Eastman, Celanese).

325211.title: Plastics Material and Resin Manufacturing
325211.rationale: PE/PP/PVC/PS dominated by Dow, LyondellBasell, ExxonMobil, Chevron Phillips, Westlake (>65% capacity per ACC); 1258 firms but small ones are compounders, not resin producers. Commodity cycle.
sources: American Chemistry Council Plastics Industry Producers Statistics 2024

325212.title: Synthetic Rubber Manufacturing
325212.rationale: SBR/butyl/EPDM dominated by Goodyear Chemical, Lion Elastomers, ExxonMobil, Kraton; tire-industry captive demand limits independent operators. Only 143 firms.
sources: International Institute of Synthetic Rubber Producers

325220.title: Artificial and Synthetic Fibers and Filaments Manufacturing
325220.rationale: Polyester/nylon/acrylic largely offshored; remaining US production dominated by Indorama, Unifi, Ascend Performance Materials, Invista. Structural decline industry vs. Asian capacity.

325311.title: Nitrogenous Fertilizer Manufacturing
325311.rationale: CF Industries, Nutrien, Yara, Koch Fertilizer hold >70% of NA ammonia/urea capacity; nat-gas-feedstock economics. 205 firms includes many small blenders misclassified.

325312.title: Phosphatic Fertilizer Manufacturing
325312.rationale: Mosaic and Nutrien control ~80% of US phosphate per SEC filings; vertically integrated with Florida phosphate rock mines. 78 firms misleading - most are subsidiaries/blenders.
sources: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries phosphate rock

325314.title: Fertilizer (Mixing Only) Manufacturing
325314.rationale: 520 firms avg 18 employees - true fragmentation of regional ag-retail blenders; classic roll-up target similar to ag-distribution. Local-route economics, NSA/CHS coop overhang limits.

325315.title: Compost Manufacturing
325315.rationale: CBP shows zero data (suppressed - tiny industry). US Composting Council estimates ~500 commercial facilities, highly local/regional. Waste-mgmt adjacency, but feedstock + permitting heavy.

325320.title: Pesticide and Other Agricultural Chemical Manufacturing
325320.rationale: Bayer, Corteva, Syngenta, BASF, FMC hold >70% of US crop-protection sales; 246 firms includes formulators/distributors. EPA FIFRA registration cost ($200M+/molecule) is structural moat.
sources: USDA NASS Agricultural Chemical Use Survey

325411.title: Medicinal and Botanical Manufacturing
325411.rationale: API/botanical extract manufacturers (754 firms); CDMO consolidation thesis already crowded (Catalent, Lonza, Thermo Fisher acquired major players). Mid-tier targets exist but FDA cGMP costly.

325412.title: Pharmaceutical Preparation Manufacturing
325412.rationale: Pfizer, J&J, Merck, AbbVie dominate branded; generics tier (Teva, Sandoz, Viatris) somewhat fragmented but margin-compressed. FDA ANDA process + cGMP cost prohibitive for sub-scale players.

325413.title: In-Vitro Diagnostic Substance Manufacturing
325413.rationale: Roche Dx, Abbott, Danaher, Siemens Healthineers, Thermo Fisher dominate but specialty IVD/companion-dx niches show consolidation activity (Quidel-Ortho 2022, Bio-Techne acquisitions). AI/ML in dx interpretation = real value.

325414.title: Biological Product (except Diagnostic) Manufacturing
325414.rationale: Biologics CDMO is hot M&A theme (Catalent-Novo Holdings $16.5B 2024); large molecule platform manufacturers command premium multiples. Cell/gene therapy contract mfg sub-segment most fragmented.

325510.title: Paint and Coating Manufacturing
325510.rationale: Sherwin-Williams, PPG, AkzoNobel, Axalta hold >55% of US coatings (ACA 2024); 1142 firms includes specialty industrial coaters with credible roll-up dynamics, but trades on EBITDA arbitrage not AI.

325520.title: Adhesive Manufacturing
325520.rationale: Henkel, H.B. Fuller, 3M, Bostik (Arkema), Avery Dennison dominate; 539 firms with specialty/industrial adhesives mid-market. Strategic acquirers (3M, Henkel) compress PE multiples.
sources: Adhesive and Sealant Council market report

325611.title: Soap and Other Detergent Manufacturing
325611.rationale: P&G, Unilever, Henkel, Church & Dwight, Colgate dominate branded retail; 866 firms includes large private-label/contract manufacturers (KIK Custom, Voyant) where roll-up has historically happened.

325612.title: Polish and Other Sanitation Good Manufacturing
325612.rationale: 452 firms avg 38 emp - genuinely fragmented industrial/institutional cleaning supplies; Ecolab, Diversey, Reckitt dominate top-tier but mid-market jan-san contract manufacturing is roll-up territory.

325613.title: Surface Active Agent Manufacturing
325613.rationale: Surfactants (LAB, AES, betaines) dominated by BASF, Stepan, Croda, Solvay; only 116 firms and feedstock-integrated to oleo/petrochemical chains. Specialty bio-surfactant niche is small.

325620.title: Toilet Preparation Manufacturing
325620.rationale: Cosmetics/personal-care (1124 firms avg 48 emp) is most fragmented sub-segment in NAICS 325; indie beauty brands and contract manufacturers (Kolmar, Cosmetic Solutions) drive active M&A. Brand consolidation thesis well-validated (e.g., L Catterton, TSG).

325910.title: Printing Ink Manufacturing
325910.rationale: Sun Chemical (DIC), Flint Group, Siegwerk dominate >50%; 301 firms includes regional specialty/UV/digital ink houses. Print industry secular decline limits multiple expansion.
sources: National Association of Printing Ink Manufacturers

325920.title: Explosives Manufacturing
325920.rationale: Orica, Dyno Nobel (Incitec Pivot), Austin Powder, Maxam control >80% of commercial explosives per industry data; ATF licensing, BATFE/DOT regs create monopolistic barriers. Mining-cycle dependent.
sources: Institute of Makers of Explosives statistics

325991.title: Custom Compounding of Purchased Resins
325991.rationale: 422 firms avg 50 emp - genuinely fragmented service-oriented industry; Avient, Ravago, Polymer Industries consolidating but long tail of regional compounders remains. Roll-up active (Mill Rock Capital, Wynnchurch).
sources: Society of Plastics Engineers compounder directory

325992.title: Photographic Film, Paper, Plate, Chemical, and Copy Toner Manufacturing
325992.rationale: Kodak, Fujifilm, Canon, HP-toner dominate; 147 firms is structurally declining industry (digital displacement). No AI thesis fixes the secular demand collapse.

325998.title: All Other Miscellaneous Chemical Product and Preparation Manufacturing
325998.rationale: 1303 firms avg 33 emp - largest residual bucket including water-treatment chemicals, custom formulations, specialty additives. Genuinely fragmented; PE active in water-treatment (Ecolab, Veolia roll-ups) sub-niche.
sources: ACS Chemical & Engineering News specialty chem coverage

326111.title: Plastics Bag and Pouch Manufacturing
326111.rationale: Berry Global, Novolex, Inteplast already consolidated top tier; commodity film extrusion with thin margins (~8-12%). AI helps yield/scrap reduction but capex-heavy and resin pass-through limits arbitrage.

326112.title: Plastics Packaging Film and Sheet (including Laminated) Manufacturing
326112.rationale: Heavily consolidated by Sealed Air, Berry, Amcor, ProAmpac; multi-layer co-ex requires $20M+ lines so SMB roll-up window narrow. AI vision for defect detection mature but ROI capped.

326113.title: Unlaminated Plastics Film and Sheet (except Packaging) Manufacturing
326113.rationale: More fragmented than packaging film (industrial/agricultural/construction films) but still capex-intensive; Plastic Industry Assoc shows top10 share ~35%. Multiples 5-7x EBITDA.

326121.title: Unlaminated Plastics Profile Shape Manufacturing
326121.rationale: Custom extruded profiles for building/auto/industrial — fragmented (avg 55 emp/firm) but commodity-grade margins. AI limited to extrusion line tuning; no major rollup precedent. Multiples 4-6x.

326122.title: Plastics Pipe and Pipe Fitting Manufacturing
326122.rationale: Aliaxis, JM Eagle, Westlake, Atkore consolidated; PVC/HDPE pipe enjoys construction demand and 12-16% margins but commodity resin volatility. AI thin (extrusion control). Strategic multiples 7-9x.

326130.title: Laminated Plastics Plate, Sheet (except Packaging), and Shape Manufacturing
326130.rationale: Niche industrial laminates (HPL, decorative, electrical) — Wilsonart/Formica dominate decorative but industrial slice fragmented across ~220 firms. AI for QC/lamination control modest uplift.

326140.title: Polystyrene Foam Product Manufacturing
326140.rationale: Pactiv Evergreen, Dart Container, WinCup dominate foodservice EPS; bans on EPS foodware in 11 states erode growth. AI limited; multiples deflated by ESG headwinds 4-6x.

326150.title: Urethane and Other Foam Product (except Polystyrene) Manufacturing
