326150.rationale: 662 firms avg 49 emp — most fragmented in 3261. Custom PU foam for furniture, auto, insulation, packaging; Carpenter Co, FXI, Future Foam tier1 but long tail. Roll-up plausible; multiples 5-7x. AI for formulation/QC modest.

326160.title: Plastics Bottle Manufacturing
326160.rationale: Amcor, Berry, Alpha Packaging, Plastipak heavily consolidated PET/HDPE bottle; capex blow-molders $5-15M each. AI vision QC mature. Limited SMB targets; multiples 6-8x but few left.

326191.title: Plastics Plumbing Fixture Manufacturing
326191.rationale: Acrylic/composite tubs, showers, sinks; Lasco, Maax, Mansfield, Kohler dominate. Mid-tier fragmentation (~300 firms). Construction-cyclical 10-14% margins. Multiples 5-7x; AI limited to mold/thermoforming control.

326199.title: All Other Plastics Product Manufacturing
326199.rationale: Massive catch-all bucket: 5566 firms (custom injection molders, contract manufacturers) — most fragmented sub-industry in entire 326. AI for quote-to-order, scheduling, predictive maintenance has real arbitrage. Strategic 6-8x; classic PE roll-up zone (Wirtz/Centerfield/Berry adjacencies).
sources: AMP Capital plastics rollup case studies 2023-2024

326211.title: Tire Manufacturing (except Retreading)
326211.rationale: Pure oligopoly: Goodyear, Bridgestone, Michelin, Continental, Yokohama, Pirelli >85% share; 131 firms avg 391 emp = mega-cap only. Capex $500M+ greenfield plant. Zero roll-up viability.

326212.title: Tire Retreading
326212.rationale: 350 firms avg 18 emp — true mom-and-pop; Bandag (Bridgestone) and Michelin Recamic franchise systems dominate brand. Commercial truck retread cyclical, EBITDA 7-11%. Roll-up possible but small TAM (<$2B US).

326220.title: Rubber and Plastics Hoses and Belting Manufacturing
326220.rationale: Continental, Gates, Eaton, Parker Hannifin consolidated industrial hose/belt; 267 firms but top10 ~60% share. Auto/industrial cyclical. Multiples 6-8x for SMB tuck-ins. AI for predictive QA emerging.

326291.title: Rubber Product Manufacturing for Mechanical Use
326291.rationale: Seals, gaskets, O-rings, vibration mounts for auto/industrial; Trelleborg, Freudenberg, NOK, Parker tier1, but ~385 firms with mid-fragmentation. AI quoting and DOE design useful. Multiples 6-8x; viable mid-market roll-up.

326299.title: All Other Rubber Product Manufacturing
326299.rationale: 681 firms avg 52 emp — fragmented catch-all (rubber mats, balloons, latex products, custom moldings). No tier1 dominator. PE roll-up plausible; multiples 5-7x. AI for batch control and lab-mixing useful.

327110.title: Pottery, Ceramics, and Plumbing Fixture Manufacturing
327110.rationale: Niche category dominated by Kohler, TOTO, Lixil in plumbing fixtures (oligopoly); pottery side is fragmented but low margin and tariff-exposed from Chinese imports. AI limited to kiln optimization and defect vision.

327120.title: Clay Building Material and Refractories Manufacturing
327120.rationale: Brick/refractories sector consolidated around Acme Brick, Glen-Gery, HarbisonWalker; declining demand from concrete substitution. Some AI use in kiln control and predictive maintenance for refractory wear.

327211.title: Flat Glass Manufacturing
327211.rationale: Global oligopoly (Guardian, Vitro, NSG/Pilkington, AGC) with float-line economics requiring $200M+ capex; no roll-up opportunity. AI used in furnace optimization and quality inspection.

327212.title: Other Pressed and Blown Glass and Glassware Manufacturing
327212.rationale: Mixed fragmentation: specialty/lab glass moderately fragmented, but Corning and Owens-Illinois dominate technical segments. Import competition compresses margins. Limited AI roll-up thesis.

327213.title: Glass Container Manufacturing
327213.rationale: Tight oligopoly: O-I Glass, Ardagh, Verallia control most US capacity; furnaces cost $70M+. No fragmentation to roll up. AI use in IS-machine optimization mature.

327215.title: Glass Product Manufacturing Made of Purchased Glass
327215.rationale: Most fragmented glass sub-segment (~976 firms, avg 51 employees) — fabricators of insulated units, tempered/laminated glass for construction. PGT Innovations, Cardinal Glass consolidating; AI for cutting optimization and quoting workflows is real opportunity.

327310.title: Cement Manufacturing
327310.rationale: Concentrated industry — Holcim, Heidelberg, Cemex, CRH, Eagle Materials control ~80%. Kiln economics and permitting are unrepeatable moats; already largely rolled up. AI in kiln control, alternative-fuel optimization mature.

327320.title: Ready-Mix Concrete Manufacturing
327320.rationale: Highly fragmented (~5,969 firms, avg 15 employees) — classic active roll-up thesis (Vulcan, Martin Marietta, US Concrete, Summit Materials). Local hub-and-spoke economics, 30-min delivery radius. AI in dispatch/load optimization (Command Alkon) drives 3-5pp margin uplift; multiple arbitrage from ~5x single-plant to 9-11x platform.

327331.title: Concrete Block and Brick Manufacturing
327331.rationale: Fragmented (~632 firms, avg 27 employees); Oldcastle/CRH leading consolidator. Heavy/bulky product limits service radius — regional roll-up viable. AI for batching, curing optimization, and dispatch.

327332.title: Concrete Pipe Manufacturing
327332.rationale: Moderately consolidated (~144 firms) — Forterra, Oldcastle Infrastructure, Northern Concrete Pipe. Infrastructure-driven demand (IIJA tailwind). Smaller field for new roll-up; AI for casting/curing automation.

327390.title: Other Concrete Product Manufacturing
327390.rationale: Highly fragmented (~1,831 firms): precast panels, pavers, septic, vaults — regional players. Oldcastle and Quikrete leading consolidators. AI for CAD-to-mold, quoting, and shop scheduling has tangible roll-up multiple-arbitrage potential.

327410.title: Lime Manufacturing
327410.rationale: Tight oligopoly: Lhoist, Mississippi Lime, Graymont, Carmeuse. Kiln capex barrier. No roll-up; commodity AI use in kiln/CCS.

327420.title: Gypsum Product Manufacturing
327420.rationale: Five-firm oligopoly: USG (Knauf), Georgia-Pacific, National Gypsum, CertainTeed, American Gypsum control ~95%. No PE roll-up opportunity.

327910.title: Abrasive Product Manufacturing
327910.rationale: Fragmented mid-tier (~261 firms) under top players (Saint-Gobain, 3M, Tyrolit). Specialty/coated abrasives have niche fragmentation; AI in formulation and demand forecasting moderate.

327991.title: Cut Stone and Stone Product Manufacturing
327991.rationale: Very fragmented (~2,134 firms, avg 17 employees) — countertop fabricators, monument shops. Local-service economics, Cosentino/Cambria leading branded consolidators downstream. AI for CNC nesting, quoting, scheduling is genuine lever; labor-intensive limits replaceability.

327992.title: Ground or Treated Mineral and Earth Manufacturing
327992.rationale: Moderately fragmented (~265 firms) — talc, kaolin, silica grinding. Imerys, Covia, Minerals Technologies consolidating. AI used in particle-size control and demand modeling.

327993.title: Mineral Wool Manufacturing
327993.rationale: Consolidated: Owens Corning, Rockwool, Johns Manville, Knauf Insulation control most insulation/wool capacity. Capex barriers. No roll-up.

327999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Nonmetallic Mineral Product Manufacturing
327999.rationale: Catch-all category (~408 firms) — too heterogeneous (vermiculite, mica products, ceramic fiber, statuary) for unified roll-up thesis. Niche AI applications.

331110.title: Iron and Steel Mills and Ferroalloy Manufacturing
331110.rationale: Heavily consolidated: Nucor + Steel Dynamics + Cleveland-Cliffs + US Steel control ~75% of US capacity per AISI 2024. Capital intensity ($1B+ EAF) and union labor make AI replacement minimal; predictive maintenance only.

331210.title: Iron and Steel Pipe and Tube Manufacturing from Purchased Steel
331210.rationale: Moderate fragmentation (260 firms, 95 avg employees) with OCTG concentration (Tenaris, Vallourec) but plenty of mid-market welded pipe shops. AI mostly for scheduling/quality; physical forming process limits replaceability.

331221.title: Rolled Steel Shape Manufacturing
331221.rationale: Fragmented (230 firms, 57 avg employees) cold-rolled/special shapes serving construction & auto. Commodity margins thin; AI delivers modest gains in yield optimization but cap-ex barriers limit roll-up exit multiples.

331222.title: Steel Wire Drawing
331222.rationale: Fragmented (219 firms, 67 avg employees), Insteel/Bekaert lead but many regional shops. AI useful for tension/draw control; product remains commodity with cyclical pricing tied to construction.

331313.title: Alumina Refining and Primary Aluminum Production
331313.rationale: Extreme oligopoly: Alcoa, Century Aluminum, Rio Tinto operate ~all US smelters (34 firms total per CBP). Energy-intensive Hall-Heroult process; no roll-up runway, AI limited to pot-line optimization.

331314.title: Secondary Smelting and Alloying of Aluminum
331314.rationale: More fragmented than primary (95 firms, 60 avg employees) - secondary aluminum recyclers serve auto/can/construction. AI valuable for scrap sorting (LIBS, XRF) and metallurgy; Real Alloy and Novelis have rolled up regional players.

331315.title: Aluminum Sheet, Plate, and Foil Manufacturing
331315.rationale: Highly consolidated (80 firms, 233 avg employees): Novelis, Arconic, Constellium, Kaiser dominate; supplies auto body sheet & beverage can stock. Capital-intensive hot/cold rolling mills; AI for surface defect detection only.

331318.title: Other Aluminum Rolling, Drawing, and Extruding
331318.rationale: Most fragmented in 3313 (233 firms, 127 avg employees) - extrusions for construction, transportation, electrical. Bonnell/Hydro/Western consolidation underway but mid-market shops persist; press control & die-design AI emerging.

331410.title: Nonferrous Metal (except Aluminum) Smelting and Refining
331410.rationale: Copper, lead, zinc smelters - Freeport/Asarco dominate primary copper; 115 firms include smaller precious-metal refiners. Capital-intensive smelting; AI helps with ore-grade prediction but no roll-up thesis at scale.

331420.title: Copper Rolling, Drawing, Extruding, and Alloying
331420.rationale: Mid-fragmented (222 firms, 113 avg employees): Wieland (acquired Olin Brass 2019), Aurubis, Mueller Industries lead but ~200 specialty brass/copper shops remain. Wire & tube for HVAC/plumbing/EV; AI for melt chemistry.

331491.title: Nonferrous Metal (except Copper and Aluminum) Rolling, Drawing, and Extruding
331491.rationale: Highly fragmented (225 firms, 58 avg employees) - titanium, nickel, zinc, magnesium specialty mills. Defense/aerospace exposure (TIMET, ATI in titanium) but many small specialty alloy shops viable for roll-up.

331492.title: Secondary Smelting, Refining, and Alloying of Nonferrous Metal (except Copper and Aluminum)
331492.rationale: Most fragmented in 3314 (185 firms, 49 avg employees) - recyclers of zinc, lead, nickel, precious metals. Battery recycling growth (Li-Cycle, Redwood) attracting PE; AI for scrap sorting and assay.

331511.title: Iron Foundries
331511.rationale: Genuinely fragmented (286 firms, 113 avg employees) - gray iron, ductile iron castings for auto/heavy equipment/pipe. Neenah/Grede/Waupaca have consolidated but long tail remains; aging owners create roll-up opp.

331512.title: Steel Investment Foundries
331512.rationale: Precision castings for aerospace/medical/energy (112 firms, 128 avg employees). Howmet/PCC dominate aerospace tier-1 but mid-market for industrial/medical fragmented; AI valuable for wax-pattern/CT inspection.

331513.title: Steel Foundries (except Investment)
331513.rationale: Highly fragmented (148 firms, 66 avg employees) - sand-cast steel for mining/rail/construction equipment. Cyclical, capital-intensive; Bradken (Hitachi) consolidating mining sector but most independents remain.

331523.title: Nonferrous Metal Die-Casting Foundries
331523.rationale: Most fragmented in 3315 (321 firms, 89 avg employees) - zinc/magnesium die-casting for auto/electronics/hardware. Tesla giga-press disruption + EV migration; AI for machine vision QC and shot-control optimization.

331524.title: Aluminum Foundries (except Die-Casting)
331524.rationale: Most fragmented sub-industry in 331 (264 firms, 46 avg employees) - permanent mold/sand aluminum castings for auto/industrial. True roll-up profile: small owner-operated shops, succession issues; AI for pattern/gating design.

331529.title: Other Nonferrous Metal Foundries (except Die-Casting)
331529.rationale: Extremely fragmented (236 firms, 39 avg employees) - copper/brass/bronze/zinc sand & permanent mold castings for plumbing, marine, art. Classic mom-and-pop foundries; succession-driven roll-up but small TAM.

332111.title: Iron and Steel Forging
332111.rationale: Heavy CapEx (hammer/press lines), die-design AI helps but unionized labor and steel pass-through dominate margins; FIA reports concentration in top 30 forgers.
sources: Forging Industry Association (FIA) State of Industry 2024

332112.title: Nonferrous Forging
332112.rationale: Only ~52 firms, already consolidated around aero/defense OEMs (Howmet, Alcoa); limited roll-up runway and qualification barriers slow new AI adoption.

332114.title: Custom Roll Forming
332114.rationale: 391 firms with avg 41 employees — fragmented job-shop work; CAD-to-tool AI (BendTech, Bystronic) reduces setup time but mill tonnage CapEx caps multiple expansion.

332117.title: Powder Metallurgy Part Manufacturing
332117.rationale: MPIF reports ~140 PM firms with auto-tier exposure; additive/binder-jet AI (Desktop Metal, GKN) speeds DfAM but EV transition shrinks ICE part volumes.

332119.title: Metal Crown, Closure, and Other Metal Stamping (except Automotive)
332119.rationale: 1,145 firms, avg 41 emp — classic fragmented stamping; PMA notes succession crisis and AI quote/CAM (Paperless Parts, Tempus) gaining share, multiples 5-7x EBITDA.

332215.title: Metal Kitchen Cookware, Utensil, Cutlery, and Flatware (except Precious) Manufacturing
332215.rationale: 213 small firms but consumer-brand-driven, import competition crushing margins; AI marketing helps DTC plays but not core manufacturing economics.

332216.title: Saw Blade and Handtool Manufacturing
332216.rationale: 819 firms avg 31 emp — highly fragmented; Stanley/TTI dominate retail but specialty/industrial niches consolidable, AI helps SKU/inventory planning more than production.

332311.title: Prefabricated Metal Building and Component Manufacturing
332311.rationale: MBMA tracks ~800 firms; AI structural design (Tekla, IDEA StatiCa) and digital project management drive 2-3 pt margin lift in pre-engineered metal buildings.

332312.title: Fabricated Structural Metal Manufacturing
332312.rationale: 3,430 firms avg 31 emp — most fragmented sub in 3323; AISC notes Tekla+robotic welding+AI nesting (SigmaNEST) cut labor 15-25%; PE rollups active (CIVCO, AISC members).

332313.title: Plate Work Manufacturing
332313.rationale: 1,694 firms avg 25 emp — pressure vessels and plate fab very fragmented; AI weld inspection (Novarc, Kemppi) and nesting drive scrap reduction, multiples 5-6x.

332321.title: Metal Window and Door Manufacturing
332321.rationale: 1,018 firms but residential/comm subsegments differ; AAMA members consolidating (Cornerstone Building, MITER); AI quoting helps custom commercial work, residential is commoditized.

332322.title: Sheet Metal Work Manufacturing
332322.rationale: 4,605 firms avg 27 emp — most fragmented in NAICS 332; Paperless Parts/Xometry/Plethora deploying AI quoting/MES; HVAC-tier rollups (CECO, Comfort Systems) prove thesis.

332323.title: Ornamental and Architectural Metal Work Manufacturing
332323.rationale: 2,801 firms avg 14.8 emp — extremely fragmented micro-shop space (railings, stairs, decorative); AI design/quote tools relevant, but small ticket sizes limit M&A appeal below $5M EBITDA.

332410.title: Power Boiler and Heat Exchanger Manufacturing
332410.rationale: 258 firms, ASME-stamp barrier and EPC project nature; AI helps CFD/thermal design and field-service scheduling; PE active (Cleaver-Brooks, Babcock Wilcox spinouts).

332420.title: Metal Tank (Heavy Gauge) Manufacturing
332420.rationale: 708 firms avg 48 emp — regional tank fabricators serve oil/gas/water/chem; field-erection labor intensive, AI helps design/permitting; multiples 5-7x with cyclical exposure.

332431.title: Metal Can Manufacturing
332431.rationale: 175 plants but oligopoly (Ball, Crown, Ardagh control >80%); high-speed CapEx lines, AI vision QC mature; no roll-up opportunity left.

332439.title: Other Metal Container Manufacturing
332439.rationale: 276 firms making drums, IBCs, pails; Greif/Mauser dominate but regional pail/drum shops exist; AI limited impact on commoditized steel container production.

332510.title: Hardware Manufacturing
332510.rationale: 550 firms — locks/hinges/builders hardware; Allegion/ASSA ABLOY dominate residential, but commercial/specialty niches consolidable; import pressure from China caps margins.

332613.title: Spring Manufacturing
332613.rationale: 340 firms, SMI tracks consolidation; AI in coiler programming (WAFIOS, Bamatec) cuts setup; precision spring for med/aero earns higher multiples (6-8x).

332618.title: Other Fabricated Wire Product Manufacturing
332618.rationale: 676 firms avg 27 emp — wire mesh, baskets, racks; fragmented but commodity wire pass-through limits margin upside; AI helps custom-wire CAM and weld scheduling.

332710.title: Machine Shops
332710.rationale: 17,433 firms avg 12.5 emp — most fragmented in all manufacturing; NTMA succession crisis; AI quoting (Paperless Parts, Tempus, ToolingDocs) + CAM automation (Mastercam AI) drives 4-5 pt margin lift; PE thesis active (CORE Industrial, OpenGate, Lincoln Park).
sources: NTMA (National Tooling & Machining Assn) 2024 State of Industry

332721.title: Precision Turned Product Manufacturing
332721.rationale: 3,939 firms avg 26 emp — Swiss-screw/CNC turning shops; PMPA tracks high fragmentation; lights-out cells + AI tool monitoring (Caron Engineering, MachineMetrics) lift OEE 10-15%.

332722.title: Bolt, Nut, Screw, Rivet, and Washer Manufacturing
332722.rationale: 709 firms but IFI dominant majors (Hillman, MW Industries); cold-heading CapEx + import pressure; AI sortation/vision QC (Cognex, Keyence) standard; mid-tier rollups possible.

332811.title: Metal Heat Treating
332811.rationale: 725 firms avg 26 emp — Bodycote/Paulo aggressive consolidators; high margins from regional captive service; AI furnace control (Inductotherm, AFC-Holcroft) marginal vs. core operator skill.

332812.title: Metal Coating, Engraving (except Jewelry and Silverware), and Allied Services to Manufacturers
332812.rationale: 2,677 firms avg 20 emp — powder coating/painting job shops; very fragmented, regional moats; AI scheduling/job-tracking (Steelhead, JobBOSS) helps; environmental compliance friction.

332813.title: Electroplating, Plating, Polishing, Anodizing, and Coloring
332813.rationale: 2,005 firms — EPA/RCRA-heavy wet chemistry; NASF members face succession; rollup play exists (Pioneer Metal Finishing, Vergason) but AI applicability low — chemistry/permitting dominate.

332911.title: Industrial Valve Manufacturing
332911.rationale: 458 firms but VMA majors (Emerson, Flowserve, Crane); spec-driven oil/gas/power; AI helps engineered-to-order quoting and predictive maintenance services; multiples 7-9x for niche.

332912.title: Fluid Power Valve and Hose Fitting Manufacturing
332912.rationale: 343 firms but NFPA dominated by Parker, Eaton, Bosch Rexroth; distributor channel (FPDA) consolidating; AI helps config/quote and aftermarket service routing.

332913.title: Plumbing Fixture Fitting and Trim Manufacturing
332913.rationale: 128 firms — already consolidated (Masco, Fortune Brands, Kohler, Moen); lead-free regs raise barriers; limited rollup runway, AI applicable to design but not core thesis.

332919.title: Other Metal Valve and Pipe Fitting Manufacturing
332919.rationale: 215 firms — specialty fittings/couplings (Victaulic, Anvil); spec-driven, qualification heavy; modest rollup activity, AI impact limited to ERP/quoting.

332991.title: Ball and Roller Bearing Manufacturing
332991.rationale: 178 firms but global oligopoly (SKF, Timken, NSK, Schaeffler); precision grinding CapEx prohibitive; AI condition-monitoring services are bearing-OEM products, not roll-up plays.

332992.title: Small Arms Ammunition Manufacturing
332992.rationale: 175 firms — Vista Outdoor/Olin/Remington dominate; ATF licensing barriers; political/ESG headwinds restrict PE; AI applicable to QC but no consolidation thesis.

332993.title: Ammunition (except Small Arms) Manufacturing
332993.rationale: Only 61 firms avg 227 emp — defense primes (General Dynamics OTS, Northrop, BAE); DoD-spec, ITAR; not a rollup category for mid-market PE.

332994.title: Small Arms, Ordnance, and Ordnance Accessories Manufacturing
332994.rationale: 517 firms — firearms + accessories; FFL/ATF heavy regulation, ESG limits institutional capital; AI minor in CNC/marketing only.

332996.title: Fabricated Pipe and Pipe Fitting Manufacturing
332996.rationale: 724 firms — spool fab for oil/gas/petrochem; project-driven cyclicality; AI weld tracking (Lincoln, Miller) and digital spool drawings (Coade, Smap3D) standard; rollup pockets in pipe-fab niche.

332999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing
332999.rationale: 3,410 firms avg 20 emp — catch-all bucket (safes, ladders, etc.) so heterogeneous it's uninvestable as single thesis; sub-niche rollups possible but require granular sourcing.

333111.title: Farm Machinery and Equipment Manufacturing
333111.rationale: Dominated by Deere/CNH/AGCO duopoly with long tail of attachment makers; AEM data shows top 4 control 65%+ of large ag equipment shipments. AI useful for precision-ag firmware but core CapEx-heavy fabrication unchanged.

333112.title: Lawn and Garden Tractor and Home Lawn and Garden Equipment Manufacturing
333112.rationale: Highly consolidated under Husqvarna, Toro, Stanley B&D, MTD/Stihl; only 143 firms with avg 128 employees indicates branded scale players. Commodity outdoor power equipment with thin margins, low PE roll-up appeal.

333120.title: Construction Machinery Manufacturing
333120.rationale: Caterpillar/Komatsu/Volvo/Deere dominate; 666 firms include attachment and specialty equipment makers in tail. AI telematics meaningful at OEM level (Cat VisionLink) but fabrication economics unchanged.

333131.title: Mining Machinery and Equipment Manufacturing
333131.rationale: 268 firms avg 38 employees—more fragmented than other 3331 codes due to specialty underground/surface equipment niches. Cyclical with commodity prices; PE active in mining services but equipment OEMs rare targets.

333132.title: Oil and Gas Field Machinery and Equipment Manufacturing
333132.rationale: 626 firms avg 48 employees—fragmented frac/wellhead/completion suppliers below SLB/Halliburton/Baker. Highly cyclical with rig count; PE roll-ups attempted in pressure pumping but commodity exposure deters.

333241.title: Food Product Machinery Manufacturing
333241.rationale: 512 firms avg 36 employees—genuinely fragmented with regional specialists in baking, meat, dairy, beverage equipment. Active PE consolidation (JBT, Middleby, Duravant) at strategic multiples 10-13x EBITDA.

333242.title: Semiconductor Machinery Manufacturing
333242.rationale: Extreme oligopoly: ASML/AMAT/LAM/TEL/KLA control 85%+ of wafer-fab equipment; 181 firms includes specialty parts suppliers. Trades at 18-25x EBITDA—zero PE roll-up arbitrage, strategic-only consolidation.

333243.title: Sawmill, Woodworking, and Paper Machinery Manufacturing
333243.rationale: 375 firms avg 35 employees—fragmented serving declining paper and stable lumber markets. Mature low-growth industry; PE roll-ups rare due to end-market headwinds.

333248.title: All Other Industrial Machinery Manufacturing
333248.rationale: Catch-all bucket covering plastics, rubber, textile, glass, chemical, pharma equipment—genuinely fragmented by sub-niche. PE interest selective (Roper, IDEX play here) at 11-14x for niche leaders.

333310.title: Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
333310.rationale: Includes commercial laundry, vending, dry cleaning, optical equipment—fragmented sub-sectors with regional players. Modest PE interest at category level (Alliance Laundry, Welbilt) but parent NAICS lacks coherent roll-up thesis.

333413.title: Industrial and Commercial Fan and Blower and Air Purification Equipment Manufacturing
333413.rationale: 472 firms avg 61 employees; benefits from IAQ tailwinds post-COVID and data center cooling demand. Madison Industries/Nortek/Greenheck roll-ups active at 10-13x EBITDA in fan/blower segment.

333414.title: Heating Equipment (except Warm Air Furnaces) Manufacturing
333414.rationale: 360 firms include boilers, water heaters, radiators—moderate fragmentation below Rheem/AO Smith/Bradford White. Electrification headwinds for gas products offset by heat pump growth; selective PE activity.

333415.title: Air-Conditioning and Warm Air Heating Equipment and Commercial and Industrial Refrigeration Equipment Manufacturing
333415.rationale: Concentrated at top (Carrier/Trane/Lennox/Daikin/Goodman) but 829 firms include component and refrigeration specialists. Heat pump tailwinds + data center cooling driving strategic premium (15-18x EBITDA); roll-up arb modest.

333511.title: Industrial Mold Manufacturing
333511.rationale: 1,286 firms avg 24 employees—classic fragmented job-shop sector. AI/generative design + CAM automation meaningful for mold design cycle time; active PE roll-up theses (Westfall, Industrial Growth Partners) at 5-7x EBITDA.

333514.title: Special Die and Tool, Die Set, Jig, and Fixture Manufacturing
333514.rationale: 2,006 firms avg 20 employees—most fragmented machinery sub-code. CAM software + AI quoting/programming material; succession-driven sub-$5M shops at 4-6x EBITDA exit to PE platforms (Pridgeon & Clay, etc).

333515.title: Cutting Tool and Machine Tool Accessory Manufacturing
333515.rationale: 1,094 firms avg 22 employees—fragmented inserts/tooling/accessories segment below Kennametal/Sandvik/IMC. Some PE consolidation but specialty carbide tooling requires deep metallurgy expertise.

333517.title: Machine Tool Manufacturing
333517.rationale: 1,200 firms avg 24 employees—US machine tool fragmented but dominated by imports (DMG Mori, Mazak, Okuma Japan/Germany). Cyclical CapEx with thin margins; US PE roll-ups rare due to import competition.

333519.title: Rolling Mill and Other Metalworking Machinery Manufacturing
333519.rationale: 312 firms avg 37 employees—specialty rolling mill, forming, shearing equipment. Heavy engineered project work serving steel/aluminum mills; cyclical with metals capex, limited PE roll-up logic.

333611.title: Turbine and Turbine Generator Set Units Manufacturing
333611.rationale: Extreme oligopoly: GE Vernova/Siemens Energy/Mitsubishi/Vestas dominate; 208 firms include component suppliers. Strategic asset class with national security implications; not a PE roll-up category.

333612.title: Speed Changer, Industrial High-Speed Drive, and Gear Manufacturing
333612.rationale: 201 firms avg 53 employees—gear/gearbox makers serving wind, mining, oil & gas. Regal Rexnord, Timken, Sumitomo dominate; selective PE in precision gears (Genesis, NN Inc) at 8-11x.

333613.title: Mechanical Power Transmission Equipment Manufacturing
333613.rationale: 226 firms avg 69 employees—bearings/couplings/chains/belts; oligopoly at top (Timken, Regal, SKF, Schaeffler) with tail of specialty. Industrial distribution roll-ups more attractive than manufacturing.

333618.title: Other Engine Equipment Manufacturing
333618.rationale: 273 firms avg 151 employees—diesel/gas engines, dominated by Cummins/Caterpillar/MTU/Wartsila. Scale-driven engineering category; electrification headwinds for some applications. Not a roll-up category.

333912.title: Air and Gas Compressor Manufacturing
333912.rationale: 282 firms avg 68 employees—Atlas Copco, Ingersoll Rand, Gardner Denver, Sullair dominate; tail of specialty/portable compressors. Service/aftermarket roll-ups more active than OEM consolidation.

333914.title: Measuring, Dispensing, and Other Pumping Equipment Manufacturing
333914.rationale: 547 firms avg 68 employees—industrial pumps, dispensing systems. Flowserve, Xylem, Pentair, ITT dominate; PE active in specialty pumps (CIRCOR, SPX Technologies) at 11-14x.

333921.title: Elevator and Moving Stairway Manufacturing
333921.rationale: 182 firms but Otis/KONE/Schindler/TK Elevator control 70%+ globally. Manufacturing oligopoly; service/maintenance is the roll-up category (NAICS 238290) not manufacturing.

333922.title: Conveyor and Conveying Equipment Manufacturing
333922.rationale: 785 firms avg 53 employees—fragmented serving warehousing/logistics/material handling boom. E-commerce + automation tailwinds; Honeywell Intelligrated/Dematic/Daifuku consolidate at premium 12-16x.

333923.title: Overhead Traveling Crane, Hoist, and Monorail System Manufacturing
333923.rationale: 321 firms avg 62 employees—Konecranes, Columbus McKinnon, Demag dominate; tail of regional crane fabricators. Service+rental more attractive than manufacturing roll-up.

333924.title: Industrial Truck, Tractor, Trailer, and Stacker Machinery Manufacturing
333924.rationale: Forklifts/lift trucks—Toyota Material Handling, KION, Hyster-Yale, Jungheinrich dominate globally. AGV/AMR autonomous adoption growing but at OEM/strategic level, not roll-up.

333991.title: Power-Driven Handtool Manufacturing
333991.rationale: 121 firms but consolidated under Stanley Black & Decker, TTI (Milwaukee/Ryobi), Bosch, Makita, Hilti. Strong brands + battery platform moats; not a PE roll-up category.

333992.title: Welding and Soldering Equipment Manufacturing
333992.rationale: 520 firms avg 29 employees—fragmented below Lincoln Electric, Miller (ITW), ESAB. Specialty welding automation/robotic systems attracting PE; commodity equipment less attractive.

333993.title: Packaging Machinery Manufacturing
333993.rationale: 516 firms avg 51 employees—genuinely fragmented serving food/beverage/pharma/consumer. Most active PE roll-up category in 333 (Duravant, ProMach, Barry-Wehmiller, IPG) at 11-14x EBITDA.

333994.title: Industrial Process Furnace and Oven Manufacturing
333994.rationale: 312 firms avg 32 employees—specialty industrial heat treating, batch ovens, kilns. Highly engineered project work; thermal processing PE activity exists (Wabash, Despatch) but niche.

333995.title: Fluid Power Cylinder and Actuator Manufacturing
333995.rationale: 289 firms avg 67 employees—Parker Hannifin, Eaton, Bosch Rexroth dominate; tail of specialty cylinder makers. Mature industrial hydraulics with electrification disruption risk.

333996.title: Fluid Power Pump and Motor Manufacturing
333996.rationale: 149 firms avg 85 employees—Parker, Eaton/Danfoss, Bosch Rexroth, Kawasaki dominate hydraulic pumps/motors. Concentrated scale category, not a roll-up target.

333998.title: All Other Miscellaneous General Purpose Machinery Manufacturing
333998.rationale: Catch-all bucket for scales, balances, fire suppression, lab equipment, vacuum equipment—fragmented by sub-niche. Selective PE interest (Roper, IDEX, Watts) at category-leader premiums.

334111.title: Electronic Computer Manufacturing
334111.rationale: Dominated by Apple/Dell/HP/Lenovo with 224 firms but top 5 >85% share (Gartner 2025); contract assembly already automated by Foxconn/Pegatron. No PE roll-up thesis.

334112.title: Computer Storage Device Manufacturing
334112.rationale: Seagate/WD/Samsung/Kioxia oligopoly with ~90% HDD+SSD share (TrendForce 2025); 68 US firms are mostly enterprise NAS niche. Capital-intensive, no fragmentation.

334118.title: Computer Terminal and Other Computer Peripheral Equipment Manufacturing
334118.rationale: 299 firms but Logitech/HP/Zebra/Honeywell dominate keyboards/printers/scanners; small POS/AIDC niche players exist. Hardware-heavy capex; AI mostly software-side.

334210.title: Telephone Apparatus Manufacturing
334210.rationale: Cisco/Poly/Mitel/Yealink dominate VoIP+handsets; secular decline as cloud UCaaS replaces hardware. 172 firms but shrinking TAM.

334220.title: Radio and Television Broadcasting and Wireless Communications Equipment Manufacturing
334220.rationale: Ericsson/Nokia/Samsung/ZTE/Huawei dominate base stations; Qualcomm/Broadcom dominate chips. 664 US firms include smaller RF/antenna/military comms specialists with some niche fragmentation.

334290.title: Other Communications Equipment Manufacturing
334290.rationale: 334 firms, avg 38 employees — fire alarms, intercoms, traffic signals, intercommunication systems with regional installers; meaningfully fragmented. Some bolt-on roll-up logic for security/access subsegments.

334310.title: Audio and Video Equipment Manufacturing
334310.rationale: Sony/Samsung/LG/Bose/Sonos dominate consumer; 543 US firms incl. pro audio/AV integrators (Shure, QSC, Crestron). Margins low; commoditization severe; AI mostly in DSP firmware.

334412.title: Bare Printed Circuit Board Manufacturing
334412.rationale: 376 US firms, genuinely fragmented (TTM Tech largest at ~15% domestic share); 90%+ global PCB output offshored to China/Taiwan. CHIPS Act reshoring tailwind; defense/medical mil-spec niches. Heavy capex limits multiples.

334413.title: Semiconductor and Related Device Manufacturing
334413.rationale: TSMC/Samsung/Intel/SK Hynix/Micron oligopoly; leading-edge fab >$20B capex. 791 US firms include specialty analog/power (TI, ADI, ON Semi) and small fabless — but PE roll-up impossible at scale. AI demand boosts incumbents not roll-ups.

334416.title: Capacitor, Resistor, Coil, Transformer, and Other Inductor Manufacturing
334416.rationale: Murata/TDK/Yageo/Vishay/KEMET dominate passives globally (>60%); 330 US firms include specialty mil/aero magnetics and custom transformer shops. Some bolt-on logic for niche power magnetics.

334417.title: Electronic Connector Manufacturing
334417.rationale: TE Connectivity/Amphenol/Molex/JAE control ~50% global; 205 US firms incl. mil-spec/RF specialists. Higher margins than PCBs but consolidation already advanced (TE+Amphenol 100+ acquisitions historically).

334418.title: Printed Circuit Assembly (Electronic Assembly) Manufacturing
334418.rationale: EMS sector genuinely fragmented below tier-1 (Foxconn/Flex/Jabil/Sanmina); 852 US firms, mostly low-volume/high-mix shops serving mil/aero/medical. Active PE roll-up thesis (e.g., NEO Tech, IEC, Cyient DLM). Multiples 6-8x EBITDA.

334419.title: Other Electronic Component Manufacturing
334419.rationale: 1,097 firms, avg 47 employees — most fragmented in 3344. Includes wire harness, cable assembly, custom electronic modules. Several active roll-ups (e.g., Volex, Bel Fuse acquisitions). Reasonable bolt-on candidate.

334510.title: Electromedical and Electrotherapeutic Apparatus Manufacturing
334510.rationale: GE Healthcare/Philips/Siemens/Medtronic dominate imaging+monitoring; 923 firms include specialty device makers. FDA moat + high margins; AI in imaging/diagnostics adds 4pp. Medtech PE roll-ups exist but require FDA QSR expertise; multiples 10-14x.

334511.title: Search, Detection, Navigation, Guidance, Aeronautical, and Nautical System and Instrument Manufacturing
334511.rationale: Defense primes (RTX/L3Harris/Northrop/Lockheed/General Dynamics) consume most demand; 503 firms but 250 avg employees indicates large incumbents. ITAR moat blocks PE roll-ups; Garmin dominates commercial GPS.

334512.title: Automatic Environmental Control Manufacturing for Residential, Commercial, and Appliance Use
334512.rationale: Honeywell/Johnson Controls/Emerson/Siemens/Schneider dominate building controls; 240 US firms include niche BMS/HVAC controls. AI (predictive maintenance, energy optimization) genuine tailwind. Some bolt-on potential for specialty controls.

334513.title: Instruments and Related Products Manufacturing for Measuring, Displaying, and Controlling Industrial Process Variables
334513.rationale: Emerson/Honeywell/Yokogawa/Endress+Hauser/ABB lead industrial automation; 840 US firms, avg 41 employees — meaningful niche specialty fragmentation in sensors/transmitters/process analyzers. Reasonable roll-up candidate (e.g., Roper Tech model).

334514.title: Totalizing Fluid Meter and Counting Device Manufacturing
334514.rationale: Badger Meter/Roper/Xylem/Itron dominate water+gas meters; 165 firms with regulated utility customers. Smart meter cycle ongoing; AI in leak detection. Moderate consolidation, some niche bolt-ons.

334515.title: Instrument Manufacturing for Measuring and Testing Electricity and Electrical Signals
334515.rationale: Keysight/Tektronix(Fortive)/Rohde&Schwarz/Anritsu dominate T&M; 675 firms include specialty calibration/test shops. Some fragmentation in low-end calibration services. Fortive/Roper history of bolt-on consolidation.

334516.title: Analytical Laboratory Instrument Manufacturing
334516.rationale: Thermo Fisher/Danaher/Agilent/Waters/Bruker oligopoly in lab instruments (>60% share); 647 US firms include niche specialty analyzers. High margins+AI in chromatography/MS/sequencing analytics. Limited PE entry due to incumbent acquisition appetite.

334517.title: Irradiation Apparatus Manufacturing
334517.rationale: Varian(Siemens Healthineers)/Elekta/Accuray/Mevion dominate radiotherapy and industrial irradiation; 130 firms but avg 90 employees → consolidated. FDA+NRC moats; AI in treatment planning. Not a PE roll-up sector.

334519.title: Other Measuring and Controlling Device Manufacturing
334519.rationale: 887 firms, avg 39 employees — catch-all for specialty measurement (geophysical, watches, drafting, surveying). Genuine fragmentation; Trimble/Hexagon active acquirers. Bolt-on candidate for niche sensor/survey instruments.

334610.title: Manufacturing and Reproducing Magnetic and Optical Media
334610.rationale: Secularly dead: CD/DVD/Blu-ray/tape duplication replaced by streaming+cloud. CBP suppressed employment (de minimis). LTO tape sole growth area (IBM/Quantum/Fujifilm). No PE thesis.

335131.title: Residential Electric Lighting Fixture Manufacturing
335131.rationale: Moderately fragmented but commodity hardware dominated by import competition (China/Vietnam); design/sourcing partially AI-augmentable, manufacturing largely manual assembly.

335132.title: Commercial, Industrial, and Institutional Electric Lighting Fixture Manufacturing
335132.rationale: Semi-consolidated (Acuity, Hubbell, Signify ~40%+ share); spec-grade controls/IoT integration creates real AI/automation lever in design and lighting controls software.

335139.title: Electric Lamp Bulb and Other Lighting Equipment Manufacturing
335139.rationale: LED transition consolidated category around Signify, GE Current, Cree/Wolfspeed; capital-intensive with commoditized LED chip economics — limited PE roll-up runway.

335210.title: Small Electrical Appliance Manufacturing
335210.rationale: 180 firms but heavily import-dependent (SharkNinja, Hamilton Beach, Conair dominate brand layer); domestic manufacturing thin-margin contract assembly with limited AI lever.

335220.title: Major Household Appliance Manufacturing
335220.rationale: Pure oligopoly: Whirlpool, GE Appliances (Haier), Electrolux, LG, Samsung control ~80%+ US market; not roll-uppable, capital-intensive plants, scale-dependent.

335311.title: Power, Distribution, and Specialty Transformer Manufacturing
335311.rationale: Tight supply (grid backlog 2-4yr lead times), Eaton/Hitachi Energy/Siemens Energy/Schneider dominant in large units but specialty/distribution tier has 200+ smaller shops; multiples expanding from supply scarcity.

335312.title: Motor and Generator Manufacturing
335312.rationale: 402 firms — relatively fragmented in industrial motors below NEMA frame top tier (ABB, Nidec, Regal Rexnord, WEG lead); repair/rewind and specialty generator shops viable for roll-up.

335313.title: Switchgear and Switchboard Apparatus Manufacturing
335313.rationale: Data center / grid supercycle driving 18-24mo lead times; Eaton, Schneider, ABB, Siemens dominate but 467 firms include custom switchboard integrators benefiting from spillover demand; multiples elevated.

335314.title: Relay and Industrial Control Manufacturing
335314.rationale: Most fragmented in 3353 with 729 firms avg 46 emp; control panel/systems integrators viable roll-up (Rockwell/ABB/Siemens dominate components, integration layer fragmented); AI lever in engineering/quoting software.

335910.title: Battery Manufacturing
335910.rationale: Capital-intensive consolidating around LG ES, CATL, Panasonic, Tesla, Samsung SDI (Li-ion); lead-acid tier (EnerSys, Stryten) also consolidated; not PE roll-up territory, scale-driven.

335921.title: Fiber Optic Cable Manufacturing
335921.rationale: Concentrated around Corning, CommScope, Prysmian, Sumitomo; only 116 firms with avg 68 emp; AI data center buildout creating supply tightness but oligopoly structure limits roll-up.

335929.title: Other Communication and Energy Wire Manufacturing
335929.rationale: Southwire, Encore Wire, Prysmian, Nexans dominate; commodity copper exposure compresses margins; 212 firms but capital-intensive extrusion limits sub-scale viability.

335931.title: Current-Carrying Wiring Device Manufacturing
335931.rationale: Leviton, Hubbell, Legrand, Eaton dominate branded outlets/plugs/EV connectors; 390 firms include EV charging hardware specialty shops with strategic-buyer demand; multiples elevated by electrification.

335932.title: Noncurrent-Carrying Wiring Device Manufacturing
335932.rationale: Boxes, conduit fittings, raceways — Atkore, Hubbell, ABB Thomas & Betts, nVent dominant; 148 firms with healthy 100-emp avg suggest consolidation already advanced; commodity steel/plastic exposure.

335991.title: Carbon and Graphite Product Manufacturing
335991.rationale: GrafTech, Tokai Carbon, Mersen dominate electrodes; specialty graphite for EV/semis tightening supply; 177 firms but materials science moat blocks classic PE consolidation.

335999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Electrical Equipment and Component Manufacturing
335999.rationale: Most fragmented bucket in 335 with 805 firms avg 46 emp — heterogeneous mix of surge protectors, electromagnets, capacitors, specialty components; thesis viability depends entirely on narrow sub-segment selection.

336110.title: Automobile and Light Duty Motor Vehicle Manufacturing
336110.rationale: Pure oligopoly: Ford, GM, Stellantis, Toyota, Honda, Tesla dominate US light-vehicle assembly with capex >$5B/plant. Zero roll-up viability; SEC 10-Ks show top 5 OEMs >90% share.
sources: AAM (Alliance for Automotive Manufacturers) 2024 production data

336120.title: Heavy Duty Truck Manufacturing
336120.rationale: Class 8 truck oligopoly: PACCAR, Daimler Truck NA, Volvo/Mack, Navistar control ~98% share per AAM. 97 firms but top 4 = >95% volume; no roll-up arbitrage.

336211.title: Motor Vehicle Body Manufacturing
336211.rationale: 818 firms avg 67 employees — genuinely fragmented (truck bodies, upfitters, specialty bodies). Roll-up plays exist (Reading Truck, Knapheide). Multiples 5-7x for sub-$50M EBITDA.

336212.title: Truck Trailer Manufacturing
336212.rationale: Wabash, Great Dane, Utility Trailer dominate dry/refrigerated van; 549 firms but top 5 ~70% share. Tail of specialty trailer makers fragmented but cyclical (Wabash 10-K shows -30% peak-trough).

336213.title: Motor Home Manufacturing
336213.rationale: Class A/B/C motorhomes: Thor Industries + Winnebago + REV Group = ~80% share per SEC filings. 76 firms but consolidation already done; deeply cyclical (RVIA shipments -40% 2022-23).

336214.title: Travel Trailer and Camper Manufacturing
336214.rationale: Travel trailer/camper: Thor + Forest River (Berkshire) ~75% share but 723 firms in long tail (teardrop, overland, specialty). Cyclical demand kills multiples; 5-6x typical.

336310.title: Motor Vehicle Gasoline Engine and Engine Parts Manufacturing
336310.rationale: 699 firms making ICE engines/parts — EV transition is structural headwind, depressing multiples. Tier-2 fragmentation real but exit-risk high. AI helps in CNC/QA scheduling.

336320.title: Motor Vehicle Electrical and Electronic Equipment Manufacturing
336320.rationale: 557 firms in EV-relevant niche (lighting, harnesses, sensors, infotainment subassembly). Tailwind from EV/ADAS BoM growth. Aptiv/Lear/Visteon at top but mid-market fragmented; 6-8x multiples.

336330.title: Motor Vehicle Steering and Suspension Components (except Spring) Manufacturing
336330.rationale: Steering/suspension dominated by ZF, American Axle, Dana, Nexteer. 277 firms but top 6 ~75%. Tier-3 stamping/forging shops thin margins, low multiples.

336340.title: Motor Vehicle Brake System Manufacturing
336340.rationale: Brake systems: Bosch, Continental, Brembo, ZF TRW oligopoly per SEC filings; 166 firms but tail is friction-material makers tied to OEM contracts. Low fragmentation.

336350.title: Motor Vehicle Transmission and Power Train Parts Manufacturing
336350.rationale: Transmissions/power train: ZF, Allison, BorgWarner, Magna PT lead; 464 firms avg 173 employees suggests capital-intensive, not roll-up-friendly. EV simplification compresses long-term TAM.

336360.title: Motor Vehicle Seating and Interior Trim Manufacturing
336360.rationale: Seating: Lear + Adient + Magna Seating ~85% global share per 10-Ks. 434 firms but most are sub-suppliers (foam, trim); OEM-captive contracts crush margins. Not roll-up viable.

336370.title: Motor Vehicle Metal Stamping
336370.rationale: Metal stamping: 717 firms, avg 141 employees — genuinely fragmented. Mid-market roll-ups (MMC, Trans-Matic) exist; AI delta in CNC/press scheduling. 5-7x multiples, cyclical risk.

336390.title: Other Motor Vehicle Parts Manufacturing
336390.rationale: 1,326 firms — most fragmented auto-parts code. Catch-all for HVAC, exhaust, mirrors, fasteners. Roll-up plays exist (Modine, Standard Motor Products acquisitions). 6-8x multiples for quality assets.

336411.title: Aircraft Manufacturing
336411.rationale: Boeing + Airbus duopoly in commercial; Lockheed/Northrop/Boeing in defense. 368 firms but top 5 = >95% revenue. Zero roll-up; FAA/DOD certification moats absolute.

336412.title: Aircraft Engine and Engine Parts Manufacturing
336412.rationale: Aircraft engines: GE Aerospace, RTX/Pratt, Rolls-Royce, Safran oligopoly per AIA. 438 firms but top 4 OEMs ~90% share; tail is MRO/parts shops subject to FAA PMA process.

336413.title: Other Aircraft Parts and Auxiliary Equipment Manufacturing
336413.rationale: 908 firms in aerospace parts/auxiliary equipment — most fragmented aero code. AS9100-certified machine shops roll-up plays (TransDigm strategy, Heico). 8-12x multiples for niche IP holders.

336414.title: Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Manufacturing
336414.rationale: Missile/space vehicles: Lockheed, Northrop, RTX, SpaceX, Boeing dominate. 49 firms with avg 737 employees = capital-heavy primes. ITAR/DOD moats; no roll-up.

336415.title: Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Propulsion Unit and Propulsion Unit Parts Manufacturing
336415.rationale: Rocket propulsion: Aerojet (now L3Harris), Northrop, SpaceX, Blue Origin. 34 firms, avg 1,070 employees — pure prime contractor space. No fragmentation.

336419.title: Other Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Parts and Auxiliary Equipment Manufacturing
336419.rationale: 43 firms avg 162 employees in missile parts — small but ITAR-certified niche suppliers. Some roll-up potential via defense-tech consolidators but tiny TAM and clearance gating.

336510.title: Railroad Rolling Stock Manufacturing
336510.rationale: Rail rolling stock: Wabtec, Trinity Industries, Greenbrier, Progress Rail dominate per SEC filings. 211 firms but top 4 ~85% share; capital-intensive. AAR car build data cyclical.

336611.title: Ship Building and Repairing
336611.rationale: Ship building: HII, General Dynamics NASSCO/EB lead Navy work; commercial yards limited. 489 firms includes repair yards but big yards = oligopoly. Jones Act protects but caps growth.

336612.title: Boat Building
336612.rationale: 903 boat builders avg 53 employees — highly fragmented (Brunswick #1 ~20% share). Roll-up plays (Brunswick, Malibu, MasterCraft acquisitions). Cyclical luxury demand; 5-7x.

336991.title: Motorcycle, Bicycle, and Parts Manufacturing
336991.rationale: 429 firms avg 20 employees — micro-fragmented (Harley, Polaris at top; long tail of e-bike, specialty bicycle, scooter assembly). E-bike tailwind but import competition fierce.

336992.title: Military Armored Vehicle, Tank, and Tank Component Manufacturing
336992.rationale: Military armored: General Dynamics Land Systems, BAE, Oshkosh Defense. 71 firms but top 3 = >90% DOD contracts. Clearance/ITAR moats; no PE roll-up viable.

336999.title: All Other Transportation Equipment Manufacturing
336999.rationale: 457 firms avg 42 employees — catch-all (ATVs, golf carts, snowmobiles, specialty utility vehicles). Polaris, Textron dominate top; long tail of niche makers. Roll-up plausible but TAM small.

337110.title: Wood Kitchen Cabinet and Countertop Manufacturing
337110.rationale: Highly fragmented (~6,100 firms, avg 17 employees) with strong local installer/dealer relationships; AI helps with CNC nesting, quoting, and design-to-cut but physical fabrication and on-site install limit replaceability. Cabinet Discounters/MasterBrands consolidation showing 7-9x EBITDA arbitrage for sub-$10M targets.

337121.title: Upholstered Household Furniture Manufacturing
337121.rationale: Only ~1,000 firms with avg 71 employees; ~75% of US demand met by imports from Vietnam/China per AHFA, leaving domestic mfrs squeezed on margins. La-Z-Boy (LZB) and RH show single-digit EBITDA at scale; roll-up arbitrage muted by capital-intensive cut-and-sew and labor dependency.

337122.title: Nonupholstered Wood Household Furniture Manufacturing
337122.rationale: ~2,100 firms avg 11 employees, mostly small custom/Amish shops; US production collapsed post-2000 as imports (Vietnam, Malaysia) captured 70%+ of case-goods market per AHFA. Low margins and weak pricing power make roll-up math thin despite fragmentation.

337126.title: Household Furniture (except Wood and Upholstered) Manufacturing
337126.rationale: Small residual category (metal/plastic/rattan furniture) heavily import-dominated; CBP suppresses firm count suggesting <200 establishments. Limited domestic roll-up opportunity at scale.

337127.title: Institutional Furniture Manufacturing
337127.rationale: 530 firms avg 39 employees serving K-12, healthcare, dorms, corrections; contract-driven with GPO/sourcewell channels favor sub-scale players. Virco (VIRC) and KI show 8-11% EBITDA; recurring institutional refresh cycles support 6-9x multiples in mid-market.

337211.title: Wood Office Furniture Manufacturing
337211.rationale: 307 firms but post-COVID office furniture demand fell ~25% per BIFMA; Kimball International acquired by HNI 2023, signaling consolidation done at top. Smaller custom desk/conference shops face weak demand and execution risk on roll-up.

337212.title: Custom Architectural Woodwork and Millwork Manufacturing
337212.rationale: Most fragmented sub-sector: 2,352 firms avg 18 employees, project-based work for hospitality/retail/luxury residential with strong AWI-certified niche pricing. AI estimating, CNC programming, and BIM-to-shop drawing automation deliver meaningful margin lift; sub-$5M EBITDA shops trade 4-6x, consolidator can re-rate to 8-10x.

337214.title: Office Furniture (except Wood) Manufacturing
337214.rationale: Highly consolidated: 205 firms avg 92 employees; Steelcase (SCS), MillerKnoll (MLKN, post-Knoll), HNI control ~65% of US systems furniture per BIFMA. No room for roll-up arbitrage at scale.

337215.title: Showcase, Partition, Shelving, and Locker Manufacturing
337215.rationale: 823 firms avg 37 employees serving retail fixtures, lab casework, storage; mixed consolidation with leaders like Leggett & Platt store fixtures unit and Hufcor. Custom project work and B2B contract nature support 7-9x multiples for $1-3M EBITDA platforms.

337910.title: Mattress Manufacturing
337910.rationale: Top 4 (Serta Simmons, Tempur Sealy, Sleep Number, Purple) hold ~70% share per ISPA; Tempur-Mattress Firm merger 2024 finalized vertical integration. AI in DTC marketing/personalization helps online challengers but physical mfg is consolidated. Roll-up window largely closed.
sources: ISPA (International Sleep Products Association) 2024 Industry Report

337920.title: Blind and Shade Manufacturing
337920.rationale: 329 firms avg 35 employees; Hunter Douglas (private/3G), Springs Window Fashions dominate top tier but install/dealer network of custom workrooms is fragmented. AI configurators, automated cutting, and motorized smart-shade upsell create both margin and tech-enabled platform thesis at 7-9x.

339112.title: Surgical and Medical Instrument Manufacturing
339112.rationale: Oligopoly: Medtronic, Stryker, BD, J&J MedTech dominate; top 50 firms ~70% revenue per AdvaMed. FDA 510(k)/PMA regulatory moat blocks small-PE roll-ups; multiples 15-25x EBITDA at top-tier — no arbitrage left.

339113.title: Surgical Appliance and Supplies Manufacturing
339113.rationale: More fragmented than 339112 (1828 firms, avg 52 employees) — orthotics, bandages, hospital supplies — but Cardinal/Owens & Minor/Medline distribution control + FDA Class I/II compliance burden cap roll-up returns. Some PE activity in wound care.

339114.title: Dental Equipment and Supplies Manufacturing
339114.rationale: Henry Schein/Patterson/Dentsply Sirona consolidate distribution; 500 manufacturers moderately fragmented. CAD/CAM and AI-driven imaging (Align, 3Shape) reshaping; mid-market multiples 8-12x — some arbitrage for clear-aligner/3D-print niches.
sources: SEC 10-K: Dentsply Sirona, Henry Schein, Align Technology

339115.title: Ophthalmic Goods Manufacturing
339115.rationale: EssilorLuxottica + Alcon + Bausch+Lomb + CooperVision oligopoly — contacts/IOLs/lenses globally consolidated. 450 US firms but top 10 ~80% revenue. No PE arbitrage; EssilorLuxottica trades 13-15x.

339116.title: Dental Laboratories
339116.rationale: 4928 firms, avg 8.6 employees — highly fragmented service biz (crown/bridge/denture fabrication). Modern Dental + National Dentex roll-ups validate PE thesis; AI/CAD-CAM/3D printing displacing manual technicians (V=6). Sub-$1M EBITDA multiples 4-6x → platform 8-10x arbitrage.

339910.title: Jewelry and Silverware Manufacturing
339910.rationale: 1879 firms, avg 10.6 employees — atomized artisan/family workshops. Tiny EBITDA ($50-150K typical), brand-driven economics, lab-grown diamonds disrupting margins. Jewelers of America notes PE largely avoids; no scalable thesis.
sources: Jewelers of America 2024 State of the Industry
MJSA cost of doing business report

339920.title: Sporting and Athletic Goods Manufacturing
339920.rationale: Brand-led (Nike, Wilson, Callaway, YETI public); 1673 firms include niche bike/fish/hunt manufacturers. PE plays at category level (Sycamore-Hayfin gym equipment); long-tail too brand-dependent for pure manufacturing roll-up.

339930.title: Doll, Toy, and Game Manufacturing
339930.rationale: 503 US firms — 80%+ toy production offshored to China (Toy Assoc). Hasbro/Mattel/Spin Master capture brand value; US manufacturing residual. Hit-driven, cyclical, IP-licensed economics — PE roll-up returns weak.

339940.title: Office Supplies (except Paper) Manufacturing
339940.rationale: Secular decline as offices digitize; ACCO Brands, Newell Rubbermaid, 3M dominate. 376 firms with import competition from China. Low growth + low multiples (5-7x) — limited PE interest.

339950.title: Sign Manufacturing
339950.rationale: 5910 firms, avg 12.2 — among most fragmented in 339. Active PE roll-up: Stratus (Cortec), Identiti (Trivest), FASTSIGNS franchisor model. Digital sign design + AI quoting/CAD automation lift margins. Sub-$1M EBITDA at 4-5x → platform 8-10x arbitrage.
sources: PitchBook PE deals in sign manufacturing 2022-2024

339991.title: Gasket, Packing, and Sealing Device Manufacturing
339991.rationale: Industrial B2B niche; 632 firms with Parker Hannifin/Freudenberg/Trelleborg consolidators. Mid-market PE active (Audax, Incline) in specialty seals serving aero/energy. Multiples 7-9x; AI in CAD/quote less transformative.

339992.title: Musical Instrument Manufacturing
339992.rationale: 615 firms — heritage brands (Fender, Gibson, Steinway, Yamaha-imports). Craft-driven, brand-loyal, post-COVID demand cooling per NAMM. PE largely absent; weak operating leverage.

339993.title: Fastener, Button, Needle, and Pin Manufacturing
339993.rationale: Only 94 US firms — small absolute pool limits roll-up scale. YKK + apparel-trim importers dominate buttons; industrial fasteners mixed with 332722. Niche PE interest only.

339994.title: Broom, Brush, and Mop Manufacturing
339994.rationale: 150 firms, larger avg (60 emp). Newell (Quickie), Libman, 3M anchor; private label for Walmart/Costco. Stable but no-growth; some PE add-ons (Wind Point/Libman). Low AI leverage in commodity production.

339995.title: Burial Casket Manufacturing
339995.rationale: Only 83 firms — Hillenbrand/Batesville + Matthews oligopoly ~70% share. Cremation rates >60% per NFDA shrinking TAM ~3%/yr. No PE thesis.

339999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Manufacturing
339999.rationale: Catch-all (3080 firms, avg 14 emp) — candles, lampshades, taxidermy, hair accessories etc. Heterogeneous prevents thematic roll-up; sub-segment plays only (e.g., candles by Newell/private).

423110.title: Automobile and Other Motor Vehicle Merchant Wholesalers
423110.rationale: OEM-controlled distribution with franchise/allocation constraints (Manheim/ADESA dominate auctions); low fragmentation (6K firms, ~$8M avg rev) and razor-thin margins (~3-4%) leave little arbitrage. AI limited to pricing/inventory.

423120.title: Motor Vehicle Supplies and New Parts Merchant Wholesalers
423120.rationale: 12K firms, highly fragmented behind giants (LKQ, GPC/NAPA, AutoZone WD) creates roll-up runway in specialty/regional jobbers; AI fitment lookup + DTC e-commerce lifts margins from 5% toward 7-8%. Multiples 6-8x for $20M+ EBITDA platforms.

423130.title: Tire and Tube Merchant Wholesalers
423130.rationale: Consolidation already underway (ATD, US Autoforce, K&M); 2.5K firms with regional pockets remain. Heavy working capital (tire inventory), thin margins (~4-5%), commodity exposure; AI mostly demand-forecasting.

423140.title: Motor Vehicle Parts (Used) Merchant Wholesalers
423140.rationale: Recyclers/dismantlers — 1.7K firms, mostly sub-$5M (high fragmentation 7/10). AI computer vision for parts cataloging + dynamic pricing is real edge (Pull-A-Part, LKQ Keystone). Margins 7-10%; multiples 5-7x platform.

423210.title: Furniture Merchant Wholesalers
423210.rationale: 5K firms, cyclical (housing-correlated), low margins (4-6%); heavy showroom/sample inventory. AI helps with visual catalog/AR but doesn't fix structural margin compression from DTC (Wayfair effect).

423220.title: Home Furnishing Merchant Wholesalers
423220.rationale: 6.7K firms, fragmented but margin-compressed by Amazon/Wayfair drop-ship. AI demand sensing + image-based merchandising helps; multiples soft (4-6x) absent niche/brand moat.

423310.title: Lumber, Plywood, Millwork, and Wood Panel Merchant Wholesalers
423310.rationale: 7K firms; active PE roll-up theme (US LBM, BFS/BMC, ABC Supply adjacencies). AI price-risk hedging + commodity inventory optimization meaningful. Multiples 7-10x at scale, 4-5x small — strong arbitrage spread.

423320.title: Brick, Stone, and Related Construction Material Merchant Wholesalers
423320.rationale: 3.7K firms avg ~10 employees — very fragmented; CRH/Oldcastle, SiteOne consolidating hardscape. Heavy/local distribution moats; AI delta modest (route, quoting). Multiples 6-8x for $5M+ EBITDA platforms.

423330.title: Roofing, Siding, and Insulation Material Merchant Wholesalers
423330.rationale: Hot PE thesis — ABC Supply, Beacon (BECN), SRS (sold to Home Depot for $18B at ~14x EBITDA 2024). 3.5K firms remain; AI for storm-demand forecasting + contractor CRM. Strongest multiple arbitrage in 423.

423390.title: Other Construction Material Merchant Wholesalers
423390.rationale: Catch-all (concrete accessories, drywall, specialty); fragmented (3.7K), margins 5-8%. PE active (GMS, Foundation Building Materials). AI delta moderate; multiples 6-8x at scale.

423410.title: Photographic Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423410.rationale: Secular decline (smartphone displacement); only 550 firms left. Margins thin, low buyer interest — multiples 3-5x. AI can't reverse demand collapse; not a roll-up candidate.

423420.title: Office Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423420.rationale: 8.5K firms incl. copier/MPS dealers — active PE roll-up (Visual Edge, Flex, UBEO). Service-attached margins (10-15% on MPS) lift blended EBITDA; AI managed print + cybersecurity bolt-ons. Multiples 5-7x at $5M+ EBITDA.

423430.title: Computer and Computer Peripheral Equipment and Software Merchant Wholesalers
423430.rationale: Distribution-tier (TD SYNNEX, Ingram Micro) dominates; downstream VAR/MSP layer fragmented (6.8K firms). PE consolidates MSPs (covered under 541512). Pure-wholesale margins thin (~3-5%); multiples 6-8x for solution-led.

423440.title: Other Commercial Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423440.rationale: Mixed bucket (store fixtures, weighing/measuring); 3.7K firms. Fragmented but niche; AI for spec config + e-commerce. Multiples 5-7x; modest roll-up potential.

423450.title: Medical, Dental, and Hospital Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423450.rationale: 10.6K firms; PE heavily active (Henry Schein, Patterson, McKesson Medical-Surgical, DSO supply chains). GPO/compliance moats. AI for clinical-protocol kits, par-level mgmt. Multiples 8-11x for specialty distributors.

423460.title: Ophthalmic Goods Merchant Wholesalers
423460.rationale: EssilorLuxottica + VSP dominate; only 1K firms. Strong margins (7-10%) but limited fragmentation. AI for rx-fit/AR try-on. Multiples 7-9x but few targets.

423490.title: Other Professional Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423490.rationale: Lab/scientific instruments + religious/funeral/etc. — 2.4K firms; Thermo/VWR/Fisher dominate scientific. Niche specialty pockets; AI catalog + technical-sales copilots. Multiples 6-8x.

423510.title: Metal Service Centers and Other Metal Merchant Wholesalers
423510.rationale: 8.6K firms; Reliance Steel (RS) & Ryerson lead consolidation. Heavy working capital + commodity exposure cap margins (5-8%). AI for steel-price hedging + cut-to-length scheduling. Multiples 6-8x.

423520.title: Coal and Other Mineral and Ore Merchant Wholesalers
423520.rationale: Only 303 firms; ESG-disfavored, secular decline (US coal -40% since 2015). Not a roll-up target. Multiples depressed 3-5x.

423610.title: Electrical Apparatus and Equipment, Wiring Supplies, and Related Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423610.rationale: 13.5K firms — most fragmented in 423; active PE/strategic (Wesco/Anixter, Sonepar, Rexel, Border States). Data-center + EV tailwinds. AI for take-off automation + project quoting. Multiples 8-11x platform.

423620.title: Household Appliances, Electric Housewares, and Consumer Electronics Merchant Wholesalers
423620.rationale: 2.5K firms; OEM-direct + big-box (Best Buy, HD/Lowes, Costco) bypass distributors. Margins crushed (2-4%); multiples 4-6x. Not a roll-up target.

423690.title: Other Electronic Parts and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423690.rationale: 10K firms; Arrow + Avnet dominate authorized; long-tail of independent/specialty (RF, fiber). AI for BOM-sourcing + obsolescence mgmt valuable. Multiples 7-9x.

423710.title: Hardware Merchant Wholesalers
423710.rationale: 5.9K firms; Orgill, Ace, True Value, Do it Best dominate co-op/distribution. Independent hardware/industrial fastener niches still fragmented. AI for SKU rationalization. Multiples 7-9x.

423720.title: Plumbing and Heating Equipment and Supplies (Hydronics) Merchant Wholesalers
423720.rationale: 6.7K firms; Ferguson (FERG, ~$30B rev), Reece, Hajoca leading; Morsco/Reece roll-ups ongoing. AI for contractor quoting + counter-sales copilot. Multiples 9-12x for $10M+ EBITDA platforms — strong arbitrage.

423730.title: Warm Air Heating and Air-Conditioning Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423730.rationale: Watsco (WSO) blueprint — $7B rev, 13x+ EBITDA. 6.4K firms remain; OEM-territory constraints limit pure-play but specialty/parts roll-ups viable. IRA heat-pump tailwind. AI for contractor mobile ordering + load calc.

423740.title: Refrigeration Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423740.rationale: Small universe (1.2K firms) but very fragmented; commercial refrigeration tied to grocery/food-service capex + EPA refrigerant transitions (A2L) create disruption window. Multiples 7-9x; AI for refrigerant-tracking compliance.

423810.title: Construction and Mining (except Oil Well) Machinery and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423810.rationale: 4.9K firms; Caterpillar/Komatsu/Deere franchise structure limits roll-up to rental/parts/used. Strong margins from rental attach (12-18%). United Rentals/H&E playbook. Multiples 7-10x for rental-heavy.

423820.title: Farm and Garden Machinery and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423820.rationale: 7.5K firms; Deere/CNH/AGCO/Kubota dealer networks consolidating (Titan Machinery, RDO Equipment). AI for precision-ag retrofit + service scheduling. Multiples 6-9x; cyclical with ag commodity cycle.

423830.title: Industrial Machinery and Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
423830.rationale: 26.7K firms — largest, most fragmented bucket in 423. Active PE thesis (Applied Industrial, Motion/GPC, DXP). AI for technical-spec lookup + quote automation (Tavant/Tacton). Multiples 8-11x platform — strong arbitrage.

423840.title: Industrial Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423840.rationale: MRO/safety/fastener; 9K firms; Grainger/Fastenal/MSC dominate but long tail. AI for VMI (vending), e-commerce search, vendor consolidation strong. Multiples 8-11x at $10M+ EBITDA. Hot PE thesis.

423850.title: Service Establishment Equipment and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423850.rationale: Barber/salon, janitorial-supply, hotel/motel equipment; 4.5K firms small (~11 emp). Fragmented but slow consolidation; Edward Don, Imperial Dade adjacencies. Multiples 5-7x.

423860.title: Transportation Equipment and Supplies (except Motor Vehicle) Merchant Wholesalers
423860.rationale: Aircraft, marine, rail parts; 2.4K firms. Aerospace aftermarket margins strong (10-15%); HEICO/TransDigm acquire upstream. AI for parts-traceability (AS9100/FAA). Multiples 8-12x aerospace; 5-7x marine.

423910.title: Sporting and Recreational Goods and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423910.rationale: 5.4K firms; brand-DTC pressure (Nike, On, Lulu bypass distributors); margins compressed (4-6%). AI for demand sensing limited upside. Multiples 5-7x. Not a top-tier roll-up.

423920.title: Toy and Hobby Goods and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
423920.rationale: 2K firms; seasonal, hit-driven, Amazon-dominated retail; margins thin (3-6%). AI for demand forecasting only. Multiples 4-6x. Weak roll-up case.

423930.title: Recyclable Material Merchant Wholesalers
423930.rationale: Scrap metal/paper/plastic; 7.8K firms; ESG-tailwind + EV battery recycling. AMP Robotics computer-vision sorting real productivity boost. Radius Recycling/Sims/Nucor consolidating metal; long tail in plastics/paper. Multiples 6-9x; cyclical commodity exposure.

423940.title: Jewelry, Watch, Precious Stone, and Precious Metal Merchant Wholesalers
423940.rationale: 7K firms avg 5.5 employees — extremely fragmented (8/10) but trust/relationship-based; lab-grown disruption + DTC squeeze margins. AI for stone-grading (GIA-adjacent) limited. Multiples 3-5x; tough roll-up.

423990.title: Other Miscellaneous Durable Goods Merchant Wholesalers
423990.rationale: Catch-all (musical instruments, pets, monuments, etc.); 9.9K firms avg 8.9 emp — very fragmented but no thematic coherence. Multiples vary 4-7x by sub-niche; AI delta limited without focus.

424110.title: Printing and Writing Paper Merchant Wholesalers
424110.rationale: Printing/writing paper wholesale is a structurally declining category (-4 to -6% CAGR per NAW/Census shipments) consolidated around Veritiv, Lindenmeyr Munroe; AI helps in dynamic pricing and inventory but cannot reverse secular demand loss.

424120.title: Stationery and Office Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
424120.rationale: Office supplies wholesale fragmented but compressed by Amazon Business and Staples B2B; AI improves SKU rationalization and demand forecast, yet structural disintermediation caps multiple arbitrage at ~4-5x EBITDA.

424130.title: Industrial and Personal Service Paper Merchant Wholesalers
424130.rationale: Industrial/janitorial paper (jan-san) is a proven roll-up vertical with Imperial Dade and BradyIFS active consolidators paying 7-9x EBITDA; AI uplift in route density, dynamic pricing, and consumption-based replenishment.

424210.title: Drugs and Druggists' Sundries Merchant Wholesalers
424210.rationale: Drug wholesale is an oligopoly (McKesson, Cencora, Cardinal hold ~92% per HDA Factbook) with razor-thin 1-2% margins; specialty/repack niche exists but DEA/HIPAA compliance and primary-vendor contracts make sub-scale roll-ups unviable.

424310.title: Piece Goods, Notions, and Other Dry Goods Merchant Wholesalers
424310.rationale: Piece goods/notions fragmented (~2,900 firms, 8 emp avg) and serves declining domestic apparel manufacturing; AI inventory tools help but secular demand contraction and import substitution cap rollup math.

424340.title: Footwear Merchant Wholesalers
424340.rationale: Footwear wholesale increasingly disintermediated by brand-direct (Nike DTC, Skechers, Crocs) and Amazon; AI useful for size-curve forecasting but customer concentration risk and brand-vendor leverage limit consolidation premium.

424350.title: Clothing and Clothing Accessories Merchant Wholesalers
424350.rationale: Apparel wholesale faces structural decline from DTC and fast-fashion vertical integration (Shein, Zara); AI demand forecasting helps but brand consolidation upstream and retailer power downstream squeeze margins.

424410.title: General Line Grocery Merchant Wholesalers
424410.rationale: General-line grocery wholesale dominated by C&S, UNFI, KeHE, Associated Wholesale Grocers; ~50 emp avg and concentrated, leaving little fragmentation. Margins 1-3% (UNFI 10-K) and primary-supplier contracts limit roll-up opportunity.

424420.title: Packaged Frozen Food Merchant Wholesalers
424420.rationale: Frozen food wholesale (Reinhart, Dot Foods etc) has cold-chain moat and decent margins; AI route optimization and temperature/spoilage monitoring deliver 1-3pp uplift; PE active (Performance Food Group, US Foods tuck-ins) at 7-9x.

424430.title: Dairy Product (except Dried or Canned) Merchant Wholesalers
424430.rationale: Dairy wholesale tied to commodity pricing volatility (USDA AMS); cold-chain capex barrier limits new entrants but margins thin and major players (Saputo, DFA distribution arms) dominate; AI for spoilage and pricing helps modestly.

424440.title: Poultry and Poultry Product Merchant Wholesalers
424440.rationale: Poultry wholesale (~436 firms) concentrated near Tyson/Pilgrim's vertical channels; small independent distributors face vendor squeeze; AI yield/forecasting useful but margin headroom limited.

424450.title: Confectionery Merchant Wholesalers
424450.rationale: Confectionery wholesale (candy, snacks) higher-margin than food avg with c-store/vending channels; PE-backed rollups like Eby-Brown, McLane, Core-Mark patterns; AI demand forecasting strong fit for impulse-SKU mix.
sources: NCA State of the Treats Industry 2024

424460.title: Fish and Seafood Merchant Wholesalers
424460.rationale: Fish/seafood wholesale highly fragmented (1,964 firms, 12 emp avg) with quality-grading and spoilage challenges where AI vision/IoT add meaningful value; recent PE interest (e.g., Sea Port, North Coast) at 6-8x.

424470.title: Meat and Meat Product Merchant Wholesalers
424470.rationale: Meat wholesale moderately fragmented; cold-chain and HACCP barriers; AI yield optimization and demand forecasting on perishables can add 1-3pp; Sysco/PFG tuck-ins establish 6-8x comps.

424480.title: Fresh Fruit and Vegetable Merchant Wholesalers
424480.rationale: Fresh produce wholesale most fragmented food vertical (4,923 firms); high spoilage (~12% per USDA) and dynamic pricing make AI/vision+forecasting genuinely impactful; PE rollups (Sundia, Sun Belle) active at 6-8x.

424490.title: Other Grocery and Related Products Merchant Wholesalers
424490.rationale: Other grocery (specialty, ethnic, foodservice) most populous code in 4244 (~14.8K firms); ethnic/specialty distributors trade 6-8x with strong PE interest (Greco, Chefs' Warehouse model); AI fit strong on long-tail SKUs.
sources: Specialty Food Association State of Industry 2024

424510.title: Grain and Field Bean Merchant Wholesalers
424510.rationale: Grain merchant wholesale dominated by ABCD majors (ADM, Bunge, Cargill, Dreyfus) with co-op networks (CHS); thin commodity margins ~1-2% and hedging-driven; AI helps in logistics/basis trading but consolidation already largely done.

424520.title: Livestock Merchant Wholesalers
424520.rationale: Livestock dealers/wholesalers serve auction markets; commodity-tied 1-2% margins; volatile cattle cycle and minimal AI digitization opportunity beyond pricing tools. Not a roll-up candidate.

424590.title: Other Farm Product Raw Material Merchant Wholesalers
424590.rationale: Other farm raw materials (cotton, leaf tobacco, wool, hides) tied to commodity cycles with thin margins; AI helps in basis pricing and logistics but limited consolidation thesis.

424610.title: Plastics Materials and Basic Forms and Shapes Merchant Wholesalers
424610.rationale: Plastic resins/materials wholesale moderately fragmented; Univar Solutions and Brenntag acquisitive but petrochemical price volatility and tariff exposure are risks; AI useful for inventory/blending logistics.

424690.title: Other Chemical and Allied Products Merchant Wholesalers
424690.rationale: Specialty/industrial chemicals distribution is a proven PE roll-up vertical (Univar, Brenntag, Azelis, IMCD trade 10-14x) with regulatory moat (DOT, OSHA, EPA); AI fit in formulation matching, route, and compliance documentation.

424710.title: Petroleum Bulk Stations and Terminals
424710.rationale: Petroleum bulk stations/terminals capital-intensive (tank farms, EPA compliance); jobber consolidation ongoing (Pilot, World Fuel, Mansfield); AI limited beyond fleet routing and pricing; EV transition is long-term overhang.

424720.title: Petroleum and Petroleum Products Merchant Wholesalers (except Bulk Stations and Terminals)
424720.rationale: Non-terminal petroleum wholesale (lubricants, fuel jobbers) similar dynamics to 424710; PE interest in lube distribution (RelaDyne, ABCO at 7-9x) but commodity-pass-through limits AI margin uplift.

424810.title: Beer and Ale Merchant Wholesalers
424810.rationale: Beer wholesale is a regulated three-tier oligopoly with state franchise laws (Reyes, Andrews, Hensley) and high barriers to consolidation; high margin (~6-8%) but ownership transfers severely restricted by state alcoholic beverage codes.

424820.title: Wine and Distilled Alcoholic Beverage Merchant Wholesalers
424820.rationale: Wine/spirits wholesale also three-tier with Southern Glazer's and RNDC dominating ~60% combined; franchise/state-license regime limits free M&A; margins healthy but consolidation gated by regulation.

424910.title: Farm Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
424910.rationale: Farm supply wholesale (ag inputs, fertilizer, seed) moderately fragmented (~9K firms) with cooperative competition (Land O'Lakes, Nutrien Ag Solutions); AI for agronomy/route helps; PE interest in independent ag retail.

424920.title: Book, Periodical, and Newspaper Merchant Wholesalers
424920.rationale: Book/periodical/newspaper wholesale is structurally declining (print circulation -7%/yr per Pew, magazine wholesalers TNG/Hudson News in distress); Baker & Taylor exited trade in 2019. Not a roll-up thesis.
sources: Pew Research State of the News Media

424930.title: Flower, Nursery Stock, and Florists' Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
424930.rationale: Florist/nursery wholesale highly fragmented (~3,900 firms) with high perishability where AI demand forecasting and dynamic pricing valuable; rollup precedent in Cherrylake/BrightFarms, but importer concentration (Colombia/Ecuador) creates supply risk.

424940.title: Tobacco Product and Electronic Cigarette Merchant Wholesalers
424940.rationale: Tobacco/vape wholesale consolidated around McLane, Core-Mark, Eby-Brown serving c-stores; FDA PMTA regulation and declining cigarette volumes are structural headwinds; AI value limited beyond route density.

424950.title: Paint, Varnish, and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
424950.rationale: Paint/coatings wholesale fragmented serving body shops, contractors; PPG/Sherwin/AkzoNobel have captive distribution but independent jobbers remain; PE rollup precedent (Single Source, Single Source Inc) at 6-8x; AI fit on color matching/inventory.

424990.title: Other Miscellaneous Nondurable Goods Merchant Wholesalers
424990.rationale: Catch-all 'other nondurable' wholesale extremely fragmented (10.6K firms, 7 emp avg) covering toys, pet supplies, art, etc.; heterogeneity makes single rollup thesis difficult but sub-vertical platforms (pet, beauty) viable; AI moderate fit.

425120.title: Wholesale Trade Agents and Brokers
425120.rationale: 35.8K firms, avg 6.8 employees — highly fragmented commission-based brokers across food, industrial, durable goods. Low capex, working-capital light. Roll-up thesis: regional broker consolidation with shared CRM/sourcing/AI matching engines. Margin compression risk from direct e-commerce disintermediation and manufacturer DTC. AI delta moderate: automated buyer-seller matching, demand forecasting, contract automation. Examples: McKesson-style consolidation in specialty chemicals/food brokerage. Mid M=4 due to disintermediation headwind.

441110.title: New Car Dealers
441110.rationale: Top 10 dealer groups (Lithia, AutoNation, Penske, Group 1, Sonic, Asbury) consolidated ~10% share via $300M+ acquisitions; NADA 2025 reports 16,800+ rooftops but state franchise laws (Texas, Florida) cap consolidation pace. Lithia revenue $36B (FY2024) shows scale economics, yet OEM allocation veto rights limit pure roll-up thesis.

441120.title: Used Car Dealers
441120.rationale: Highly fragmented (25,607 firms, avg 7.3 employees) with CarMax (~3% share) and Carvana dominating online; independent BHPH dealers concentrated regionally. Carvana's post-2023 turnaround validates inventory/pricing AI moat, but franchise-free environment makes operational roll-up feasible for sub-$10M EBITDA targets.
sources: Cox Automotive Used Car Market Report 2024

441210.title: Recreational Vehicle Dealers
441210.rationale: Camping World (CWH) operates 200+ locations consolidating top tier, but 2,979 firms with avg 20 employees leave long tail of family dealers; RVIA reports post-COVID demand normalization with 2024 shipments ~330K (down from 2021 peak 600K). Service/parts attach offers margin uplift; floorplan financing risk material.

441222.title: Boat Dealers
