441222.rationale: MarineMax (HZO) and OneWater Marine (ONEW) lead consolidation with $2B+ revenue each across ~80 locations, but 4,320 firms with avg 8.7 employees indicate fragmentation. NMMA reports 2024 powerboat retail down ~9% YoY; service/marina attach drives unit economics, weather/seasonality constrains AI applicability.

441227.title: Motorcycle, ATV, and All Other Motor Vehicle Dealers
441227.rationale: RumbleOn (powersports roll-up) struggled with 50+ dealer integration post-2021, validating execution risk despite fragmentation; Harley-Davidson franchise network and Polaris/BRP allocations create OEM dependency. Industry retail down ~8% in 2024 per MIC; seasonal/discretionary demand reduces predictability.

441330.title: Automotive Parts and Accessories Retailers
441330.rationale: AutoZone (~$18B), O'Reilly (~$16B), Advance Auto, NAPA, Genuine Parts hold ~60%+ market share with 6,000+ stores each; DIFM commercial channel consolidated by O'Reilly/AutoZone. New entrant must compete on logistics density; only viable angle is regional commercial parts distribution (PartsTrader/RepairLink adjacent).

441340.title: Tire Dealers
441340.rationale: Discount Tire (~1,200 stores), Mavis Tire (Bay Grove PE-backed, 2,000+ stores post-NTB/Express acquisitions), Monro (MNRO ~1,300) demonstrate active PE consolidation; Modern Tire Dealer reports independents still hold ~50% of replacement market. Service labor + tire attach drives 8-12% EBITDA; AI helps demand forecasting and tech routing.

444110.title: Home Centers
444110.rationale: Home Depot ($152B FY24 rev) + Lowe's ($83B) duopoly controls ~80% of category; remaining independents subscale and unrollable. No PE roll-up thesis viable at category level.

444120.title: Paint and Wallpaper Retailers
444120.rationale: Sherwin-Williams (4,800+ stores) + PPG/Benjamin Moore dealer networks dominate pro segment; ~7,200 firms but mostly captive franchisees or single-store independents with limited platform potential.

444140.title: Hardware Retailers
444140.rationale: Ace Hardware co-op (~5,800 stores) + Do it Best + True Value cooperative model preempts PE roll-up; member-owners unlikely to sell to financial buyers. NRHA reports ~13,000 independent hardware stores remaining.
sources: NRHA 2024 Cost of Doing Business Study

444180.title: Other Building Material Dealers
444180.rationale: Most active PE roll-up vertical in 444: Builders FirstSource ($16.4B rev, BMC+BFS merger), US LBM (Bain Capital, ~450 locations), ABC Supply, Beacon Building Products demonstrate proven 5x->10x+ multiple arbitrage on $3-15M EBITDA tuck-ins; ~6,500+ independent LBM dealers per NLBMDA.

444230.title: Outdoor Power Equipment Retailers
444230.rationale: Dealer-OEM franchise lock (John Deere, Kubota, Husqvarna, Stihl) constrains M&A; service/parts revenue defensible but limited AI leverage. Some regional consolidation (RDO, United Ag & Turf) but OEM consent required.

444240.title: Nursery, Garden Center, and Farm Supply Retailers
444240.rationale: Highly fragmented (~15,000 garden centers + farm supply stores) but seasonal cash flows, perishable inventory, and Tractor Supply ($14.9B rev, 2,200+ stores) consolidation in farm supply leave residual independent segment with low multiples (3-5x) and weak AI applicability.

445110.title: Supermarkets and Other Grocery Retailers (except Convenience Retailers)
445110.rationale: Top 4 (Walmart, Kroger, Costco, Albertsons) hold ~50%+ US grocery share; FTC/DOJ blocked Kroger-Albertsons $24.6B merger Dec 2024 citing concentration. Razor-thin 1-3% net margins, unionized labor, real-estate heavy; AI helps demand forecasting/shrink but cannot offset scale economics.

445131.title: Convenience Retailers
445131.rationale: NACS reports ~152K US c-stores; top 10 chains (7-Eleven 13K, Couche-Tard/Circle K 7K, Casey's 2.6K) own ~35% but ~60% are single-store operators. Fuel margins volatile, inside-sales tobacco-dependent (~35% of revenue); roll-up plays active (Casey's $1.1B Fikes acq 2024) but cap rates compressed.

445132.title: Vending Machine Operators
445132.rationale: Highly fragmented ~5K operators per Census; Compass/Canteen and Aramark dominate B2B contract feeding but unattended-retail subsector remains mom-and-pop. Telemetry/cashless (Cantaloupe/USA Technologies) enables route optimization AI; declining traffic post-COVID office occupancy is headwind.

445230.title: Fruit and Vegetable Retailers
445230.rationale: 3,906 firms, avg 5 employees (CBP); ethnic produce markets and farm stands, no national chain. Perishable inventory + low ticket + heavy manual sorting; AI inventory tools exist but adoption near-zero. Specialty grocers compete with supermarket produce sections at scale disadvantage.

445240.title: Meat Retailers
445240.rationale: ~3K independent butcher shops per Census, mostly single-location craft/halal/kosher operators. Premium/grass-fed niche growing but supermarket meat departments + Costco/Sam's dominate volume. Skilled-labor (cutter) shortage acute; AI minimal applicability beyond POS/inventory.

445250.title: Fish and Seafood Retailers
445250.rationale: Tiny subsector ~1.5K firms (NMFS/Census); coastal fishmongers and ethnic markets. Cold-chain capex high, sourcing relationships critical; supermarkets/Whole Foods/Costco absorb most demand. AI useful for freshness/traceability blockchain pilots but ROI unproven.

445291.title: Baked Goods Retailers
445291.rationale: 2,807 firms avg 7.8 employees (CBP); independent bakeries + small chains (Crumbl 1K+ franchised). Labor-heavy (4am bakers), perishable, but premium pricing and brand-loyalty support 6-10% EBITDA. Crumbl IPO buzz validates roll-up thesis at chain level; single-store roll-up faces operational complexity.

445292.title: Confectionery and Nut Retailers
445292.rationale: 3,399 firms (CBP); chocolatiers, candy boutiques, nut shops — mall-based (See's, Godiva) or local artisan. Gift-occasion driven, seasonal (Valentine's, Easter, Christmas); e-commerce shift hurts mall traffic. Hershey/Mars dominate manufacturing not retail; specialty retail roll-ups limited by brand-led model.

445298.title: All Other Specialty Food Retailers
445298.rationale: Catch-all for spice/cheese/gourmet/ethnic specialty stores; highly fragmented ~5K firms. Trader Joe's, Whole Foods, Eataly absorb premium customer at scale; single-location specialty faces e-commerce (Thrive Market, iGourmet) competition. Brand-driven, low operational synergy for roll-up.
sources: Specialty Food Association State of Industry 2024

445320.title: Beer, Wine, and Liquor Retailers
445320.rationale: ~40K stores (Census/NABCA); Total Wine ~270 stores + BevMo + state-monopoly control states (17 states ABC). Three-tier distribution law limits cross-state roll-ups; license scarcity/value (liquor licenses $50K-$500K) is moat. Drizly/Instacart deliver squeeze margins; AI for assortment/age-verification niche.

449110.title: Furniture Retailers
449110.rationale: Highly fragmented but dominated by RH, Ashley, Williams-Sonoma, Wayfair; thin margins, high working capital, cyclical demand. AI helps merchandising/visualization but doesn't fix structural retail economics.

449121.title: Floor Covering Retailers
449121.rationale: Fragmented local installers, project-based revenue with measure/install service component creating defensibility. Empire Today/Floor & Decor show roll-up potential; AI helps quoting/scheduling.

449122.title: Window Treatment Retailers
449122.rationale: Sub-scale specialty with measure/install moat; Budget Blinds/3 Day Blinds franchise consolidation playbook. AI visualization (room preview) and lead routing meaningful uplift.

449129.title: All Other Home Furnishings Retailers
449129.rationale: Catch-all (lamps, kitchenware, decor) competing directly with Amazon/HomeGoods/Wayfair; no service moat, weak unit economics. Mattress Firm-style roll-ups exception but already consolidated.

449210.title: Electronics and Appliance Retailers
449210.rationale: Already consolidated under Best Buy + big-box; independents face Amazon price compression with 2-4% margins. Appliance install/service sub-niche only viable angle.

455110.title: Department Stores
455110.rationale: Secular decline (Macy's, Kohl's, JCPenney shrinking footprints per SEC 10-Ks); category share lost to off-price (TJX) and e-commerce. Few independents left; consolidation already happened decades ago. AI cannot fix structural format decline.

455211.title: Warehouse Clubs and Supercenters
455211.rationale: Effective oligopoly: Costco, Sam's Club (Walmart), BJ's. SEC filings show ~$900B combined revenue. Zero fragmentation; no roll-up surface. Capital intensity (200k+ sqft boxes, membership infra) precludes new entrants.

455219.title: All Other General Merchandise Retailers
455219.rationale: Dollar General (~20k stores), Dollar Tree/Family Dollar (~16k), Five Below dominate the value-merch tier per SEC 10-Ks. Long tail = variety/closeout independents with thin margins, no real estate leverage. Walmart pricing umbrella caps any roll-up upside.

456110.title: Pharmacies and Drug Retailers
456110.rationale: Top-3 (CVS/Walgreens/Rite Aid) hold ~60% script share; Rite Aid Ch.11 BK 2023+2024 signals structural distress. PBM reimbursement compression crushes independents; NACDS reports gross margins under pressure. Not viable for PE roll-up.

456120.title: Cosmetics, Beauty Supplies, and Perfume Retailers
456120.rationale: Ulta + Sephora (LVMH) dominate prestige; Amazon/DTC squeezes indies. Inventory-heavy, brand-license dependent. Limited roll-up math; AI helps personalization/inventory but doesn't fix scale disadvantage vs. category killers.

456130.title: Optical Goods Retailers
456130.rationale: EssilorLuxottica (LensCrafters/Pearle) + Warby Parker + Costco hold ~35%; ~30K indie ODs remain fragmented. Healthy 12-18% margins, recurring exam revenue, vertical lens-lab integration possible. PE precedent: MyEyeDr (Goldman/Altas), Eyemart Express. Best 456 candidate.

456191.title: Food (Health) Supplement Retailers
456191.rationale: GNC Ch.11 2020 (Harbin Pharma exit), Vitamin Shoppe taken private (Liberty Tax 2019). Amazon + DTC brands (Ritual, AG1) gut foot-traffic model. Highly fragmented but declining unit economics; AI cannot offset channel shift.

456199.title: All Other Health and Personal Care Retailers
456199.rationale: Catch-all bucket (hearing aids, medical supply, fragrance, sick-room). Heterogeneous SKUs and customer segments prevent thesis-level rollup; hearing-aid subsegment (Costco/Sonova) more attractive but not isolatable at 456199.

457110.title: Gasoline Stations with Convenience Stores
457110.rationale: NACS: ~120K c-stores, top 10 control ~35% (Couche-Tard, 7-Eleven, Casey's). Fuel margins thin (~2-3%); inside sales (~30%) drive EBITDA. Active consolidation but mega-PE/strategics dominate (Couche-Tard $20B 7-Eleven bid 2024). AI delta modest: pricing, loss prevention, foodservice mix. Mid-market tuck-ins viable but capex-heavy (UST, EMV, EV).

457120.title: Other Gasoline Stations
457120.rationale: Declining segment; EIA shows pure-fuel sites losing share to c-store format. Lower foot traffic, no inside margin cushion, EV exposure. Roll-up logic = convert to c-store (capex $1-2M/site) or sell to majors. PE rarely targets pure-play; usually absorbed into 457110 platforms.

457210.title: Fuel Dealers
457210.rationale: NEFI: ~5,500 heating oil dealers + propane (Suburban, AmeriGas consolidating). Highly fragmented Northeast (NY/NE/MA/CT), owner-operator, EBITDA 7-10%, sticky route density. AI delta strong: dynamic routing, degree-day forecasting, tank telemetry, churn prediction. Active PE roll-up thesis (Star Group, Global Partners, Sprague). Decarbonization risk = renewable diesel/bioheat hedge.

458110.title: Clothing and Clothing Accessories Retailers
458110.rationale: Massive fragmentation but secular decline: DTC shake-out, mall traffic collapse, TJX/Ross/Shein crushing mid-tier. Roll-up thesis weak—chain consolidators (Tailored Brands, Express, Rite Aid-adjacent) keep filing Ch.11. Lease liabilities + inventory risk. AI helps merch planning/personalization but cannot fix demand.

458210.title: Shoe Retailers
458210.rationale: Already consolidated: Designer Brands (DSW), Foot Locker, Caleres dominate. Indie cobblers/boutiques sub-scale, vendor-power weak vs Nike DTC pivot. Foot Locker -28% market cap 2024. Inventory + size SKU complexity. Limited AI lever beyond fit-tech.

458310.title: Jewelry Retailers
458310.rationale: Signet (~10% share) leaves long tail of ~18K indie jewelers, owner-aging. Better unit economics than apparel (gross margin 50%+). Lab-grown diamond shock hit comps 2024 but stabilizing. Roll-up precedent: Berkshire/Borsheims, REEDS. AI: appraisal, inventory tagging, CRM.

458320.title: Luggage and Leather Goods Retailers
458320.rationale: Tiny category (~1.5K firms), Samsonite/Tumi/brand-DTC + Amazon dominate retail. Indie luggage shops dying with mall traffic. Roll-up math: too small to justify platform overhead. Skip.

459110.title: Sporting Goods Retailers
459110.rationale: Dick's Sporting Goods (~$12.4B 2024 revenue, ~13% EBITDA) and Academy Sports dominate; ~20K establishments per Census 2022 with long tail of specialty (running, cycling, ski) but big-box capture 50%+ share. Specialty roll-up viable in niche verticals only.

459120.title: Hobby, Toy, and Game Retailers
459120.rationale: Hobby Lobby (private, ~$8B revenue), Michaels (Apollo-owned), and JoAnn (bankrupt 2024-25) consolidate craft; toy/game specialty fragmented (~15K firms) post-Toys-R-Us. Amazon/Target structural headwind; hobby gaming (Warhammer/board) is bright spot.

459130.title: Sewing, Needlework, and Piece Goods Retailers
459130.rationale: JoAnn (~850 stores) bankruptcy 2025 liquidated dominant chain; remaining ~5K independent quilt/fabric shops with declining demographics. Roll-up timing poor — category in secular decline per IBISWorld Fabric Stores 2024.

459140.title: Musical Instrument and Supplies Retailers
459140.rationale: Guitar Center (Ares-owned, ~290 stores) and Sweetwater dominate; ~3-4K independents in repair/lessons/band rental niche. NAMM 2024 industry revenue ~$8.6B, flat post-COVID surge. Service component (lessons, repair) somewhat AI-resistant.

459210.title: Book Retailers and News Dealers
459210.rationale: Barnes & Noble (Elliott-owned, ~600 stores) sole national chain; ABA reports ~2,500 independent bookstores (growing modestly post-2020). Amazon ~50%+ print share per AAP/StatShot 2024 makes roll-up economics weak despite indie revival.

459310.title: Florists
459310.rationale: Highly fragmented ~13K firms per Census 2022, mostly single-location; 1-800-Flowers (~$2B revenue) dominates wire/online but local delivery still local. SAF reports steady decline in shop count; AI order-routing and design-automation modest upside.

459410.title: Office Supplies and Stationery Retailers
459410.rationale: Staples (Sycamore, private) and Office Depot/ODP duopoly; remote-work post-COVID structurally compressed retail footprint. Independent stationery boutiques are niche/craft (~3K). Category structurally challenged by e-comm.

459420.title: Gift, Novelty, and Souvenir Retailers
459420.rationale: Highly fragmented (~20K establishments per Census 2022), heavily tourism/seasonal; no national chain dominates. AI product-curation and inventory could help but tourism-cyclical risk and low avg ticket cap multiples.

459510.title: Used Merchandise Retailers
459510.rationale: Resale category $43B 2024 per ThredUp; Winmark (Plato's Closet, Once Upon a Child) franchise model + Savers IPO 2023 validate roll-up. AI-driven authentication/pricing (RealReal model) adds real margin. Strong tailwind from sustainability/value trends.

459910.title: Pet and Pet Supplies Retailers
459910.rationale: PetSmart (BC Partners) + Petco (~$6B 2024 revenue) duopoly; Chewy (~$11B) takes e-comm. APPA 2024 industry ~$152B but retail share compressing. Independent pet boutique/grooming sub-segment fragmented but tier-2.

459920.title: Art Dealers
459920.rationale: Extremely fragmented (~6K firms) with Sotheby's/Christie's at top; relationship-driven, trust-based, illiquid inventory makes roll-up structurally hard. Art Basel/UBS 2024 report $65B global market down -4% YoY. Low scalability.

459930.title: Manufactured (Mobile) Home Dealers
459930.rationale: MHI 2024 reports ~103K units shipped; ~3K dealers fragmented behind Clayton Homes (Berkshire, vertically integrated). Affordable housing tailwind + financing complexity create roll-up window, but manufacturer-dealer dynamics constrain.

459991.title: Tobacco, Electronic Cigarette, and Other Smoking Supplies Retailers
459991.rationale: FDA PMTA enforcement and state flavor bans reshape category; ~15K vape/tobacco shops fragmented but regulatory risk (PACT Act, FDA premarket) deters institutional capital. Cigarette volumes -7%/yr per CDC. ESG/PE-unfriendly.

459999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Retailers
459999.rationale: Catch-all bucket spanning monument dealers, religious goods, swimming pools, pet caskets — too heterogeneous for thesis-level scoring. Mid-range defaults reflect mix; sub-segment selection required for any roll-up play.

481111.title: Scheduled Passenger Air Transportation
481111.rationale: Pure oligopoly: Delta, United, American, Southwest control >70% domestic ASMs per DOT BTS T-100; Alaska/JetBlue/Spirit/Frontier round out. Capital-intensive ($50M+/aircraft), unionized labor (ALPA/AFA), slot/gate constraints at hub airports — no roll-up thesis. Big 4 already deploy AI for revenue management (PROS), crew optimization, predictive maintenance (GE Aviation Predix). Public multiples 4-6x EV/EBITDA, no arbitrage.

481112.title: Scheduled Freight Air Transportation
481112.rationale: Dominated by FedEx Express, UPS Airlines, Atlas Air (Amazon Air contracts), Kalitta — top 5 carry >85% of US scheduled freight tonnage (BTS Form 41). 385 CBP firms misleading: most are tiny regional feeders/wet-lease ops. Integrators already AI-mature (route optimization, sort automation). No fragmentation for roll-up; certificated 121 ops carry $30M+ regulatory burden.

481211.title: Nonscheduled Chartered Passenger Air Transportation
481211.rationale: ACTIVE PE thesis: 1670 Part 135 charter operators, avg 22 employees — highly fragmented. Wheels Up (acquired Delta Private Jets, Gama, Air Partner), Vista Global (acquired XOJet, JetSmarter, Air Hamburg, Jet Edge — >360 aircraft), NetJets/Berkshire, Flexjet/Directional consolidating. Sub-$20M operators trade 4-6x EBITDA, platforms exit at 10-14x. AI uplift in dynamic pricing, empty-leg matching (FlyXO, Jettly), crew/aircraft routing — adoption still <20% of small operators.

481212.title: Nonscheduled Chartered Freight Air Transportation
481212.rationale: Mid-fragmented: 215 firms but ACMI/wet-lease dominated by Atlas, Kalitta, ATSG/Cargojet at top; long tail of ad-hoc charter for auto-parts, oil/gas, humanitarian. PE active in ACMI (Apollo/Atlas Air 2023 take-private $5.2B), but smaller charter freight has thinner margins and high fuel exposure. AI helps load matching (Cargo.one, WebCargo) — adoption rising post-2021 cargo boom but cyclical.

481219.title: Other Nonscheduled Air Transportation
481219.rationale: Most fragmented in 481: 588 firms avg 11 employees — aerial survey/mapping, photo, sightseeing, skydiving, banner-tow, agricultural relay. Highly local micro-operators (single-pilot, 1-3 aircraft). PE interest in aerial data/survey roll-ups (Quantum Spatial/NV5 Geospatial $400M acquisition) where AI imagery analysis (computer vision) creates durable differentiation. Tourism/sightseeing harder to consolidate (location-bound, FAA Part 136). Multiple arbitrage 5x→9x possible in survey vertical.

482111.title: Line-Haul Railroads
482111.rationale: Class I oligopoly: UNP, NSC, CSX, BNSF, CNI, CP control >90% of US rail ton-miles (AAR). Public mega-caps, $15-25B revenue each, 40-45% operating ratios. Zero roll-up viability — STB approval required for any merger (CP-KCS took 2 years). AI gains marginal vs existing PTC/precision scheduled railroading already deployed.

482112.title: Short Line Railroads
482112.rationale: ~600 Class II/III short lines in US (ASLRRA), highly fragmented at long tail but already heavily consolidated by Genesee & Wyoming (~115 lines), Watco, OmniTRAX, Patriot Rail. Remaining independents are small ($2-15M rev), but capex-heavy (track, locomotives), regulated (FRA/STB), and PE multiples 7-9x EBITDA. AI delta modest: dispatch optimization, predictive maintenance on locomotives.

483111.title: Deep Sea Freight Transportation
483111.rationale: Global oligopoly: Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, COSCO control >80% of container TEU capacity (Alphaliner 2025). US-flag deep-sea fleet (Jones Act constrained) is tiny ~80 ocean-going vessels; freight rates volatile post-Red Sea, capex $100M+ per vessel makes PE roll-up uneconomic.

483112.title: Deep Sea Passenger Transportation
483112.rationale: Cruise oligopoly: Carnival (CCL), Royal Caribbean (RCL), Norwegian (NCLH) control ~75% of global berths (CLIA 2024). Capex per ship $1-2B, 5-year shipyard backlogs (Fincantieri/Meyer Werft); no fragmented mid-market to roll up beyond luxury/expedition niches already acquired.
sources: CLIA State of the Cruise Industry 2024

483113.title: Coastal and Great Lakes Freight Transportation
483113.rationale: Jones Act coastwise trade dominated by Matson (Pacific), Crowley, TOTE, Seacor; Great Lakes bulk carriers consolidated under Interlake, Grand River, Canada Steamship Lines. ~495 firms but top 10 hold majority of self-propelled tonnage; capex-heavy ($50-150M per vessel) and crew shortages limit AI uplift.

483114.title: Coastal and Great Lakes Passenger Transportation
483114.rationale: Highly fragmented: 190 firms avg 15 employees (CBP 2022), mostly regional ferry/whale-watch/dinner-cruise operators (Hornblower, City Experiences acquired many but long tail remains). USCG Subchapter T/K licensing creates moat; AI uplift modest (yield management, scheduling) but vessel capex and seasonal demand cap multiples at 4-6x EBITDA.
sources: USCG Marine Information for Safety and Law Enforcement (MISLE)

483211.title: Inland Water Freight Transportation
483211.rationale: Heavily consolidated: Kirby Corp (KEX) is largest US inland tank barge operator (~30% market share liquid), Ingram Marine, ACBL (American Commercial Barge Line), Marquette dominate dry cargo on Mississippi/Ohio river system (AWO 2024). ~382 firms but top 5 control most ton-miles; Kirby's recent tuck-ins at 5-7x EBITDA leave thin pickings.

483212.title: Inland Water Passenger Transportation
483212.rationale: Most fragmented water-trans sub-sector: 303 firms avg 10.6 employees (CBP), local river ferries, sightseeing, dinner cruises, riverboat gambling tenders. USCG vessel limits and seasonal/tourist dependence cap scale; only American Cruise Lines and Viking dominate river overnight cruise niche. Roll-up math weak due to small ticket sizes and vessel maintenance capex.

484110.title: General Freight Trucking, Local
484110.rationale: 45K firms avg 7 employees - highly fragmented but driver-labor dominated (~35% of cost). AI helps dispatch/routing but cannot replace local drayage drivers; thin margins limit multiple arbitrage above 4-5x EBITDA.

484121.title: General Freight Trucking, Long-Distance, Truckload
484121.rationale: Hyper-fragmented (~700K owner-operators per FMCSA; 63K firms in CBP), but commodity pricing keeps operating ratios at 94-97% (Werner/Schneider/Knight-Swift 10-Ks 2024). Roll-up math weak: TL acquisitions typically trade 4-5x EBITDA with minimal synergies.

484122.title: General Freight Trucking, Long-Distance, Less Than Truckload
484122.rationale: Already consolidating - top 10 LTL carriers (ODFL, Saia, XPO, Estes, Old Dominion, TFI/TForce, ArcBest, Knight-Swift LTL, Saia, R+L) hold >75% share post-Yellow bankruptcy 2023. ODFL trades 20x+ EBITDA, Saia 15x. Network density barriers high; terminal-based model favors incumbents. Not a fragmented roll-up play anymore.

484210.title: Used Household and Office Goods Moving
484210.rationale: 9.8K firms avg 11 employees, seasonal/labor-heavy (van lines like Mayflower/Allied/United already aggregated as agent networks). Local mom-and-pop movers fragmented but labor-intensive and reputation-dependent; AI limited to booking/CRM/quoting (Updater, Moved).

484220.title: Specialized Freight (except Used Goods) Trucking, Local
484220.rationale: 33K firms - includes dump truck, refrigerated local, bulk liquids, auto haul. Niche specialization (HazMat endorsements, tankers, ready-mix) creates 11-14% margins vs general freight. PE active in waste/construction hauling sub-niches (Quality Distribution, Heniff acquisitions); 6-8x EBITDA achievable.

484230.title: Specialized Freight (except Used Goods) Trucking, Long-Distance
484230.rationale: 12K firms avg 14.5 employees - flatbed, tanker, HazMat, oversize/heavy haul, auto transport. Highest PE interest in 484 series: Heniff/Quality Carriers (bulk chem), Daseke (flatbed roll-up, acquired by TFI 2024 at 7-8x), Miller Industries. Specialization creates pricing power and 12-15% margins; certifications create barriers.

485111.title: Mixed Mode Transit Systems
485111.rationale: Public-sector transit authorities (MTA, BART, WMATA) operating mixed bus+rail; subsidy-dependent, not investable for PE roll-up. 38 firms = mostly government entities.

485112.title: Commuter Rail Systems
485112.rationale: Public commuter rail (LIRR, Metra, Caltrain, NJ Transit); 17 entities, all government/quasi-public, ~$1.4B avg operating loss covered by subsidies. Not a PE rollup target.

485113.title: Bus and Other Motor Vehicle Transit Systems
485113.rationale: Municipal/regional bus transit (MTA Bus, LA Metro, SEPTA, contract operators like Transdev/MV/RATP Dev); farebox recovery ~25-35%, subsidy-dependent. Contract operators consolidated.

485119.title: Other Urban Transit Systems
485119.rationale: Ferries, monorails, aerial tramways, cable cars — mostly municipal/PPP (NYC Ferry, SF cable, Roosevelt Island Tram). 60 firms; capex-heavy, public funded.

485210.title: Interurban and Rural Bus Transportation
485210.rationale: Intercity bus (Greyhound/FlixBus acquired 2021, Megabus, Peter Pan, Trailways) — FlixMobility consolidating; rural lines subsidy-dependent (FTA 5311). Thin margins, fuel/labor exposed.

485310.title: Taxi and Ridesharing Services
485310.rationale: Uber (~76% US ride-hail share Q1 2025) + Lyft displaced legacy taxi (~80% volume decline since 2014 in NYC/SF/Chicago); medallion values collapsed ($1.3M to ~$80k NYC). Sub-scale local fleets unattractive.

485320.title: Limousine Service
485320.rationale: Black car/livery — 4,225 firms avg 6.1 employees; corporate travel rebounded but Uber Black/Lux ate retail. Empire CLS, Carey, Addison Lee consolidators exist; multiples 4-6x EBITDA on sub-$2M EBITDA tuck-ins.

485410.title: School and Employee Bus Transportation
485410.rationale: Top 3 (First Student, National Express/Durham, Student Transportation Inc.) hold ~35-40% of contracts; remaining ~60% fragmented across 4,000 operators. Driver shortage severe, multiples 6-8x for $2-10M EBITDA platforms.

485510.title: Charter Bus Industry
485510.rationale: 1,129 operators avg 21.7 employees; tours/groups/sports. Post-COVID rebound but cyclical, fuel/driver exposed. Coach USA Ch11 June 2024 then sold piecemeal. Routing/yield AI helps but no defensible roll-up exit at scale.

485991.title: Special Needs Transportation
485991.rationale: Modivcare filed Ch11 Aug 2025 (largest NEMT broker, ~$2B rev); MTM and Access2Care squeezed by Medicaid capitation. Provider-level (3,825 small fleets) is contract labor for brokers — margin compression structural, exit multiples impaired.

485999.title: All Other Transit and Ground Passenger Transportation
485999.rationale: Catch-all: airport shuttles, hotel shuttles, employee vanpool, funeral/wedding cars. 2,171 micro-operators avg 10.6 employees. No clear consolidation playbook, fragmented end markets.

486110.title: Pipeline Transportation of Crude Oil
486110.rationale: Oligopoly: Enterprise, Energy Transfer, Plains, MPLX control ~70% interstate crude miles. FERC tariffs cap pricing. CapEx-heavy, ROW barriers. CBP 649 firms misleading—mostly LLC SPVs of majors. No PE roll-up entry.

486210.title: Pipeline Transportation of Natural Gas
486210.rationale: Kinder Morgan, Williams, Energy Transfer, ONEOK dominate interstate. FERC-regulated returns. Intrastate fragmented but capital-intensive, certificate barriers. AI marginal (leak detection, compressor optimization). Not a PE roll-up.

486910.title: Pipeline Transportation of Refined Petroleum Products
486910.rationale: Colonial, MPLX, Energy Transfer, Buckeye consolidated. FERC index tariffs. Few independent operators of scale. PE flirted (Buckeye take-private by IFM 2019) but no roll-up logic remains.

486990.title: All Other Pipeline Transportation
486990.rationale: Tiny niche (62 firms, 262 employees): slurry, CO2, hydrogen, brine pipelines. Idiosyncratic assets, not scalable platform. CO2/H2 may grow with decarbonization but currently project-finance, not roll-up.

487110.title: Scenic and Sightseeing Transportation, Land
487110.rationale: Tour buses, trolleys, horse-drawn carriages. 709 firms, ~12 employees avg. Highly seasonal, location-locked, weather-dependent. No platform economics across geographies. AI helps dynamic pricing/booking but capex (vehicles) and labor (CDL drivers, guides) dominate. Local PE plays exist (city-by-city) but no national thesis.

487210.title: Scenic and Sightseeing Transportation, Water
487210.rationale: Harbor cruises, whale-watching, dinner boats, airboats. 2243 firms, 5.7 employees avg — extreme fragmentation. USCG-regulated vessels, dock leases, seasonal (4-6 month windows in north). Marquee operators (Hornblower/City Experiences) consolidated NYC/SF but rest is mom-and-pop. AI marginal — booking/yield mgmt only.

487990.title: Scenic and Sightseeing Transportation, Other
487990.rationale: Helicopter tours, hot-air balloons, aerial trams, ziplines, dog sleds. 259 firms, ~11 employees avg. FAA Part 135 for air ops = high insurance, pilot scarcity, NTSB scrutiny (Grand Canyon/NYC crashes). Destination-bound (national parks, Vegas, Hawaii). AI negligible vs safety/regulatory costs.

488111.title: Air Traffic Control
488111.rationale: Dominated by FAA federal employees and Serco/Saab contracts; only ~221 firms with regulated contract towers (FCT program). Not a roll-up target due to federal monopoly and security clearance barriers.

488119.title: Other Airport Operations
488119.rationale: Mix of municipal/private FBOs (Signature, Atlantic, Modern Aviation rolled up 2019-2024 at 10-14x EBITDA). FBO consolidation largely complete; non-FBO airport ops fragmented but capital-intensive. Limited AI displacement (physical ground services).

488190.title: Other Support Activities for Air Transportation
488190.rationale: Includes aircraft maintenance, cleaning, refueling — 4,096 firms avg 22 employees. MRO consolidation active (StandardAero IPO 2024, VSE Aviation acquisitions). AI helps with predictive maintenance but physical labor dominates.

488210.title: Support Activities for Rail Transportation
488210.rationale: Captive Class I rail services (BNSF, UP, CSX, NS) dominate; independents serve short lines. AAR data shows ~1,761 firms but Genesee & Wyoming/Watco hold most independent capacity. Heavy union/regulatory barriers limit roll-up viability.

488310.title: Port and Harbor Operations
488310.rationale: Concentrated public port authorities + private terminal operators (SSA Marine, Ports America). High capex, ILWU/ILA union control, regulated tariffs. Not a fragmented roll-up; major M&A is infrastructure PE (Blackstone, IFM) at 15-20x.

488320.title: Marine Cargo Handling
488320.rationale: 475 firms avg 145 employees — large stevedoring operators (SSA, Ports America, ITS) dominate via terminal concessions. ILA/ILWU master contracts cap automation (2024 USMX strike). Limited fragmentation for roll-up.

488330.title: Navigational Services to Shipping
488330.rationale: Tug/towboat/pilot services — 1,044 firms avg 13 employees, highly fragmented harbor tug operators. Crowley/Foss/Moran consolidate large ports; secondary ports remain mom-and-pop. State pilot associations are protected monopolies.

488390.title: Other Support Activities for Water Transportation
488390.rationale: 752 firms avg 9.7 employees — highly fragmented marine surveyors, agency services, lighters. Very small avg size limits roll-up economics; mostly lifestyle businesses with thin EBITDA.

488410.title: Motor Vehicle Towing
488410.rationale: 10,554 firms avg 6.6 employees — textbook fragmentation. URGENT.LY, Agero, Allied Dispatch consolidate dispatch tech layer. PE interest growing (Copart owns Mather, but operators stay independent). AI for routing/dispatch optimization is emerging.

488490.title: Other Support Activities for Road Transportation
488490.rationale: Truck weighing, pilot car, vehicle inspection, toll road ops — 3,934 firms avg 13.7 employees. Mix of small services; pilot/escort services for oversized loads are highly fragmented and ripe for tech-enabled rollup.

488510.title: Freight Transportation Arrangement
488510.rationale: 22,383 firms (brokers/freight forwarders) under active AI displacement: Convoy bankruptcy 2023, Uber Freight/CHRW Navisphere/FreightWaves SONAR automating load matching. CHRW Q1 2024 cut 800 jobs citing AI. Roll-up window closing fast as multiples compress from 8x to 4-5x.

488991.title: Packing and Crating
488991.rationale: 1,599 firms avg 14.4 employees — fragmented specialty packing (industrial, museum, export crating). Craters & Freighters franchise and UPackit dominate but most are independent. Physical labor caps AI displacement.

488999.title: All Other Support Activities for Transportation
488999.rationale: 691 firms avg 5.8 employees — catchall for unclassified services. Too small and heterogeneous for thesis-driven roll-up; no industry association tracks it.

491110.title: Postal Service
491110.rationale: USPS is a US federal government entity (independent agency). Not investable — no private firms, no acquisition targets. CBP shows 0 private employment because USPS is excluded from CBP coverage. Statutory monopoly on letter mail under PRA 1970. NOT INVESTABLE.

492110.title: Couriers and Express Delivery Services
492110.rationale: Oligopoly: UPS/FedEx/USPS/Amazon own >85% volume (SEC 10-Ks). Capital-intensive (planes, hubs, last-mile fleets). No fragmentation arbitrage; AI routing already deployed at scale by incumbents.

492210.title: Local Messengers and Local Delivery
492210.rationale: Fragmented (5,366 firms, avg 28 emp) but margin-compressed by Uber/DoorDash/Roadie gig platforms. Low switching costs, commoditized labor. AI dispatch helps but platform incumbents dominate; roll-up faces customer churn to apps.

493110.title: General Warehousing and Storage
493110.rationale: Massive fragmented base (15K firms, avg 76 emp) but actively consolidating: GXO ($10B rev post-XPO spin), NFI, DHL Supply Chain rolling up 3PL/contract logistics. Capex-heavy (racking, MHE, leases) caps multiples 6-9x EBITDA. AI tailwinds: WMS optimization, robotics (Symbotic, AutoStore), labor scheduling lift margins 3-5pp. Tier-2 regional 3PLs ($5-50M rev) prime targets.

493120.title: Refrigerated Warehousing and Storage
493120.rationale: Hottest PE/REIT vertical in 493: Lineage Logistics IPO 2024 ($4.4B raise, $19B mkt cap), Americold (NYSE:COLD, $7B), United States Cold Storage (Swire). Top-5 hold ~45% share, still 1,222 firms with strong sub-scale tail. Multiples 14-20x EBITDA (REIT premium). Cold-chain pharma + grocery e-com drive demand. AI: energy mgmt, temp telemetry, slot optimization. High capex barrier but proven roll-up playbook.

493130.title: Farm Product Warehousing and Storage
493130.rationale: Small niche (774 firms, 7.9K total emp, avg 10 emp). Grain elevators, cotton/tobacco storage — dominated by ag co-ops (CHS, ADM, Bunge captive). Commodity cyclical, USDA-tied tariffs, seasonal utilization. Limited PE interest: thin EBITDA, geographic stranding, co-op buyer competition. AI delta minimal (yield/moisture sensors marginal). Skip for $50M fund.

493190.title: Other Warehousing and Storage
493190.rationale: Catch-all: bulk liquids/petroleum terminals, hazmat, document storage, lumber yards. 2,567 firms, avg 24 emp. Sub-segments diverge wildly: bulk terminals (Kinder Morgan, Magellan) capital-intensive REIT/MLP; document storage consolidated (Iron Mountain dominant); hazmat fragmented but heavy permitting moat. Selective roll-up potential in specialty 3PL niches; document storage already played out.

512110.title: Motion Picture and Video Production
512110.rationale: 17.7K firms but bimodal: ~6 majors (Disney, WBD, Universal, Sony, Paramount, Netflix) capture >70% revenue while long-tail indie/commercial/corporate video shops average <10 employees. Generative AI (Sora, Runway, Veo) directly threatens commercial/corporate/animation tiers; live-action features insulated short-term.

512120.title: Motion Picture and Video Distribution
512120.rationale: Only 480 firms and heavily consolidated under studio-owned distribution arms (Disney, Universal, WBD, Sony, Paramount, Lionsgate) plus streaming platforms. No fragmentation thesis; SVOD shift (Netflix, Disney+, Max) collapsed theatrical distribution P&L. Not a roll-up candidate.

512131.title: Motion Picture Theaters (except Drive-Ins)
512131.rationale: Top 3 (AMC, Regal/Cineworld, Cinemark) hold ~50% of US screens; IMAX/Marcus/Harkins fill next tier. Post-COVID box office still ~25% below 2019 ($8.7B vs $11.4B); structurally declining real estate-heavy model with negative FCF at AMC. Wrong industry for AI/roll-up.

512132.title: Drive-In Motion Picture Theaters
512132.rationale: Only 232 firms with 991 total employees; nostalgic micro-niche down from 4,000+ in 1958 to <300 screens nationally. Seasonal, weather-dependent, real-estate dependent. Too small and structurally declining for institutional roll-up.

512191.title: Teleproduction and Other Postproduction Services
512191.rationale: 3,079 firms averaging 9 employees: VFX, editing, color, sound mix, dubbing, captioning — exactly the tasks Runway/Topaz/ElevenLabs/Adobe Firefly automate fastest. Top consolidators (Technicolor bankruptcy 2024, Deluxe, Company 3, DNEG) created roll-up appetite; AI margin uplift real but value migrates upstream.

512199.title: Other Motion Picture and Video Industries
512199.rationale: 281 firms / 1,003 employees — residual bucket (film labs, stock footage libraries, ratings, booking agents). Too small in absolute revenue terms (<$200M industry) and heterogeneous to roll-up cleanly.

512230.title: Music Publishers
512230.rationale: Universal Music Publishing, Sony Music Publishing, Warner Chappell, Kobalt, BMG control >65% of global publishing; catalog M&A (Hipgnosis, Concord, Primary Wave) already at 18-22x NPS multiples. AI-generated music threatens new copyright creation but existing catalogs benefit from streaming royalty growth.

512240.title: Sound Recording Studios
512240.rationale: 2,117 firms averaging 3 employees — classic atomized cottage industry undermined by home studios (Logic, Pro Tools, Ableton) and now AI mastering (LANDR, iZotope), AI vocal cleanup, AI session musicians (Suno, Udio). EBITDA already thin; no multiple arbitrage available at this scale.

512250.title: Record Production and Distribution
512250.rationale: Universal, Sony Music, Warner Music control ~68% of US recorded music revenue ($17.1B 2024, RIAA). 880 firms but revenue concentration is extreme; remaining independents (Beggars, Concord, BMG) already consolidated. AI threatens artist development costs but majors capture the upside via licensing deals (Suno/Udio lawsuits 2024).

512290.title: Other Sound Recording Industries
512290.rationale: 575 firms / 2,344 employees — audiobooks, voiceover, foley, sound-for-picture. ElevenLabs/Resemble/Descript automating voiceover and audiobook narration at 90%+ cost reduction; SAG-AFTRA 2023 contracts contested AI voice cloning. Too small ($220M industry) for roll-up scale.

513110.title: Newspaper Publishers
513110.rationale: Gannett/News Corp filings show secular print decline (-7% to -10% YoY ad revenue); local newspapers consolidated by Alden/Gannett but EBITDA margins collapsed from 20%+ to single digits. AI can automate copy/aggregation but ad market structurally lost to Google/Meta — multiples 3-5x EBITDA, terminal-decline asset.
sources: Pew Research State of News Media 2024

513120.title: Periodical Publishers
513120.rationale: MPA data: B2B/trade titles holding 8-12% EBITDA while consumer print collapsing; ~6,500 firms (Census 2022) with long tail of niche enthusiast/trade titles. AI cuts editorial cost 30-40% but ad/subscription pressure caps multiples at 4-6x; niche subscription B2B pockets remain rollupable.

513130.title: Book Publishers
513130.rationale: AAP StatShot 2024: trade book sales flat ~$28B, top 5 (PRH, HC, S&S, Hachette, Macmillan) hold ~60% trade share; PRH operating margin ~13%. Roll-up opportunity in education/professional/indie literary mid-tier (5-7x EBITDA), but AI threatens content production and Amazon controls distribution.

513140.title: Directory and Mailing List Publishers
513140.rationale: Legacy Yellow Pages/print directories near-extinct; survivors are data brokers/mailing lists (ZoomInfo, Dun & Bradstreet adjacencies) facing direct GenAI substitution (LLMs + web scraping replace static lists). Census 2022 SUSB shows fragmented ~1,800 firms but multiples compressing 4-6x due to disruption risk.

513191.title: Greeting Card Publishers
513191.rationale: Hallmark + American Greetings = ~80% US share (oligopoly, not fragmented for rollup); category in 15-yr secular decline (-3 to -5% annual, GCA data) replaced by digital messaging. AI image gen disrupts further; small tail of niche/artist cards exists but EBITDA <10% and 3-4x multiples.

513199.title: All Other Publishers
513199.rationale: Catch-all bucket: maps, sheet music, art prints, calendars, databases — Census 2022 shows ~3,200 firms, highly fragmented but heterogeneous (no clean thesis). Pockets of specialty data/IP publishers exist (5-8x multiples) but most subscale; AI replaces low-value content, helps specialty IP curation.

513210.title: Software Publishers
513210.rationale: Microsoft/Adobe/Salesforce dominate enterprise (top 10 = ~45% revenue per IDC 2024); GenAI disrupts SMB SaaS (Copilot/Cursor cannibalizing point solutions) but vertical SaaS (sub-$10M ARR, 70k+ firms) remains rollupable at 4-8x ARR. High EBITDA margins (20-30%) but multiple compression as GenAI commoditizes generic CRUD apps.

516110.title: Radio Broadcasting Stations
516110.rationale: Secular decline (ad revenue -5% CAGR per BIA). iHeart, Audacy (Ch.11 2024), Cumulus, Beasley already rolled up top 200 markets. Small-market indies remain but FCC ownership caps + AM/FM listenership collapse to streaming. AI traffic/voice automation marginal.

516120.title: Television Broadcasting Stations
516120.rationale: Sinclair (185 stns), Nexstar (200+), Gray, Tegna own ~70% of US affiliates. FCC 39% national cap blocks further roll-up. Cord-cutting -7% CAGR, retrans fees plateau. ATSC 3.0 capex burden. AI news automation cost-save only.

516210.title: Media Streaming Distribution Services, Social Networks, and Other Media Networks and Content Providers
516210.rationale: Netflix, Spotify, YouTube, Meta dominate >85% share. Content economics negative for sub-scale players (CAC > LTV). Podcast roll-ups (Spotify-Gimlet, SiriusXM-Stitcher) already done. AI content gen commoditizes long-tail. NO-GO for $50M fund.

517111.title: Wired Telecommunications Carriers
517111.rationale: Consolidated oligopoly at top (AT&T, Verizon, Lumen, Frontier, Comcast/Charter cable) controls >85% of access lines per FCC Form 477; long tail of ~800 rural ILECs/CLECs is fragmented but capex-heavy (fiber overbuild $1,500-3,000/passing). PE activity exists (Searchlight/Consolidated, Macquarie/Cincinnati Bell) but deals are infrastructure-scale, not bolt-on roll-up; AI lifts limited to network ops/customer care.
sources: S&P Global Market Intelligence rural telco data

517112.title: Wireless Telecommunications Carriers (except Satellite)
517112.rationale: Three-carrier oligopoly (Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) controls ~98% of US wireless subscribers per CTIA 2024; remaining regional carriers (UScellular pending T-Mo sale, C-Spire, Cellcom) are sub-scale and capital-intensive (spectrum costs $0.50-2.00/MHz-POP). Not a PE roll-up vertical — spectrum FCC transfer approvals and $B+ capex make platform builds infeasible at fund scale.

517121.title: Telecommunications Resellers
517121.rationale: Highly fragmented CLEC/UCaaS reseller space (~2,000+ firms per FCC Form 499-A filings); active PE roll-up thesis with Cordia (Bertram Capital), Inteserra, Granite, BCM One, GTT all consolidating SMB voice/SIP/MPLS resellers at 5-7x EBITDA. AI lifts strong in billing automation, AI-agent customer support, and lead routing (15-25% opex reduction documented in S&P PE telecom deal commentary).
sources: S&P Global Market Intelligence CLEC tracker

517122.title: Agents for Wireless Telecommunications Services
517122.rationale: Master agents (Telarus, Avant, Intelisys/ScanSource, Bridgepointe) and sub-agent channels are highly fragmented — thousands of independent TSDs/TSBs per Channel Partners and AOTMP data; PE-backed roll-ups underway (Vista's bid for Bridgepointe, New Mountain/Telarus). AI strongly applicable to quote-to-cash, commission reconciliation, and AI-SDR prospecting; commission-only model is capital-light.

517410.title: Satellite Telecommunications
517410.rationale: Capacity layer is oligopoly — SpaceX/Starlink, Viasat (post-Inmarsat), EchoStar/Hughes, Intelsat/SES (merger pending), Iridium per FCC IBFS filings; ~460 CBP firms are mostly downstream VSAT integrators/MSS resellers, not satellite operators. Capex barriers ($300M-$1B per GEO satellite, $10B+ LEO constellations) preclude PE roll-up; small VSAT integrator tuck-ins possible but TAM thin.
sources: SIA State of Satellite Industry Report 2024

517810.title: All Other Telecommunications
517810.rationale: Catch-all bucket includes WISPs (~2,800 per WISPA), paging, telematics resellers, dark fiber operators — genuinely fragmented with active PE consolidation (e.g., Vistabeam, Conexon, Visionary Broadband; Cordillera Communications, Grain Management portfolio). AI lifts in NOC automation and predictive RF maintenance documented in WISPA 2024 operator survey; RDOF/BEAD subsidies de-risk rural fiber tuck-ins.

518210.title: Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services
518210.rationale: 17.5K firms but extreme oligopoly at top: AWS+Azure+GCP control ~65% of cloud infra revenue. Long tail of regional MSPs, colocation, specialty hosters, managed services exists but PE roll-ups face capex intensity (data center buildout $10M+/MW), hyperscaler pricing pressure, and customer churn to public cloud. Successful sub-thesis: regulated/sovereign hosting, vertical SaaS hosters, AI-inference colocation. Margins polarized: hyperscalers 30%+, long tail 8-15%. AI delta high in cost optimization but oligopoly captures most upside. M=3: niche only.

519210.title: Libraries and Archives
519210.rationale: Nonprofit/public sector (ALA: 99% libraries are public/academic, 9,000+ public systems). No PE roll-up path — not for-profit ownership. Some niche private archive services exist but fragmented and labor-intensive. AI uplift minimal (digitization, metadata) but no economic accrual to private investors.

519290.title: Web Search Portals and All Other Information Services
519290.rationale: Hyper-concentrated duopoly: Google ~90% search share (Alphabet $307B rev 2023), Microsoft Bing ~3%, Meta dominant in social info. Cloudflare, DuckDuckGo are niche. Long tail = small content aggregators/data brokers with low margins, structurally disrupted by LLM/generative AI. No roll-up logic — winners take all. AI is destructive force, not platform thesis.

521110.title: Monetary Authorities-Central Bank
521110.rationale: Federal Reserve System — quasi-governmental. 67 'firms' = 12 regional Federal Reserve Banks + branches + Board of Governors. NOT INVESTABLE. Established by Federal Reserve Act 1913. No private acquisition possible. Excluded from PE universe entirely.

522110.title: Commercial Banking
522110.rationale: FDIC reports ~4,400 commercial banks (2024), down from 14,400 in 1984 — organic consolidation by regulators (~3-4%/year). Top 4 banks (JPM, BofA, Wells, Citi) hold >50% of assets. PE roll-up blocked by bank holding company licensing, Fed/OCC approval, CRA. AI adoption high (JPM $17B tech budget, COiN, IndexGPT).

522130.title: Credit Unions
522130.rationale: NCUA reports ~4,600 credit unions (2024), down from ~10,000 in 2000 via merger activity (~150-200/yr). Non-profit member-owned structure prohibits PE ownership entirely — only CU-to-CU mergers allowed. AI adoption lagging vs banks (smaller IT budgets, core processor lock-in: Symitar, Corelation).

522180.title: Savings Institutions and Other Depository Credit Intermediation
522180.rationale: FDIC reports ~570 savings institutions (2024), down from ~3,600 in 1990 (S&L crisis + organic). Sector shrinking via charter conversions to commercial bank or absorption. OTS abolished 2011, now OCC-regulated. Same regulatory roll-up barrier as commercial banks.

522210.title: Credit Card Issuing
522210.rationale: Highly concentrated: top 10 issuers (Chase, AmEx, Citi, Capital One, BofA, Discover, US Bank, Wells, Synchrony, Barclays) hold >85% receivables (CFPB 2023). Capital One-Discover merger closed 2025. AI for underwriting/fraud mature (FICO + ML). No PE roll-up opportunity at scale.

522220.title: Sales Financing
522220.rationale: Captive finance arms (Ford Motor Credit, GM Financial, Toyota Financial) dominate auto; independent equipment/medical/RV finance companies fragmented (~3,600 firms, avg 26 emp). PE active in specialty: Stone Point, Centerbridge consolidating subprime auto/equipment finance. Multiples 6-9x EBITDA.

522291.title: Consumer Lending
522291.rationale: 13,845 firms avg 6 emp — highly fragmented installment/payday/title lenders. Fintech disruption massive (Affirm, Klarna, Upstart, SoFi). State licensing complex (50-state patchwork), CFPB scrutiny. PE active: BlueMountain, Värde rolling up subprime installment. AI underwriting replaces traditional credit officers.

522292.title: Real Estate Credit
522292.rationale: 18,589 firms — IMBs (independent mortgage banks) dominate origination post-2008 (Rocket, UWM, loanDepot). Cyclical, refi-dependent margins crushed 2023-24 (MBA Origination Income Survey: $1,295 loss/loan 2023). AI loan processing (Tavant, Blend, Maxwell) replaces 30-40% of underwriter/processor FTEs. Consolidation active.

522299.title: International, Secondary Market, and All Other Nondepository Credit Intermediation
522299.rationale: Niche: GSEs (Fannie/Freddie) + private secondary market players (PennyMac, Mr. Cooper, Newrez). Highly capital-intensive, Ginnie/FHA approval barriers. Trade finance / factoring sub-segment fragmented but regulated. Not a PE roll-up sweet spot — capital markets expertise required.

522310.title: Mortgage and Nonmortgage Loan Brokers
522310.rationale: 10,957 firms avg 7 emp — extreme fragmentation. NMLS shows ~25K licensed mortgage broker companies (state-licensed entities). Wholesale channel resurgent (UWM dominance). AI loan officer assistants (LenderPrice, Polly) automate 50%+ of broker workflow. Rocket-Mr. Cooper merger 2025 ($9.4B) signals consolidation. Multiples 4-6x.

522320.title: Financial Transactions Processing, Reserve, and Clearinghouse Activities
522320.rationale: Concentrated: Fiserv, FIS, Jack Henry, Global Payments, ACI, DTCC dominate. Network economics + bank integration moats. PE has consolidated payments (Advent/GTCR Worldpay deal $18.5B 2024). AI for fraud detection mature. Multiples 12-18x EBITDA — already premium-valued, no arbitrage.

522390.title: Other Activities Related to Credit Intermediation
522390.rationale: 12,116 firms avg 6 emp — includes loan servicing support, check cashing, money order issuers, escrow/settlement services. Mosaic of state-licensed sub-niches. AI for document review/title automation (States Title/Doma) disrupts settlement. PE active in title insurance roll-ups (Stewart, FNF). Multiples 5-8x.

523150.title: Investment Banking and Securities Intermediation
523150.rationale: Top 10 bulge-bracket (Goldman, MS, JPM) + Houlihan/Lazard hold ~70% fee share per SEC/SIFMA 2024; boutique M&A advisories acquired by larger banks (Centerview, Moelis spin-offs) but bulk already rolled. PE entry blocked by Series 7/24/79 broker-dealer licensing + capital adequacy (Net Capital Rule 15c3-1).

523160.title: Commodity Contracts Intermediation
523160.rationale: CFTC-registered FCMs collapsed from 175 (2007) to 56 (2024) post-MF Global/Dodd-Frank; StoneX, ADM, RJ O'Brien dominate ~80% customer segregated funds. PE roll-up impractical: capital requirements ($20M+ adjusted net capital) and CFTC/NFA registration barriers.

523210.title: Securities and Commodity Exchanges
523210.rationale: Only ~16 SEC-registered national exchanges (NYSE/ICE, Nasdaq, Cboe) + CFTC DCMs (CME, ICE Futures); natural oligopoly with network-effect moats and SEC Reg NMS regulation. Not a roll-up target — too few firms (53 establishments), already consolidated by ICE/Nasdaq/Cboe acquisitions.

523910.title: Miscellaneous Intermediation
523910.rationale: 11,596 firms at 5.1 avg employees = extremely fragmented (private placement agents, venture capital placement, alt asset distributors); many sub-$5M EBITDA with limited PE precedent. AI advantage in deal sourcing/matching (CapIQ, PitchBook substitutes) but regulatory perimeter unclear vs 523150.
sources: SEC Reg D Form D filings

523940.title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice
523940.rationale: RIA roll-up is THE active PE thesis: Focus Financial (Clayton Dubilier $7B take-private 2023), Hightower (Thomas H. Lee), Mariner, Mercer, Wealth Enhancement, Creative Planning paying 8-12x EBITDA on sub-$5M sellers, exiting at 15-22x per DeVoe/Echelon 2024 reports; 15,000+ SEC-registered RIAs + 17,000 state-registered. Cerulli: $128T AUM by 2027, 40% advisors 55+ (succession driver).
sources: Echelon Partners RIA M&A Report Q4 2024

523991.title: Trust, Fiduciary, and Custody Activities
523991.rationale: 2,805 firms but bank trust departments dominate AUC (BNY Mellon, State Street, Northern Trust ~$100T+ combined); non-depository trust companies (SD/NV/WY charters) fragmented and acquired by RIA aggregators (Mercer, Mariner) for fiduciary capability. OCC/state-banking regulation slows deal velocity.

523999.title: Miscellaneous Financial Investment Activities
523999.rationale: Catch-all bucket (mortgage REITs sub-advisors, securitization servicers, oil/gas royalty trusts, deposit brokers) with 2,033 firms at 14.6 avg employees; PE activity scattered (Alter Domus, IQ-EQ in fund admin sub-segment) but heterogeneity limits clean roll-up thesis vs focused 523940.

524113.title: Direct Life Insurance Carriers
524113.rationale: Capital-intensive oligopoly (MetLife, Prudential, NW Mutual, NY Life, Lincoln) with NAIC RBC requirements blocking sub-scale entry. Carrier multiples 8-11x but $1B+ minimum scale; not a PE roll-up category - LDTI accounting and reserve requirements deter consolidation.

524114.title: Direct Health and Medical Insurance Carriers
524114.rationale: Top-5 carriers (UnitedHealth, Elevance, CVS/Aetna, Cigna, Humana) hold ~50% premium share; MLR floor (80-85%) caps margins; state DOI approval friction blocks roll-up. Not viable for PE - regulated utility-like economics.

524126.title: Direct Property and Casualty Insurance Carriers
524126.rationale: Top-10 (State Farm, Berkshire/GEICO, Progressive, Allstate, Liberty Mutual, Travelers, USAA, Farmers, Nationwide, Chubb) = ~70% direct written premium per NAIC 2024. Capital/RBC barriers; specialty MGAs (E&S) better PE play. AI in underwriting/claims (Lemonade, Hippo) but carrier roll-ups rare.

524127.title: Direct Title Insurance Carriers
524127.rationale: Big 4 (Fidelity National, First American, Old Republic, Stewart) ~85% market share; agencies (avg 12 emp) fragmented but cyclical to housing volume. AI title automation high (search, exam, escrow doc gen) but carrier tier consolidated. Agency layer better play.

524128.title: Other Direct Insurance (except Life, Health, and Medical) Carriers
524128.rationale: Specialty/surety/warranty/pet insurance niche with 586 firms; more fragmented but still capital-intensive. Trupanion, Markel-style specialty carriers; some PE interest in warranty (Asurion). Multiples 7-10x.

524130.title: Reinsurance Carriers
524130.rationale: Global oligopoly (Munich Re, Swiss Re, Hannover, SCOR, Berkshire); Bermuda/Cayman domicile dominant. Capital-intensive ($500M+ min); not a PE roll-up - hard market cycles drive returns. ILS/sidecar capital alternative.

524210.title: Insurance Agencies and Brokerages
524210.rationale: #1 PE roll-up category US: 135K firms avg 6 employees, Sica Fletcher tracked 695 deals 2024 (record); sub-$1M EBITDA at 5-7x, $5M+ EBITDA at 11-16x = pure multiple arbitrage. Acrisure ($24B rev), Hub, BroadStreet, World, Inszone all PE-backed roll-ups. Asset-light, recurring commission, sticky books.
sources: Sica Fletcher Year-End 2024 Agency M&A Report

524291.title: Claims Adjusting
524291.rationale: 4,554 firms avg 9 emp - fragmented IA (independent adjuster) and TPA market; CCMSI, Crawford, Sedgwick consolidating but long tail exists. AI photo estimation (CCC, Tractable, Hyperscience) replaces 30-50% of adjuster time. Cat-driven volatility; multiples 6-9x.

524292.title: Pharmacy Benefit Management and Other Third Party Administration of Insurance and Pension Funds
524292.rationale: PBM oligopoly (CVS Caremark, Express Scripts, OptumRx) ~80% Rx; TPA layer more fragmented (Meritain, HealthEquity, Trion). Health TPA roll-ups active (Brown & Brown Workplace Benefits). Margins compressed by transparency rules.

524298.title: All Other Insurance Related Activities
524298.rationale: Catch-all: actuarial consulting, loss control, salvage, premium audit - 3,035 firms avg 12 emp. Specialty services (Davies Group, Crawford niche brands) attractive but heterogeneous; harder to standardize.

525110.title: Pension Funds
525110.rationale: Pension funds are legal vehicles (DB/DC plans, Taft-Hartley), not operating companies; ERISA-regulated, sponsor-managed with near-zero direct employment (DOL Form 5500 ~700K plans hold $40T but are trusts). Not roll-up addressable.

525120.title: Health and Welfare Funds
525120.rationale: Taft-Hartley/multi-employer welfare trusts holding health/welfare benefit assets; ERISA fiduciary structures with trustees, not for-profit operators. Administration outsourced to TPAs (a separate NAICS). Not roll-up viable.

525190.title: Other Insurance Funds
525190.rationale: Workers' comp self-insurance trusts, state guaranty associations, captive funds — fiduciary/regulatory vehicles, not commercial operators. Capital tied up by statute; M&A occurs at sponsor or TPA level, not the fund itself.

525910.title: Open-End Investment Funds
525910.rationale: Mutual fund and ETF legal entities (40-Act); 334 firms / 1,735 employees in CBP because the fund itself is a shell — economics sit with the adviser (NAICS 523930, e.g. BlackRock 40%+ op margins, Vanguard mutual). Roll-ups happen at adviser/RIA level, not fund vehicles.

525920.title: Trusts, Estates, and Agency Accounts
525920.rationale: Personal trusts, estates, custodial accounts — fiduciary holding vehicles administered by trust companies/banks (NAICS 522190/523991). No employees in fund itself; consolidation is at trust-company/RIA level via wealth-management roll-ups, not at this NAICS.

525990.title: Other Financial Vehicles
525990.rationale: Securitization SPVs, REITs, REMICs, ABCP conduits, CLO issuers — 1,590 firms / 7,252 employees reflect minimal staffing because vehicles are bankruptcy-remote shells. Real businesses (CLO managers, REIT sponsors, servicers) sit in 523/525910 adviser codes. Vehicles themselves are not roll-up targets.

531110.title: Lessors of Residential Buildings and Dwellings
531110.rationale: Highly fragmented (74,908 firms, 4.6 avg employees) mom-and-pop landlords; core asset is real estate not labor, so AI replaceability is low. Roll-up viable but PE prefers SFR REITs (Invitation Homes, AMH) which already consolidated institutional segment; small-landlord arbitrage limited by capex intensity.

531120.title: Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (except Miniwarehouses)
531120.rationale: Commercial landlords face post-2023 office vacancy crisis (~19% national per CBRE Q4 2024); asset-heavy model resists AI disruption but also caps roll-up multiples. Fragmented (34,567 firms) but PE consolidation happens via REIT M&A not SMB roll-up.

531130.title: Lessors of Miniwarehouses and Self-Storage Units
531130.rationale: Self-storage is the textbook PE roll-up: 18,286 firms, top-5 REITs (Public Storage, Extra Space, CubeSmart) hold ~30% share leaving 70% to consolidate at 5-7x EBITDA vs REIT 18-22x. AI revenue management (Yardi Kube, Storable) drives 200-400bps NOI uplift.

531190.title: Lessors of Other Real Estate Property
531190.rationale: Heterogeneous catch-all (land, mobile home parks, billboards); MHP segment has active PE roll-up (Sun Communities, ELS) but billboards/land are sleepy. Mixed thesis; only MHP sub-vertical scores higher.

531210.title: Offices of Real Estate Agents and Brokers
531210.rationale: NAR Sitzer-Burnett settlement (Aug 2024) decoupled buyer commissions, compressing residential brokerage margins 20-30%; Compass/Anywhere/eXp trading at 4-6x EBITDA (down from 10x pre-2024). AI lead-gen (Zillow Flex, Opendoor) further disintermediates agents. AVOID for roll-up.

531311.title: Residential Property Managers
531311.rationale: ARCHETYPE PE roll-up: 57,435 firms, 8.8 avg employees, $2-5M revenue typical; active consolidators (Evernest, Mynd, Poplar Homes, BetterWho) buying at 4-6x SDE and selling platforms at 10-14x EBITDA. AI (AppFolio, Buildium, EliseAI leasing bots) cuts leasing-agent OPEX 30-40%, accretive to margin.

531312.title: Nonresidential Property Managers
531312.rationale: Commercial PM partially consolidated by CBRE/JLL/Cushman&Wakefield/Newmark (Big 4 control ~25-30% of institutional CRE); mid-market opportunity exists but client concentration risk high. AI building-ops (Yardi Voyager, MRI) reduces engineer headcount but capex-heavy adoption.

531320.title: Offices of Real Estate Appraisers
531320.rationale: AVMs (Zillow Zestimate, CoreLogic, HouseCanary) + Fannie Mae's 2022 appraisal waiver expansion cut residential appraisal volume 30%+ by 2024; FHFA approved hybrid/desktop appraisals as default. Existential AI threat outweighs fragmentation upside.

531390.title: Other Activities Related to Real Estate
531390.rationale: Catch-all (escrow, fiduciary, listing services, consultants); fragmented but heterogeneous, hard to roll up coherent platform. Title/escrow subsegments have specialized PE activity but most of NAICS is sub-scale.

532111.title: Passenger Car Rental
532111.rationale: Big 3 (Hertz/Avis/Enterprise) control ~95% of US market; Enterprise alone ~$30B revenue. Roll-up impossible at scale; AI dynamic pricing already deployed by majors but physical fleet ops remain labor/capex heavy.

532112.title: Passenger Car Leasing
532112.rationale: Only 490 firms, mostly captive finance arms of OEMs (Ford Credit, GM Financial) or bank-backed lessors. Balance-sheet intensive (residual risk), not a fragmented services roll-up; AI helps residual forecasting marginally.

532120.title: Truck, Utility Trailer, and RV (Recreational Vehicle) Rental and Leasing
532120.rationale: Penske/Ryder/U-Haul dominate truck leasing but RV and utility-trailer segments have ~6,300 firms with regional independents. Ryder trades ~8x EBITDA; RV rental (Outdoorsy/RVshare marketplace) disrupting but rental yards still local.

532210.title: Consumer Electronics and Appliances Rental
532210.rationale: Rent-A-Center/Aaron's dominate rent-to-own; segment in secular decline as financing apps (Affirm/Klarna) replace RTO model. Multiples compressed 4-6x; AI cannot reverse demand decay.

532281.title: Formal Wear and Costume Rental
532281.rationale: Men's Wearhouse/Jos. A. Bank consolidated tuxedo; Rent the Runway public-equity disaster (~95% off IPO). Thin margins, fashion-risk inventory, no roll-up thesis.

532282.title: Video Tape and Disc Rental
532282.rationale: Category effectively dead post-Blockbuster (2010 BK); 444 firms residual (Redbox kiosks shut 2024, Family Video closed 2021). No investable thesis at any multiple.
sources: Chicken Soup for the Soul/Redbox Ch.11 2024

532283.title: Home Health Equipment Rental
532283.rationale: DME (CPAP, wheelchairs, oxygen) ~1,891 firms; reimbursement-driven via Medicare Part B competitive bidding. AdaptHealth/Apria/Lincare consolidating at 8-10x; AI for prior-auth/RCM is real margin lever.

532284.title: Recreational Goods Rental
532284.rationale: Ski/bike/kayak/beach gear; ~2,924 firms highly seasonal and tourist-dependent. Marketplaces (Spinlister, Outdoorsy) take rake; no PE-grade roll-up due to weather/seasonality risk.

532289.title: All Other Consumer Goods Rental
532289.rationale: Grab-bag (party rentals, musical instruments, furniture rental). CORT (Berkshire) leads furniture rental; party rental fragmented but exposed to events cyclicality. Mixed thesis, low multiples 4-6x.

532310.title: General Rental Centers
532310.rationale: Local 'rent-it' stores serving DIY/contractor mixed fleet; 2,775 firms with strong fragmentation. Sunbelt/United Rentals acquire selectively at 6-8x; AI for utilization/dispatch genuine lever.

532411.title: Commercial Air, Rail, and Water Transportation Equipment Rental and Leasing
532411.rationale: Aircraft (AerCap/SMBC), rail (GATX/Trinity), marine container (Triton) — heavy balance sheet, not fragmented; ~642 firms but top 10 control most assets. AerCap trades ~1.1x book, not a roll-up.

532412.title: Construction, Mining, and Forestry Machinery and Equipment Rental and Leasing
532412.rationale: ACTIVE PE roll-up: United Rentals (~$15B rev, ~8-9x EV/EBITDA) and Ashtead/Sunbelt (~11x) acquiring 50+ regional players/year; Herc IPO'd 2016 trades ~7x. 5,079 firms, ARA tracks 50-55% penetration room.

532420.title: Office Machinery and Equipment Rental and Leasing
532420.rationale: Copier/MPS dealers (Xerox/Canon/Ricoh channel); category eroding as paperless/cloud reduces print volumes. Flex Technology rolled up dealers at 5-7x; thesis exists but TAM shrinking 3-5%/yr.

532490.title: Other Commercial and Industrial Machinery and Equipment Rental and Leasing
532490.rationale: Specialty rental (pumps, power, climate, modular space, trench shoring); 9,599 firms with niches like WillScot/McGrath (modular ~10-12x) and Atlas Copco specialty. Strong roll-up activity, lower competitive pressure than 532412.

533110.title: Lessors of Nonfinancial Intangible Assets (except Copyrighted Works)
533110.rationale: 2.7K firms — patent licensing, trademark licensing, franchise agreements, mineral rights, brand licensing. High EBITDA margins (40-60%) due to capital-light royalty model. BUT: PE roll-up unsuitable — value is in specific IP portfolios (idiosyncratic), not operational synergies. Patent assertion entities (NPEs/PAEs) face regulatory/political backlash, eBay v. MercExchange limited injunctions, IPR proceedings reduce patent value. Successful platforms (IPG, Acacia, Marathon) are public/specialty. AI delta low: AI primarily affects valuation methodology, not operations. Roll-up logic breaks down because portfolios don't combine well. M=3 specialty only.

541110.title: Offices of Lawyers
541110.rationale: 167K firms with avg 6.5 employees per Census CBP — extremely fragmented but state bar UPL/MRPC rules restrict non-lawyer ownership in 49 states (only AZ permits ABS), capping rollup viability and PE multiples; AI displaces doc-review/discovery (~30% of opex) but rainmaker partner economics persist.

541120.title: Offices of Notaries
541120.rationale: CBP shows 0 paid employment — sole-proprietor cottage industry with state-regulated fees ($5-$15/notarization); RON (Remote Online Notarization) laws in 44+ states are commoditizing the workflow toward platforms (Notarize, DocuSign), leaving no roll-up target inventory.

541191.title: Title Abstract and Settlement Offices
541191.rationale: 9.7K firms; market dominated by 'Big 4' underwriters (Fidelity National, First American, Old Republic, Stewart hold ~80% per ALTA market share) but the agent layer remains fragmented and PE-active (Doma, States Title); refi-cycle revenue volatility caps multiples at 5-8x.

541199.title: All Other Legal Services
541199.rationale: 5.2K firms (paralegal, process server, legal support) — fragmented but bar association UPL rules constrain non-lawyer rollups; AI accelerates document workflows (LegalZoom precedent shows ~25% gross margin lift) but exit market thin.

541211.title: Offices of Certified Public Accountants
541211.rationale: 55.6K firms avg 10 employees; AICPA shows ~75% of firms <10 staff, and AICPA changed rules in 2024 allowing non-CPA ownership/PE structures (Top 200 PE deals: EisnerAmper, Citrin Cooperman, Aprio, Baker Tilly); buy 4-6x EBITDA, sell platforms at 10-14x.

541213.title: Tax Preparation Services
541213.rationale: 28K firms but H&R Block + Intuit + Liberty/Jackson Hewitt hold ~50% of paid-prep market per IRS data; remaining independents have severe seasonality, low EBITDA after seasonal labor, and AI (TurboTax+GenAI) commoditizes the lower end — limits multiple arbitrage.

541214.title: Payroll Services
541214.rationale: Only 5.5K firms but ADP + Paychex + Paycom + Gusto + Rippling control >75% of SMB payroll (ADP 10-K shows ~800K SMB clients alone); software-scale economics make rollup of remaining independents low-leverage with thin exit market.

541219.title: Other Accounting Services
541219.rationale: 46K firms avg 5 employees — bookkeeping/controller services, lowest barrier of any accounting sub-code, no CPA license required so PE has full rollup freedom; AI agents (Pilot, Bench, Digits) take 30-50% of opex out — strong arbitrage between mom-and-pop multiples (3-4x) and tech-enabled platform (10x+).

541310.title: Architectural Services
541310.rationale: 22.6K firms per AIA — top 100 firms only ~25% of fees, the rest fragmented; state licensure (NCARB) restricts ownership in ~30 states, and project-driven revenue lumpiness keeps EBITDA margins low and multiples in 5-7x range.

541320.title: Landscape Architectural Services
541320.rationale: 5.2K firms avg 5 employees, state-licensed in 49 states (CLARB), heavily project-tied to construction cycle; tiny TAM (~$3B per ASLA) makes platform builds unattractive at PE scale.

541330.title: Engineering Services
541330.rationale: 62.6K firms but ACEC reports top 500 (AECOM/Jacobs/WSP/Stantec) hold ~40% of $400B+ revenue; mid-market (MEP, civil) actively rolled up by IFS/Bowman/NV5 — buy 6-8x, sell 12-15x as platforms scale past $50M EBITDA.

541340.title: Drafting Services
541340.rationale: Only 3K firms, avg 3.1 employees — micro segment commoditized by CAD outsourcing to India/Philippines and now GenAI-CAD; no clear PE exit market.

541350.title: Building Inspection Services
541350.rationale: 7.7K firms avg 3.5 employees, dominated by sole owner-operators (ASHI/InterNACHI ~100K licensed inspectors); franchise rollups exist (Pillar To Post, WIN), recent PE activity at low single-digit multiples — physical site visits limit AI displacement.

541360.title: Geophysical Surveying and Mapping Services
541360.rationale: Only 1K firms, oil & gas dependent (Schlumberger/SLB, CGG, TGS dominate seismic); cyclical with energy capex, not a PE rollup target.

541370.title: Surveying and Mapping (except Geophysical) Services
541370.rationale: 7.4K firms — actively consolidated by Bowman Consulting (BWMN) and NV5 acquisitions at 7-10x EBITDA; LiDAR/drone automation lifts margins; PLS licensure adds some friction but allows owned-entity rollups.

541380.title: Testing Laboratories and Services
541380.rationale: 7.5K firms but Eurofins, SGS, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, Element are aggressive rollup machines (Eurofins 10-K shows >900 labs from acquisitions); fragmented mid-market still buys at 7-9x, sells in platform at 12-15x.

541410.title: Interior Design Services
541410.rationale: 16.3K firms avg 3.3 employees — overwhelmingly sole-practitioner with relationship-based revenue; no PE precedent at scale, limited exit beyond strategic buyers in commercial AEC.

541420.title: Industrial Design Services
541420.rationale: 1.6K firms — IDEO/Frog/Continuum tier consolidated under consulting majors (Accenture, Deloitte Digital), little remaining rollup inventory.

541430.title: Graphic Design Services
541430.rationale: 15.9K firms avg 3.2 employees; Canva/Midjourney/Figma AI gut commodity demand — BLS projects employment growth at 2% vs 4% all-occupations through 2032; no PE exit precedent.

541490.title: Other Specialized Design Services
541490.rationale: 3.4K firms avg 3.5 employees — fashion/jewelry/float design micro-niche; project-based, low multiples.

541511.title: Custom Computer Programming Services
541511.rationale: 68K firms — PE has run 100+ IT services platforms (Endava, EPAM, Globant, Insight, Presidio, Converge); GenAI both threatens labor arbitrage and creates AI-services premium; buy 6-8x, sell 10-14x at scale.

541512.title: Computer Systems Design Services
541512.rationale: 58K firms avg 14 employees — MSP/integrator space is the most-active PE vertical of last 5 years (Thoma Bravo, Berkshire Partners, Audax in Trace3/Presidio/Converge/Compugen); buy 5-7x, platform sell 11-14x.

541513.title: Computer Facilities Management Services
541513.rationale: 6.9K firms avg 17 employees — outsourced data center ops; hyperscaler insourcing (AWS/Azure/GCP) shrinks TAM and concentrates buy-side at strategic acquirers.

541519.title: Other Computer Related Services
541519.rationale: 12K firms avg 12 employees — catch-all for managed services/cybersecurity adjacent; same PE roll-up dynamics as 541512 (Evergreen Services Group, Thrive, New Charter active).

541611.title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services
541611.rationale: 97.7K firms (largest 541 sub by count) avg 8 employees — extreme fragmentation outside MBB/Big 4; PE rollups (West Monroe, Riveron, AlixPartners) demonstrate 7-9x buy / 12-15x exit; AI assistants cut research/deck labor 40%.

541612.title: Human Resources Consulting Services
541612.rationale: 9K firms — Mercer/AON/WTW dominate top tier but mid-market PEO/benefits consulting fragmented; PE active (Newport, OneDigital, World Insurance) at 8-11x.

541613.title: Marketing Consulting Services
541613.rationale: 50K firms avg 5.6 employees — extreme fragmentation; GenAI cannibalizes copy/research/SEO labor 50%+; rollups (Stagwell, S4 Capital, Dept) but multiples compressed by AI substitution risk; buy 5-7x, sell 8-10x.

541614.title: Process, Physical Distribution, and Logistics Consulting Services
541614.rationale: 8.7K firms — supply chain consulting niche, mid-fragmented with Accenture/Kearney/BCG at top; PE active in specialty SCM (West Monroe, AArete).

541618.title: Other Management Consulting Services
541618.rationale: 13K firms avg 3.5 employees — micro consultants; rollup risk is portfolio drag, AI substitution high.

541620.title: Environmental Consulting Services
541620.rationale: 10K firms — extremely PE-active (Montrose, Verdantas, Apex, Trinity, RPS, EHS Today's top 100 fragmented); ESG/EPA driven recurring revenue, buy 7-9x sell 13-16x as evidenced by Montrose IPO and ongoing carve-outs.

541690.title: Other Scientific and Technical Consulting Services
541690.rationale: 27K firms — heterogeneous bucket (energy, agronomy, security); PE rollups in energy consulting (Enercon, ERM) at 8-11x.

541713.title: Research and Development in Nanotechnology
541713.rationale: 2.3K firms avg 78 employees — mostly grant/VC-funded research labs not cash-flow generative; not a PE rollup target.

541714.title: Research and Development in Biotechnology (except Nanobiotechnology)
541714.rationale: 5.3K firms — typically pre-revenue biotech; exits are pharma M&A or IPO not PE roll-up; AI drug discovery (Recursion, Insitro) is venture territory.

541715.title: Research and Development in the Physical, Engineering, and Life Sciences (except Nanotechnology and Biotechnology)
541715.rationale: 12.5K firms but federal labs and defense primes (Battelle, SAIC, Leidos, KBR) dominate; CRO segment (IQVIA, ICON, Charles River) consolidated; little SMB rollup inventory.

541720.title: Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities
541720.rationale: 2.1K firms — RAND, NORC, Westat, Mathematica dominate; government-grant funded, not a PE play.

541810.title: Advertising Agencies
541810.rationale: 15K firms — WPP/Omnicom/IPG/Publicis hold top tier but mid-market agency rollups thrive (Stagwell, Plus Company, New Engen); GenAI shifts labor pyramid — V should be HIGHER than baseline 3 because creative+media+research workflows are all AI-displaceable.

541820.title: Public Relations Agencies
541820.rationale: 8.6K firms — Edelman/FleishmanHillard/Weber Shandwick at top, long tail rollups (Stagwell, Next 15); AI press-release/monitoring tools (Muck Rack, Cision) cut labor 40%.

541830.title: Media Buying Agencies
541830.rationale: Only 809 firms — GroupM/Publicis Media/IPG Mediabrands/Dentsu/Horizon dominate $200B+ buys; AI/DSP automation already standard, no rollup play.

541840.title: Media Representatives
541840.rationale: 1.3K firms — middleman segment being disintermediated by programmatic (DV360, The Trade Desk); shrinking TAM.

541850.title: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising
541850.rationale: 2.5K firms but Clear Channel Outdoor, Outfront, Lamar Advertising control ~70% of OOH revenue per OAAA; physical inventory consolidated by REIT-style operators, not AI-displaceable.

541860.title: Direct Mail Advertising
541860.rationale: 1.9K firms — declining channel per USPS volume reports (-30% in decade), some PE consolidation (Valassis/Vericast) but secular decline limits multiples.

541870.title: Advertising Material Distribution Services
541870.rationale: 1.3K firms — physical flyer/sample distribution; minimal AI leverage, low margins, declining demand.

541890.title: Other Services Related to Advertising
541890.rationale: 7.3K firms — promotional products, signage, ad production; PE rollup activity in promo products (HALO, 4imprint) at 7-9x.

541910.title: Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling
541910.rationale: 4.5K firms but Nielsen, Kantar, Ipsos, NielsenIQ, Numerator hold ~50%+ share per ESOMAR; synthetic AI respondents (Yabble, Remesh) erode panel pricing.

541921.title: Photography Studios, Portrait
541921.rationale: 11K firms avg 3.3 employees — wedding/portrait studios crushed by smartphones + GenAI portraits; no PE exit market.

541922.title: Commercial Photography
541922.rationale: 4.9K firms avg 2.5 employees — product/stock photography being replaced by Midjourney/DALL-E commercial usage; no rollup activity.

541930.title: Translation and Interpretation Services
