541930.rationale: 2.8K firms — TransPerfect, LanguageLine, Lionbridge, RWS in top tier; GenAI (DeepL, GPT-4) decimates literal translation pricing — strong arbitrage IF positioned as 'AI + human QA' (CSA Research shows 20-40% price compression underway).

541940.title: Veterinary Services
541940.rationale: 34K practices — HOTTEST PE rollup of decade (Mars/VCA, NVA, BluePearl, MedVet, Thrive); AVMA shows 75% still independent; buy 6-8x, exit 14-18x EBITDA per recent deals; AI replaceability LOW (physical exam) but consolidation arbitrage massive — M should be HIGHER than baseline 4.

541990.title: All Other Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services
541990.rationale: 22.9K firms — catch-all bucket (appraisers, weather forecasters, agents/managers); too heterogeneous for a single platform thesis.

551111.title: Offices of Bank Holding Companies
551111.rationale: Bank holdcos are regulated parent entities (BHC Act, Fed supervision). Not operating businesses — revenue is dividends/interest from subsidiary banks. Cannot roll up holdcos themselves; you would roll up the underlying banks (NAICS 522), which face entirely different regulatory and capital regimes.
sources: Federal Reserve Bank Holding Company Supervision Manual

551112.title: Offices of Other Holding Companies
551112.rationale: Non-bank holdcos exist to own equity/debt in subsidiaries; no operating activity. Rolling these up is meaningless — value sits in the operating subs, not the holding shell. PE acquires the underlying portfolio companies directly, never the holdco layer as a thesis.

551114.title: Corporate, Subsidiary, and Regional Managing Offices
551114.rationale: HQ/management offices of larger enterprises (avg 81 employees reflects captive corporate functions). Not standalone businesses — they are cost centers inside parent firms. No M&A market for HQ offices as such; PE outsourcing thesis would target NAICS 5614/5419 (BPO, consulting), not 551114.

561110.title: Office Administrative Services
561110.rationale: Catch-all admin/back-office work (scheduling, reporting, billing support) is highly displaceable by LLM agents; 37K firms avg 15 FTE = extreme fragmentation, but pure-admin shops have weak moats and tiny multiples (3-5x SDE) - margin arbitrage limited.

561210.title: Facilities Support Services
561210.rationale: Integrated FM (Aramark, ABM, Sodexo) bundles janitorial+maintenance+catering on long-term contracts; consolidation already advanced but mid-market IFM rollups still trade 7-9x EBITDA. Labor 65-70% of cost - AI dispatch/IoT helps but bodies still required.

561311.title: Employment Placement Agencies
561311.rationale: Perm placement = sourcing+screening+matching, all AI-displaceable (LinkedIn Recruiter, Eightfold, HireEZ). ASA data shows perm fees down YoY; 7K firms but margins compress as AI tools commoditize sourcing. Multiples 4-6x EBITDA, thin.
sources: American Staffing Association 2024 Staffing Industry Forecast

561312.title: Executive Search Services
561312.rationale: Retained C-suite search is relationship/judgment-led - Heidrick, Korn Ferry, Spencer Stuart unchallenged by AI. 6.2K boutiques avg 7.7 FTE = ultra-fragmented but personal book IP doesn't transfer - rollups historically fail (Heidrick acquisitions partial exception).

561320.title: Temporary Help Services
561320.rationale: Massive ($170B+ US) but margin-thin (3-5% EBITDA per Manpower/Kelly/RobertHalf 10-Ks); AI matching platforms (Indeed Flex, Instawork) compress middleman spread. Light industrial staffing rolls up at 5-7x but synergies modest - more scale game than AI play.

561330.title: Professional Employer Organizations
561330.rationale: PEO = co-employment + payroll/benefits/comp, regulatory moat (ESAC, IRS CPEO); Insperity/TriNet/Paychex dominate. Avg 893 worksite employees per firm = scale already; mid-market PEO rollups trade 8-11x EBITDA but compliance burden high.

561410.title: Document Preparation Services
561410.rationale: Transcription, resume prep, form filing - 100% LLM-displaceable; revenue per FTE will collapse 50%+ by 2027. Fragmented (4.9K firms, 8.9 FTE avg) but TAM evaporating; not a rollup target, more a wind-down category.
sources: BLS OES 43-9022 word processors wage data 2024

561421.title: Telephone Answering Services
561421.rationale: Live answering services (Moneypenny, AnswerConnect, Specialty Answering) directly displaceable by voice AI (Bland, Vapi, Retell). Margins currently 15-22% but per-minute pricing will compress 60%+. Roll-ups possible but acquiring melting-ice-cube revenue.

561422.title: Telemarketing Bureaus and Other Contact Centers
561422.rationale: BPO contact centers (TaskUs, Concentrix, TTEC, Teleperformance) facing existential AI threat - 30-50% seat reduction by 2027 per Bain/HFS. 3.3K firms, 386K employees = labor-heavy; pure-play telemarketing margins 6-10%. Distressed M&A opportunity.

561431.title: Private Mail Centers
561431.rationale: UPS Store/PostalAnnex franchise model; physical pack-ship-mailbox is hands-on, low AI exposure. 5.6K locations avg 5 FTE = highly fragmented but unit economics modest ($300-500K AUV); franchise rollup angle (e.g. PostNet) limited synergy.

561439.title: Other Business Service Centers (including Copy Shops)
561439.rationale: Copy/print/sign shops (FedEx Office dominant in commercial) - print volume secularly declining; AI doesn't help much, digital substitution is the real headwind. Mom-pop fragmented but not a rollup target.

561440.title: Collection Agencies
561440.rationale: ARM industry: Encore Capital, PRA Group dominate debt buying; agency-only side fragmented (3K firms). AI voice + predictive contact (TrueAccord, Skit) cuts FTE 40%+. CFPB Reg F compliance moat helps consolidators. Multiples 5-7x.

561450.title: Credit Bureaus
561450.rationale: Equifax, Experian, TransUnion = oligopoly (>80% share); 456 'firms' mostly resellers. Data moat + regulation (FCRA) blocks rollups. Margins already 28-35%; not a PE target at fragment level.

561491.title: Repossession Services
561491.rationale: Field repo agents required physically; LPR camera networks (DRN/MVTRAC owned by Motorola) + AI route optimization helping skip tracing. 655 firms avg 10 FTE = consolidation play, but cyclical with auto credit. Few PE plays (KAR/OPENLANE adjacencies).

561492.title: Court Reporting and Stenotype Services
561492.rationale: Stenographer shortage + AI transcription (Veritext, Esquire already rolling up with $1B+ revs each backed by Leonard Green/Kohlberg) makes this an active PE thesis; remote depo platforms cut cost 40%. 3.2K firms avg 4 FTE = peak fragmentation. Trades 8-11x.

561499.title: All Other Business Support Services
561499.rationale: Catch-all bucket (lobby reception, notary, virtual assistants, data entry) - high AI exposure but heterogeneous makes thesis hard to underwrite. Not actionable as single rollup.

561510.title: Travel Agencies
561510.rationale: Corporate TMC (AmEx GBT, BCD, CWT) consolidated; leisure OTAs (Booking, Expedia) ate the middle. Surviving 9.4K agencies skew niche/luxury - AI itinerary tools (Mindtrip, Otto) erode advisor value-add but luxury concierge resilient. Multiples 4-6x.

561520.title: Tour Operators
561520.rationale: Tour operations are logistics+local relationships (TUI, Intrepid, G Adventures); AI helps marketing but doesn't replace ground ops. 2.5K firms fragmented in adventure/experiential niches but post-COVID volatility scared PE.

561591.title: Convention and Visitors Bureaus
561591.rationale: CVBs are public/nonprofit DMOs funded by hotel taxes (Destinations Intl members) - not PE-investable. Not a rollup category.

561599.title: All Other Travel Arrangement and Reservation Services
561599.rationale: Ticket consolidators, DMC, timeshare exchanges - mixed bag. Sabre/Amadeus GDS dominate B2B layer; AI assists ops but no clean rollup thesis.

561611.title: Investigation and Personal Background Check Services
561611.rationale: Background screening (Sterling, First Advantage, HireRight) heavily automated already - records pulls, ID verification, MVR via APIs. PI/investigations less so. FCRA compliance moat; mid-market screeners trade 9-12x. Solid PE thesis.

561612.title: Security Guards and Patrol Services
561612.rationale: Allied Universal/GardaWorld/Securitas dominate ($25B+ each) but 10K small firms still fragmented in regional. Margins notoriously thin (4-7%); AI video analytics (Deep Sentinel, Ambient.ai) substitute some posts but liability/uniformed presence still required. Multiples 6-8x for scaled.

561613.title: Armored Car Services
561613.rationale: Brink's, Loomis, GardaWorld Cash = global oligopoly (>70% US share); cash-in-transit declining with digital payments but ATM/retail cash mgmt steady. 766 firms but top 3 dominate. Not fragmentable PE play.

561621.title: Security Systems Services (except Locksmiths)
561621.rationale: Alarm install + monitoring (ADT, Vivint/NRG, Brinks Home) with recurring RMR economics - PE darling. Mid-market dealers (Pye-Barker, Pavion) actively rolling up at 12-15x EBITDA on RMR multiples (35-50x monthly). AI video analytics expands TAM.

561622.title: Locksmiths
561622.rationale: Mobile field service, 4K firms avg 4 FTE = peak fragmentation but tiny unit economics (~$300K AUV) and smart-lock substitution slowly eroding emergency-call demand. No rollup track record at scale.

561710.title: Exterminating and Pest Control Services
561710.rationale: Rollins (Orkin), Rentokil-Terminix dominate but 16K firms remain. Classic PE rollup: recurring contracts, route density, 20-25% margins. Multiples 12-16x EBITDA, often higher for scaled platforms. AI minimal direct impact - route optimization only. Best-in-class thesis.
sources: Capstone Partners pest control M&A reports

561720.title: Janitorial Services
561720.rationale: ABM, ISS, Sodexo at top; 67K firms with 1M employees = extreme fragmentation but margins 5-8% thin. Autonomous floor scrubbers (Brain Corp, Avidbots) help labor but adoption slow. Rollups (Vonachen, Marsden) trade 6-8x; high churn customers. Volume play, not multiple-arbitrage.

561730.title: Landscaping Services
561730.rationale: BrightView, Yellowstone (KKR), Aspen Grove, Juniper, LandCare = active commercial rollups at 9-12x. 117K firms (mostly residential mow-and-blow) impossible to consolidate but commercial maintenance + snow removal proven thesis. Margins 10-14% on commercial routes.

561740.title: Carpet and Upholstery Cleaning Services
561740.rationale: Stanley Steemer, Chem-Dry franchises + 7K independents. Hard floors trend hurts demand; tiny AUV ($250-400K), franchise-dominant - no PE thesis at fragment level. AI lead gen helps marketing only.

561790.title: Other Services to Buildings and Dwellings
561790.rationale: Window cleaning, pressure washing, chimney sweep, gutter - residential home services. Apex Service Partners, Wrench Group rollup adjacency (HVAC focus though). Tiny units but route density valuable; window cleaning rollups (Labor Panes, Fish) emerging at 6-9x.

561910.title: Packaging and Labeling Services
561910.rationale: Contract packaging (co-pack) for CPG - some PE consolidation (Assembled Brands, ProAmpac adjacent). 1.8K firms with avg 27 FTE = mid-size. AI/vision automation helps QC. Multiples 7-9x but customer concentration risk.

561920.title: Convention and Trade Show Organizers
561920.rationale: Informa, RX (Reed Exhibitions), Emerald dominate top tier; 6K firms include small show producers. AI helps matchmaking/lead retrieval but in-person events resilient post-COVID. Rollups (Clarion, Tarsus) trade 10-13x but cyclical.

561990.title: All Other Support Services
561990.rationale: Catch-all (auctioneers, contractors' equipment management, water meter reading, etc.) - heterogeneous, no thesis.

562111.title: Solid Waste Collection
562111.rationale: Heavily consolidated: WM, Republic Services, Waste Connections, Casella control ~70% of US revenue (SEC 10-Ks 2024). Roll-up arbitrage exhausted at scale; route-density AI (routing, RFID bin tracking) deployed by majors but bolt-on PE multiples compressed to 8-10x EBITDA.

562112.title: Hazardous Waste Collection
562112.rationale: Clean Harbors dominates US hazwaste collection alongside Stericycle (medical) and US Ecology/Republic ES; RCRA/DOT compliance creates moat that limits new entrants. AI value limited to manifest automation and route optimization; physical handling and regulatory expertise are bottlenecks.

562119.title: Other Waste Collection
562119.rationale: Fragmented residual category (construction debris haulers, specialty collection) with avg 7.8 employees per firm (CBP). Sub-scale operators ripe for regional roll-ups at 4-6x EBITDA; AI dispatch/routing meaningful uplift but capital-intensive truck fleets cap multiple expansion.

562211.title: Hazardous Waste Treatment and Disposal
562211.rationale: Permitted TSDFs (treatment/storage/disposal facilities) are scarce capital assets; Clean Harbors, Veolia, US Ecology own most Subtitle C capacity (EPA RCRAInfo). Permit moat = high incumbent multiples (10-12x EBITDA); AI marginal to chemistry/incineration ops.

562212.title: Solid Waste Landfill
562212.rationale: Top 4 (WM, RSG, WCN, Casella) own most permitted airspace; Subtitle D permitting essentially closed (NWRA). Highest EBITDA margins in 562 but scarcity drives 12-14x multiples; AI only marginally improves gas capture and compaction.

562213.title: Solid Waste Combustors and Incinerators
562213.rationale: Only 73 firms nationally (CBP); Reworld (ex-Covanta, EQT-owned) dominates WTE. Capex-intensive permitted facilities, no greenfield builds in 20+ years; not a roll-up universe.

562219.title: Other Nonhazardous Waste Treatment and Disposal
562219.rationale: Mid-fragmented (8 employees avg, 621 firms): composting, biosolids, organics processing — beneficiary of state organics-diversion mandates (CA SB 1383, NY, MA, NJ). PE entrants (Generate Capital, Closed Loop Partners) consolidating composters; AI sensor/route gains real but operational scale matters more.

562910.title: Remediation Services
562910.rationale: Highly fragmented (5,852 firms, 15.7 employees avg per CBP): environmental consulting + field remediation (Brownfields, PFAS, UST). Active PE roll-up theme — Montrose Environmental, Inframark, Apex Companies, Trinity Consultants all PE-backed; AI helps in data analytics/modeling but project execution is people-heavy.

562920.title: Materials Recovery Facilities
562920.rationale: MRFs/recyclers fragmented (1,434 firms): AI optical sorting (AMP Robotics, Greyparrot, Recycleye, Glacier) demonstrably lifts purity 10-30% and reduces labor cost. Active PE consolidation (Schupan, Closed Loop Partners, Resource Recycling Systems); EPR laws (10 US states by 2026) pull capital in.

562991.title: Septic Tank and Related Services
562991.rationale: Most fragmented in 562: 3,997 firms, 8 employees avg, owner-operated route businesses with no national consolidator. Classic HVAC-style PE roll-up: Wind Point/Sun Capital active; entry multiples 3-5x EBITDA, platform exits 8-10x. AI helps scheduling/dispatch but physical pump-truck routing limits delta.

562998.title: All Other Miscellaneous Waste Management Services
562998.rationale: Residual catch-all (1,421 firms, 12.5 employees avg): portable toilets, dumpster brokerage, junk removal franchises (1-800-GOT-JUNK, College Hunks). Junk-removal subsegment is real PE/franchise roll-up but heterogeneous bucket weakens overall thesis.

611110.title: Elementary and Secondary Schools
611110.rationale: K-12 is ~90% public/government (NCES) with charter/private remainder; not investable for PE roll-up. AI adoption blocked by procurement cycles and union contracts.

611210.title: Junior Colleges
611210.rationale: Community colleges are overwhelmingly public/non-profit (NCES IPEDS); for-profit junior college segment <5%. Title IV regs and accreditation block consolidation.

611310.title: Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools
611310.rationale: For-profit segment (Strategic Education ~$1.2B rev, Adtalem ~$1.5B) constrained by 90/10 rule and gainful employment regs; non-profit dominates (>85% IPEDS). Multiples compressed since 2010s scandals.

611410.title: Business and Secretarial Schools
611410.rationale: Tiny segment (110 firms, 11 avg size) in secular decline as Office/admin training moves online; AI assistants directly displace secretarial skill demand.

611420.title: Computer Training
611420.rationale: Highly displaced by Coursera/Udemy/Pluralsight and now LLM-native tutors (Cursor, Copilot teach in IDE); fragmented but content commoditizing fast. Roll-up risk: customer churn to free AI.

611430.title: Professional and Management Development Training
611430.rationale: Highly fragmented (7,909 firms, 7.3 avg size) corporate L&D; recurring B2B contracts attractive. AI-generated content threatens generic courseware but enterprise compliance/leadership still human-led.

611511.title: Cosmetology and Barber Schools
611511.rationale: Classic trade-school roll-up (Empire Beauty Schools ~110 campuses, Tricoci); physical hands-on skill un-displaceable by AI. State board licensing creates moat; Title IV exposure is the risk.

611512.title: Flight Training
611512.rationale: Pilot shortage drives demand; CAE Aviation Academy and ATP Flight School consolidating. FAA Part 141/61 cert is moat. AI simulator content augments but physical hours non-replaceable.

611513.title: Apprenticeship Training
611513.rationale: Mostly union/joint-trust apprenticeships (DOL Registered Apprenticeship) — limited private equity opportunity. CDL truck driving apprenticeships are the investable slice.

611519.title: Other Technical and Trade Schools
611519.rationale: Truck driving (CRST/Schneider/Roehl), HVAC, welding schools — active PE territory (CDL roll-ups multiple). Demand driven by skilled trades shortage; AI cannot replace hands-on cert.

611610.title: Fine Arts Schools
611610.rationale: Highly fragmented (15,991 firms, 7.5 avg) — music/dance/art studios. Small ticket sizes and owner-operator economics; multi-location consolidators (School of Rock) exist but slow. Generative AI threatens visual arts demand modestly.

611620.title: Sports and Recreation Instruction
611620.rationale: 19,658 firms, 8.1 avg — gymnastics/swim/tennis/golf instruction. Physical un-replaceable by AI. Multi-unit roll-ups in swim (British Swim School, Goldfish) and gymnastics active.

611630.title: Language Schools
611630.rationale: Most exposed sub-sector — Duolingo (74M MAU, $748M rev 2024), Babbel, and ChatGPT voice mode directly replace conversational tutors. Berlitz/Inlingua under pressure; multiples deteriorating.

611691.title: Exam Preparation and Tutoring
611691.rationale: Highest AI displacement: Khan Academy Khanmigo (1M+ users), ChatGPT free tutoring, Magic School AI displacing Sylvan/Kumon/Mathnasium economics. Test-optional movement at colleges compounds pressure.

611692.title: Automobile Driving Schools
611692.rationale: Extremely fragmented (2,585 firms, 6.1 avg), state-DMV mandated. Physical road hours non-replaceable; classroom portion increasingly online. Roll-up viable (DriversEd.com, A1 Driving Schools).

611699.title: All Other Miscellaneous Schools and Instruction
611699.rationale: Catch-all (CPR, survival, cooking, public speaking) — too heterogeneous for systematic roll-up. Individual sub-segments may work but no clear thesis at NAICS level.

611710.title: Educational Support Services
611710.rationale: Includes ed consulting, testing services, student services. AI displaces grading/assessment workflows (Gradescope, MagicSchool) but K-12/HE admin services (admissions, IEPs) still labor-heavy. Fragmented and PE-active.

621111.title: Offices of Physicians (except Mental Health Specialists)
621111.rationale: 197K firms with avg 13 employees = highly fragmented; 79 PE deals in Q1 2025 with dermatology/cardiology/ophthalmology specialties at 6-12x EBITDA vs primary care 3-5x; back-office synergies yield 200-300 bps margin lift. AI replaces meaningful admin overhead (RCM, scheduling, scribing) but clinical labor cap remains.

621112.title: Offices of Physicians, Mental Health Specialists
621112.rationale: Tiny shops (avg 4.7 employees, 13K firms) ideal for roll-up; AI scribes/intake reduce 40%+ admin burden specific to psych workflows; telepsych infrastructure mature post-COVID. Smaller M arbitrage than physicians broadly since psychiatry buyer pool narrower than primary care.

621210.title: Offices of Dentists
621210.rationale: Heavily fragmented (136K firms) and DSO consolidation active at 5-8x single-site to 8-11x platform; clinical labor (hygienists, dentists) dominates cost stack so AI savings limited to scheduling/RCM/imaging review. OMS/ortho specialty subsegments command 11-15x EBITDA premiums.

621310.title: Offices of Chiropractors
621310.rationale: 40K firms avg 3.7 employees - extreme fragmentation but standalone multiples low at 2.86-3.83x EBITDA; cash-pay model limits payor synergies; physical adjustments are hands-on so V is low. Roll-up multiple arbitrage exists (8x at scale) but few sophisticated platforms vs other verticals.

621320.title: Offices of Optometrists
621320.rationale: MyEyeDr (Goldman) 900+ offices + AEG Vision/EyeCare Partners already consolidating; single-OD multiples 2.5-4x SDE vs scaled platforms 7-10x EBITDA = strong M arbitrage. AI retinal imaging emerging but clinical exam labor cap limits V.

621330.title: Offices of Mental Health Practitioners (except Physicians)
621330.rationale: 40K firms avg 5.4 employees = very fragmented (LCSWs, psychologists, LMFTs); telehealth/AI documentation dramatically lowers admin burden; small platforms 4-8x EBITDA vs scaled 10-14x outpatient mental health = real arbitrage. Headway/SonderMind/Talkiatry validate model.

621340.title: Offices of Physical, Occupational and Speech Therapists, and Audiologists
621340.rationale: USPH (775+ clinics, 37 add-ons, 17.5% EBITDA margin), ATI taken private at 11.2x EBITDA, Athletico active = mature consolidator landscape; hands-on therapy is the cost = low V. Multiple arbitrage real (median 2.33x small vs 11.2x platform).

621391.title: Offices of Podiatrists
621391.rationale: 7.5K firms avg 4.9 employees - small specialty with limited dedicated PE platforms compared to derm/optometry; ancillary revenue (custom orthotics, wound care) creates margin variability. Standalone multiples ~5-7x; few scaled exits to anchor M arbitrage.

621399.title: Offices of All Other Miscellaneous Health Practitioners
621399.rationale: Catch-all (naturopaths, dietitians, acupuncturists, midwives) - 33K firms avg 3.7 emp, mostly cash-pay micro practices; thesis weak because no dominant playbook and limited scale buyers. Heterogeneous group reduces consolidation thesis.

621410.title: Family Planning Centers
621410.rationale: 2.7K firms dominated by non-profit Planned Parenthood (700+ centers, $2B revenue, 40% govt-funded) + community health affiliates; not a PE roll-up market due to non-profit structure and political/regulatory exposure post-Dobbs. M arbitrage near-zero.

621420.title: Outpatient Mental Health and Substance Abuse Centers
621420.rationale: 16K firms avg 19 emp - active PE roll-ups (Refresh Mental Health, Discovery Behavioral, Acadia OP) at 6-12x EBITDA platform vs 3-8x single facility; residential SUD programs hit 20-35% EBITDA margins. AI documentation/intake yields meaningful admin savings.

621491.title: HMO Medical Centers
621491.rationale: Dominated by Kaiser Permanente (~$128B revenue, 40 hospitals, 610 medical offices) staff-model integrated system + a handful of regional integrated providers; near-zero fragmentation, no PE roll-up path. Capitated model already vertically integrated.

621492.title: Kidney Dialysis Centers
621492.rationale: Already a duopoly: DaVita ~37.8% + Fresenius = 71.7% combined market share, independent share dropped from 20% to 10.6%; roll-up thesis dead - no fragmentation to capture and FTC scrutiny on further consolidation. Medicare price-controlled.

621493.title: Freestanding Ambulatory Surgical and Emergency Centers
621493.rationale: USPI (38.6% EBITDA margin, 12.1% YoY growth) + SCA Health + Surgery Partners active acquirers; PE buys at 7-8x then aggregates to 8-12x platform with 14-18x compound; ASC volume migration from hospitals is strongest tailwind in 621. Highly attractive M arbitrage.

621498.title: All Other Outpatient Care Centers
621498.rationale: Includes urgent care (CityMD, GoHealth, FastMed PE-backed) - single sites 3-5x EBITDA vs 30+ center platforms 11-15x EBITDA = strong arbitrage; 21K firms avg 23 emp. Diverse subcategory (urgent care + infusion + dialysis-adjacent) drives moderate scores.

621511.title: Medical Laboratories
621511.rationale: Duopoly Quest ($9.87B) + LabCorp ($13.9B) ~25% combined market share with rest fragmented among hospital outreach and regional independents; consolidation is concentrated buyer-side (Quest/LabCorp spent $20B+ in 20 yrs) limiting independent platform path. AI in pathology/genomics validates V but FDA/CLIA gates A.

621512.title: Diagnostic Imaging Centers
621512.rationale: RadNet (418 centers, 16.2% EBITDA margin) acquires at 4-7x EBITDA then 'RadNet-izes' - clear roll-up template; AI radiology reads (FDA-approved) directly displace highest-cost clinical labor. Medicare price erosion is the offsetting headwind.

621610.title: Home Health Care Services
621610.rationale: Addus, BrightSpring, Help at Home active consolidators; Medicare home health at 4.5-8x EBITDA, hospice premium 6-12x; thin margins (4% mid-market) and Medicare HHVBP penalties = high operating risk. Field caregiver labor is the cost stack so V remains low.

621910.title: Ambulance Services
621910.rationale: GMR (KKR-backed AMR + Air Medical) cut IPO valuation from $5B to $3.3B in May 2026 - signals PE roll-up fatigue; 4.1x net debt/EBITDA, paramedic labor shortage, stagnant reimbursement. Municipal/911 contracts limit fragmentation play.

621991.title: Blood and Organ Banks
621991.rationale: Top 50 = 84% of $16.3B industry; American Red Cross alone supplies 35% of US blood; 71% of revenue is non-profit. No PE roll-up path due to non-profit dominance and FDA/AABB regulatory moat. Tissue/plasma subsegments are for-profit but already consolidated (Grifols, CSL, Octapharma).

621999.title: All Other Miscellaneous Ambulatory Health Care Services
621999.rationale: Catch-all bucket (blood pressure clinics, hearing testing, infusion services not elsewhere classified) - 9K heterogeneous firms; no dominant thesis, limited specialty-level intel makes consolidation play hard to underwrite at scale.

622110.title: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals
622110.rationale: Highly consolidated: top 10 systems (HCA, CommonSpirit, Ascension, Trinity, Kaiser, Tenet, UHS, CHS, Providence, AdventHealth) control ~30%+ of beds per AHA 2024; 67% of community hospitals are part of a system. Clinical core (surgery, ICU, ER) is physical/regulated — low AI replaceability beyond back-office (revenue cycle, coding, scheduling). Multiples 8-10x EBITDA for non-profit acquirers (per Kaufman Hall 2024), no real PE arbitrage at scale.
sources: Kaufman Hall M&A Quarterly Activity Report Q4 2024

622210.title: Psychiatric and Substance Abuse Hospitals
622210.rationale: More fragmented than general hospitals (~800 firms vs 6,000): UHS (Behavioral Health division ~$6B), Acadia Healthcare (~$3B), Universal Behavioral compete with hundreds of regional/standalone facilities; ~50% market share for top 5 per Acadia 10-K 2024. PE active (Bain, KKR backed Acadia historically; Webster Equity, Trinity Hunt in addictions). Clinical labor (psychiatrists, therapists) hard to automate; 15-18% EBITDA margins, 9-12x multiples per Provident M&A 2024 reports.
sources: Provident Healthcare Partners Behavioral Health M&A Report 2024

622310.title: Specialty (except Psychiatric and Substance Abuse) Hospitals
622310.rationale: Includes LTACH, IRF, children's, cancer, cardiac specialty hospitals. Select Medical (LTACH/IRF leader), Encompass Health (IRF, ~140 hospitals post-HHC spin), Kindred (LTACH) anchor consolidated segments but children's/cancer remain academic/non-profit. ~860 firms with mid-size avg (300 emp) supports roll-up in IRF/cardiac niches; 9-11x multiples per VMG Health 2024. CMS site-neutral payment risk caps margins.
sources: CMS LTCH/IRF PPS Final Rule FY2025

623110.title: Nursing Care Facilities (Skilled Nursing Facilities)
623110.rationale: Heavy PE involvement (Genesis HC, Consulate, ManorCare) but CMS minimum-staffing mandate (April 2024 final rule: 3.48 HPRD + 24/7 RN) + Medicaid reimbursement pressure crushed margins; Genesis bankruptcy 2021, Brookdale exited SNF segment. Ownership transparency rules (CMS 2023) increase PE scrutiny.

623210.title: Residential Intellectual and Developmental Disability Facilities
623210.rationale: Highly fragmented (35,807 firms, avg 15 employees) and nonprofit-heavy (Easterseals, ARC affiliates); Medicaid HCBS waiver dependency limits pricing power. Some PE consolidation (Sevita/Centerbridge, BrightSpring) but state-by-state licensing creates roll-up friction.
sources: ANCOR State of America's Direct Support Workforce

623220.title: Residential Mental Health and Substance Abuse Facilities
623220.rationale: Active PE consolidation (Acadia, Universal Health, BayMark, Discovery Behavioral) driven by opioid crisis funding + parity laws; commercial-pay SUD residential commands 15-20% margins. But scandal risk (PE-owned addiction centers under FTC/state AG scrutiny 2023-2025).

623311.title: Continuing Care Retirement Communities
623311.rationale: Already consolidated by nonprofit systems (Erickson, Acts, LifeSpire, Presbyterian Homes); capital-intensive entrance-fee model + actuarial risk deters PE. Multiple Type-A CCRC bankruptcies 2020-2024 (Presbyterian Senior Living asset sales). REIT-adjacent (Welltower, Ventas) but not pure roll-up territory.

623312.title: Assisted Living Facilities for the Elderly
623312.rationale: Prime roll-up target: 21,352 firms, demographic tailwind (75+ pop +50% by 2035), state-level licensing only (no CMS), RevPAR recovered post-COVID to ~$5,400 (NIC Q4 2024). Active operators: Atria/Welltower JV, Sunrise/Revera, Discovery Senior Living, Watermark — plus REIT capital (Ventas, Welltower, Healthpeak).

623990.title: Other Residential Care Facilities
623990.rationale: Catch-all (group homes for at-risk youth, halfway houses, orphanages, refugee shelters) heavily nonprofit + government-contracted; GEO Group/CoreCivic dominate adult halfway-house segment but juvenile/youth residential PE involvement is reputation-risky (Sequel Youth Services shutdowns 2021-2023). Fragmented but not investable.
sources: APSAC reports on youth residential abuse

624110.title: Child and Youth Services
624110.rationale: Predominantly nonprofit/grant-funded (per IRS 990 ACL data ~70%+ are 501(c)(3)); foster care, adoption, youth mentoring. Heavy state Medicaid/Title IV-E reimbursement caps margins and PE multiples; AI cannot replace caseworker contact hours.

624120.title: Services for the Elderly and Persons with Disabilities
624120.rationale: Non-medical home care/personal care: 41K firms, avg 32 employees, highly fragmented. Active PE roll-up (Honor, Help at Home, Addus); 5-7x EBITDA for sub-$5M, 9-11x for scaled platforms. Labor-intensive (caregiver visits) limits V; AI helps scheduling/scribing/family comms.

624190.title: Other Individual and Family Services
624190.rationale: Catch-all: family counseling, marriage counseling, crisis intervention, self-help. ~75% nonprofit per IRS 990; grant/donation funded with thin surplus. PE multiples poor due to mission-locked entities and reimbursement complexity.

624210.title: Community Food Services
624210.rationale: Food banks, soup kitchens, meal delivery (Meals on Wheels affiliates): ~95% nonprofit per IRS 990 BMF. Donation/USDA TEFAP funded, no equity ownership possible at scale. Not investable as roll-up.

624221.title: Temporary Shelters
624221.rationale: Homeless/DV/runaway shelters: near-100% nonprofit (HUD CoC grants, state DV funds). No equity vehicle; PE roll-up structurally impossible. AI use limited to case management software.

624229.title: Other Community Housing Services
624229.rationale: Transitional housing, halfway houses, sober living. Mix: sober-living can be for-profit (some small PE interest, e.g., Discovery Behavioral), but most transitional is nonprofit/HUD-funded. Limited multiple arbitrage; reimbursement risk.

624230.title: Emergency and Other Relief Services
624230.rationale: Red Cross, Salvation Army disaster relief: dominated by 3-4 mega-nonprofits + FEMA-funded local chapters. Not fragmented in investable sense; no PE play.

624310.title: Vocational Rehabilitation Services
624310.rationale: Sheltered workshops, job coaching for disabilities (Goodwill, Easterseals dominate). State VR contracts + Medicaid waivers; ~80% nonprofit per IRS 990. AbilityOne federal contracting limits for-profit entry. PE has tried (e.g., Sevita parent) but mostly via I/DD adjacency (NAICS 623).

624410.title: Child Care Services
624410.rationale: Most investable in 624: KinderCare ($2.5B IPO 2024), Bright Horizons ($BFAM), Learning Care Group, Primrose franchise are for-profit. 80K firms, avg 12 employees = real fragmentation. State subsidy-dependent (CCDBG) but tuition-driven. EBITDA 8-12%, multiples 6-9x; AI for enrollment/billing/family comms (Procare, Brightwheel).

711110.title: Theater Companies and Dinner Theaters
711110.rationale: Theater is live human craft with low AI replaceability; ~75% of US theaters are nonprofit (TCG data) making PE roll-up structurally incompatible. Margins thin (5-8%), multiples 3-4x for for-profit dinner theaters.

711120.title: Dance Companies
711120.rationale: Dance companies are >90% nonprofit (Dance/USA, NEA data) with subsidy-dependent economics; AI applies only marginally to choreography/marketing. No PE roll-up precedent identified.

711130.title: Musical Groups and Artists
711130.rationale: Highly fragmented (avg 7 employees, 5,477 firms) but each entity is artist-IP centric — PE buys catalogs (Hipgnosis, Primary Wave, KKR/BMG) not operating companies. AI generative music threatens session/library segment but multiples paid for catalog rights, not roll-up of acts.
sources: Goldman Sachs Music in the Air 2024

711190.title: Other Performing Arts Companies
711190.rationale: Small bucket (431 firms) of circus/magic/variety; mixed nonprofit and for-profit. Cirque du Soleil restructure (TPG/Caisse) shows fragility of live-arts LBOs. Limited roll-up logic.

711211.title: Sports Teams and Clubs
711211.rationale: Scarcity assets: franchise rights gated by leagues (NBA/NFL/MLB/NHL) — recent PE rule changes (Arctos, Sixth Street, Dyal HomeCourt) allow LP stakes capped <30%, not control roll-up. Valuations 6-12x revenue not EBITDA; not a roll-up vertical.

711212.title: Racetracks
711212.rationale: Consolidated to public operators (Churchill Downs, Penn Entertainment, NYRA) and gaming-licensed venues; remaining 562 firms include small fair/county tracks with structural decline (NTRA handle data). Gaming licenses are the real asset, not the racing.

711219.title: Other Spectator Sports
711219.rationale: Fragmented (3,100 firms, avg 5.3 employees): independent athletes, motor-sports, rodeo, minor league. Mostly sole-prop with no roll-up economics; PE focus has been pickleball/PPA/UPA league formation rather than club aggregation.

711310.title: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events with Facilities
711310.rationale: Live Nation/AEG duopoly controls ~70% of top US venues by ticket volume (DOJ 2024 complaint); Oak View Group consolidating mid-market arenas. Independent venues (NIVA membership ~3,000) fragmented but real-estate heavy — buyout multiples 7-9x EBITDA.
sources: DOJ v. Live Nation complaint May 2024

711320.title: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events without Facilities
711320.rationale: Fragmented (4,942 firms, avg 7.3 employees) independent promoters squeezed by Live Nation's exclusive artist deals; AI useful for marketing/ticketing analytics. Low capex but margin compression and platform risk limit roll-up appeal.

711410.title: Agents and Managers for Artists, Athletes, Entertainers, and Other Public Figures
711410.rationale: Big 4 agencies (CAA/Silver Lake, WME/Endeavor/Silver Lake take-private 2024, UTA/EQT-Investcorp, ICM merged) already PE-rolled at top; mid-market boutiques (4,878 firms) fragmented and asset-light with 18-25% margins. AI threatens junior agent work (deal screening, contract drafting).

711510.title: Independent Artists, Writers, and Performers
711510.rationale: 38,064 firms, avg 1.6 employees = essentially sole proprietors; generative AI directly replaces commercial illustration/copywriting/voiceover (BLS/AIGA 2024 freelance income reports show -20% in affected segments). Zero roll-up logic: no operating leverage, IP belongs to individuals.

712110.title: Museums
712110.rationale: IMLS counts ~33K museums; ~95% nonprofit/government. CBP 5,442 'firms' overstates investable universe (includes nonprofit EINs). No equity, no acquirable cash flow. AI helps with donor CRM and collections OCR but accrues to SaaS vendors, not operators. Not PE-investable.

712120.title: Historical Sites
712120.rationale: Almost entirely National Park Service, state parks, historical societies, and local 501(c)(3) trusts. 1,122 CBP firms mostly nonprofit. No private ownership to consolidate. AI uplift negligible at site level. M=0.

712130.title: Zoos and Botanical Gardens
712130.rationale: AZA-accredited 238 facilities; ~95% nonprofit or municipal. Higher avg employment (46.8) reflects scale but governance blocks acquisition. Some private safari/roadside zoos exist (Tiger King tier) but uninvestable for institutional PE. AI helps with visitor analytics and animal welfare monitoring, vendor-captured.

712190.title: Nature Parks and Other Similar Institutions
712190.rationale: Nature preserves, conservation areas, caverns — dominated by Nature Conservancy, Audubon, land trusts, state agencies. 745 CBP firms ~90%+ nonprofit. No roll-up thesis. AI negligible.

713110.title: Amusement and Theme Parks
713110.rationale: Highly consolidated: Six Flags-Cedar Fair merger (2024) + Disney + Universal + SeaWorld + Herschend control >80% of US gated attendance per IAAPA. Capex-heavy fixed assets (coasters), AI limited to dynamic pricing and ride-throughput optimization. No fragmentation arbitrage left.

713120.title: Amusement Arcades
713120.rationale: 3,392 firms avg 18 employees = fragmented, but Dave&Busters and Round1 consolidate the eatertainment premium tier (SEC: PLAY $2.2B revenue). Family entertainment centers (FECs) the active roll-up zone — AI relevant for redemption analytics and prize-cost optimization.

713210.title: Casinos (except Casino Hotels)
713210.rationale: Fully consolidated: MGM, Caesars, Boyd, PENN, Wynn, Bally's own >75% US commercial GGR per AGA State of the States 2024 ($66.5B). Heavy state licensing moats prevent roll-up entry; AI advanced in player-tracking/CRM (Caesars Rewards, MGM Rewards) but captured by incumbents.

713290.title: Other Gambling Industries
713290.rationale: Mix of tribal bingo halls, lotteries, sportsbook operators, route operators (Golden Entertainment, Accel). Online sports betting consolidated (DKNG/FLUT/MGM), but bingo/parlour ops fragmented across 3,069 firms. Regulatory complexity limits PE roll-up appetite outside route gaming.

713910.title: Golf Courses and Country Clubs
713910.rationale: 10,076 firms, avg 30 emp — top 5 operators (Troon 700+ managed, Invited/ClubCorp 200+, Arcis 70+, KemperSports, Century Golf) control <15% of US courses per NGF 2024. Active PE: Apollo (Arcis), Atairos (Invited), L Catterton. AI emerging in dynamic tee-time pricing (Lightspeed, Sagacity) and turf management — early stage.

713920.title: Skiing Facilities
713920.rationale: Consolidated by Vail Resorts (42 resorts, MTN 10-K $2.9B rev) and Alterra (18 resorts, Ikon Pass). Pass-based subscription model already extracted value; AI used for snowmaking optimization and crowd flow — Vail captures gains. Independent hills sub-scale and weather-exposed.
sources: NSAA Kottke National End of Season Report 2024

713930.title: Marinas
713930.rationale: 3,766 firms avg 8 emp — actively rolled up by Suntex, Safe Harbor (Sun Communities subsidiary, $836M segment rev 2023 10-K), Westrec, Legendary Capital. Real-estate-heavy economics drive PE/REIT interest; multiple arbitrage strong (single-marina 4-6x → platform 10-12x). AI mostly basic slip-management software.
sources: NMMA US Recreational Boating Statistical Abstract 2024

713940.title: Fitness and Recreational Sports Centers
713940.rationale: 40,786 firms — most fragmented in 713. Big chains exist (Planet Fitness 2,600+ PLNT 10-K, LA Fitness, Equinox, Crunch, Orangetheory) but boutique/independent segment huge. Active PE: TSG (Planet Fitness early), L Catterton (Equinox, Pure Barre), Roark (Orangetheory). AI delta strongest in 713 — programming, member churn prediction, virtual coaching (Future, Tonal).

713950.title: Bowling Centers
713950.rationale: Bowlero (BOWL) controls ~360 centers post-AMF/Bowlmor/Lucky Strike consolidation (10-K 2024 $1.15B rev), ~11% of 3,305 US centers but dominant in metro premium tier. Independent suburban centers remain but face declining bowling participation (BPAA -15% since 2019). Roll-up window largely closed at top.

713990.title: All Other Amusement and Recreation Industries
713990.rationale: 21,689 firms avg 9 emp — catch-all bucket: trampoline parks (Sky Zone/CircusTrix consolidated by Palladium), escape rooms, axe throwing, indoor playgrounds, recreational guides, climbing gyms (El Cap, Movement Climbing rolled by EagleTree). Sub-vertical specialization required; AI mostly booking/CRM optimization.

721110.title: Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels
721110.rationale: Brand-franchise consolidated: Marriott/Hilton/IHG/Hyatt/Choice/Wyndham control ~70% of US branded rooms via franchise (STR 2024); independent motels fragmented but capital-intensive real estate plays, not platform roll-ups. AI delta limited to revenue management (IDeaS, Duetto) and labor scheduling - already adopted at brand level.

721120.title: Casino Hotels
721120.rationale: Heavily consolidated (MGM, Caesars, Wynn, Las Vegas Sands, Boyd) with ~500 firms but 348K employees - avg 696/firm. Gaming license barriers + capital intensity make sub-$50M PE roll-up infeasible. AI use confined to player loyalty/CRM at enterprise level.

721191.title: Bed-and-Breakfast Inns
721191.rationale: Micro-fragmented (2,464 firms, 4.9 avg employees) but uninvestable: owner-operator lifestyle businesses, sub-$300K revenue, heritage property constraints, no platform synergy. AirDNA/OTA channel mgmt already commoditized.

721199.title: All Other Traveler Accommodation
721199.rationale: Catch-all (hostels, guest ranches, tourist homes) - 1,897 firms at 4.5 employees - sub-scale and idiosyncratic. No comparable PE roll-up precedent vs short-term rental platforms (Vacasa/Evolve) which struggled with unit economics.

721211.title: RV (Recreational Vehicle) Parks and Campgrounds
721211.rationale: Active PE roll-up thesis: Sun Communities (SUI) MH/RV portfolio, KOA franchise (500+ parks), Sand Hospitality, Blue Water, Northgate Resorts, Spot Hospitality all scaling. Real estate + recurring seasonal income, 35-45% EBITDA margins per Sun Communities 10-K. AI uplift in dynamic pricing (Campspot, RoverPass) and ops.

721214.title: Recreational and Vacation Camps (except Campgrounds)
721214.rationale: Summer camps + retreat centers - seasonal (8-12 weeks revenue), staffing-heavy, ACA accreditation barriers. Some PE interest (KKR's Camp Bow Wow adjacency, Cradlepoint) but mostly nonprofit/family-owned. AI limited to enrollment CRM.

721310.title: Rooming and Boarding Houses, Dormitories, and Workers' Camps
721310.rationale: Niche segment with PE activity in workforce housing (Target Hospitality TH for energy/government, Civeo CVEO mining/oilfield) - long-term contracts, 25-30% EBITDA. Student housing dorms separately consolidated (American Campus Communities/Blackstone). Co-living (Common, Bungalow) flamed out.

722310.title: Food Service Contractors
722310.rationale: Heavily consolidated by Compass Group, Sodexo, Aramark (>60% combined institutional share per Technomic 2024). Contract-based, low-margin (~5% EBITDA), labor-intensive; PE roll-up window largely closed at scale, only regional niches remain.

722320.title: Caterers
722320.rationale: Highly fragmented (~13K firms, avg 10 employees per Census CBP) but event-driven volatility and low repeat-revenue make multiple arbitrage weak (3-5x EBITDA typical per BizBuySell 2024). AI limited to booking/menu optimization.

722330.title: Mobile Food Services
722330.rationale: Sub-scale (avg 3.2 employees, ~$290K revenue per IBISWorld 2024); owner-operator culture, no PE roll-up precedent at scale. Food trucks resist standardization; brand value tied to individual chef.

722410.title: Drinking Places (Alcoholic Beverages)
722410.rationale: Fragmented (~40K firms) but local-license/zoning barriers, hyper-local brand equity and post-COVID structural decline (-12% units 2019-2024 per BLS QCEW) make roll-ups unattractive; no major PE platform exists.

722511.title: Full-Service Restaurants
722511.rationale: Massive fragmentation (~257K firms) but ~60% 5-year failure rate (BLS BED) + 4-6% EBITDA margins (Darden/Brinker/Bloomin' 10-Ks 2024) + locality-bound brand make roll-up structurally hard outside franchise model. Public chains trade 8-12x EBITDA but private acquisitions 3-5x.

722513.title: Limited-Service Restaurants
722513.rationale: Dominated by franchisor consolidation (McDonald's, Yum, Restaurant Brands); roll-up plays exist via multi-unit franchisee aggregation (Flynn Group ~2,600 units, Sun Holdings ~1,500). AI gains in drive-thru voice ordering (Yum/IBM, Wendy's FreshAI). Higher store-level EBITDA but unit economics weakening.

722514.title: Cafeterias, Grill Buffets, and Buffets
722514.rationale: Structurally declining category (~4.8K firms, down from 7K in 2012 per Census CBP); COVID accelerated death (Souplantation/Furr's closures). Negative multiple arbitrage, no PE interest.

722515.title: Snack and Nonalcoholic Beverage Bars
722515.rationale: Best LSR sub-category for roll-up: coffee/juice/smoothie units have 14-18% store EBITDA (Starbucks 10-K FY2024, Dutch Bros S-1) and franchise multi-unit aggregation precedent (Scooter's, 7 Brew PE-backed). Higher repeat-visit frequency than full-service.

811111.title: General Automotive Repair
811111.rationale: Driven Brands (NYSE:DRVN) Take 5/Meineke roll-up actively consolidating ~84K firms avg 4.6 employees; diagnostics partially AI-augmentable but hands-on labor dominates. Multiples 4-7x EBITDA for independents vs Driven Brands ~10x.

811114.title: Specialized Automotive Repair
811114.rationale: Transmission/brake specialty shops (AAMCO, Midas) fragmented but smaller universe than general repair; PE interest exists via Driven Brands platform. Diagnostics AI helps but physical labor anchors margins.

811121.title: Automotive Body, Paint, and Interior Repair and Maintenance
811121.rationale: Caliber Collision (Hellman & Friedman), Boyd Group (TSX:BYD), Gerber/Service King control ~30%+ MSO share; ~34K firms with sub-7 avg employment remain consolidating target. AI used in estimating (CCC, Mitchell) and insurance workflow. Top platforms trade 12-16x EBITDA.

811122.title: Automotive Glass Replacement Shops
811122.rationale: Safelite (Belron) dominates ~70% via insurance network model; ADAS recalibration creates technical moat and growth tailwind. Roll-up opportunity narrowing but tuck-ins remain at 6-9x EBITDA.

811191.title: Automotive Oil Change and Lubrication Shops
811191.rationale: Take 5 Oil Change (Driven Brands), Valvoline Instant Oil (Saudi Aramco/Stonebridge sale 2022 $2.65B), Jiffy Lube (Shell) driving aggressive PE-backed consolidation; quick-lube unit economics excellent. Platforms 12-15x EBITDA, independents 5-7x.

811192.title: Car Washes
811192.rationale: Express tunnel washes (Mister, Driven Brands' Take 5 Car Wash, ZIPS, Whistle Express, Tommy's) backed by ~$15B+ PE since 2019 (Atlantic Street, Wind Point, Freeman Spogli); high-cap-ex but ~30% EBITDA margins. ML for membership churn, license plate recognition mature. Platforms 12-16x; subscale 6-8x.

811198.title: All Other Automotive Repair and Maintenance
811198.rationale: Residual category (tire/wheel align, undercoating, rustproofing) heterogeneous; ~5K firms, small. Some PE in tire (Mavis, Discount Tire) but classified mostly under 441 retail. Limited platform thesis here.

811210.title: Electronic and Precision Equipment Repair and Maintenance
811210.rationale: Spans medical device, semiconductor tool repair, computer repair; high B2B technical complexity. AI diagnostics + remote troubleshooting (TeamViewer, ServiceMax) replacing tier-1 triage. Some PE in medical device service (Crothall, Agiliti) at 9-12x; consumer electronics declining (right-to-repair pressure).

811310.title: Commercial and Industrial Machinery and Equipment (except Automotive and Electronic) Repair and Maintenance
811310.rationale: ~22K firms avg 9.3 employees; covers industrial pumps, compressors, hydraulics. Predictive maintenance (IoT sensors, ML) reshaping service contracts. PE active via Sun Capital, Audax (e.g., Total Industrial Services, Motion Industries/Genuine Parts). Multiples 7-10x for $10M+ EBITDA platforms.

811411.title: Home and Garden Equipment Repair and Maintenance
811411.rationale: Tiny ~1.7K firms, 2.7 avg employees, mower/blower repair declining as throwaway models proliferate. No PE platform interest documented. Local moms-and-pops, low multiples 2-4x SDE.

811412.title: Appliance Repair and Maintenance
811412.rationale: ~5.4K firms avg 3.7 employees, highly local. Sears Home Services decline left vacuum; PE-backed Puls/HomeServe (Brookfield) entering on-demand model with AI dispatch/diagnostic chatbots. Roll-up viable but unit economics thin; multiples 3-6x SDE.

811420.title: Reupholstery and Furniture Repair
811420.rationale: Craft trade ~3.3K firms 3.4 avg employees; manual skilled labor not AI-substitutable. No PE platform exists. Multiples 2-3x SDE.

811430.title: Footwear and Leather Goods Repair
811430.rationale: Dying trade ~720 firms 2.4 avg employees; declining shoe repair shops nationwide per SSIA. No PE thesis. Pure micro-business territory, 1-2x SDE.

811490.title: Other Personal and Household Goods Repair and Maintenance
811490.rationale: Residual ~11K firms (bicycles, watches, musical instruments, boats per old definition pre-revision); fragmented but heterogeneous limits roll-up. Boat/marine repair has some PE interest (Marine Products, Suntex) at 7-9x; otherwise small.

812111.title: Barber Shops
812111.rationale: Physical haircut service, AI replaceability near-zero. 7,363 firms avg 3.9 emp = extreme micro-fragmentation; chair-rental model with no SDE pool. Franchises (Sport Clips, Great Clips) dominate roll-up activity but PE multiples 3-5x SDE on sub-$1M EBITDA — no real arbitrage.

812112.title: Beauty Salons
812112.rationale: 82,964 firms avg 4.7 emp — most fragmented sub-industry in 812; booth-rental model means most 'salons' are 1-2 stylist gigs not acquirable assets. AI booking/marketing (Vagaro, Boulevard) is point-solution not transformative. No institutional PE roll-up has succeeded at scale.

812113.title: Nail Salons
812113.rationale: 33,802 firms avg 4.3 emp; cash-heavy ethnic-owned (Vietnamese ~80% per Nails Magazine) with informal labor — diligence nightmare for PE. No meaningful AI lever; physical service immune to automation. Zero institutional consolidation precedent.

812191.title: Diet and Weight Reducing Centers
812191.rationale: 2,513 firms but franchise-dominated (Jenny Craig bankruptcy 2023, WW International struggling) — category disrupted by GLP-1s (Ozempic, Wegovy) not AI roll-ups. Telehealth platforms (Ro, Hims) capturing share. Legacy brick-and-mortar in secular decline.

812199.title: Other Personal Care Services
812199.rationale: Catch-all (tattoo, tanning, massage, ear piercing) — 29,936 firms avg 5.7 emp; heterogeneous so no single PE thesis. MedSpa subset attracting PE (Skin Spirit, LaserAway) but that's in NAICS 6212/6211, not here. Pure 812199 too fragmented.

812210.title: Funeral Homes and Funeral Services
812210.rationale: 15,375 firms; Service Corp Intl (SCI) ~16% share, Carriage Services ~2% — top 5 only ~25% (NFDA 2024). Classic PE roll-up: stable cash flow, recession-proof, 6-9x EBITDA at scale vs 3-5x for singles. Demographic tailwind (boomer mortality) but cremation rate 60%+ compressing AOV. AI marginal (digital obituaries, scheduling).

812220.title: Cemeteries and Crematories
812220.rationale: 5,117 firms; SCI/StoneMor consolidated ~15-20%. Pre-need trust accounting + perpetual care trust = capital-intensive but predictable. Real estate + recurring care fees attractive to PE (7-10x EBITDA). AI lever minimal — operations are land/inventory not knowledge work.

812310.title: Coin-Operated Laundries and Drycleaners
812310.rationale: 10,911 firms avg 3.8 emp — owner-operator dominant; high margins from utilities arbitrage but ceiling on growth (real estate bound). Some PE interest (Wash Multifamily Laundry Systems via EQT) but mostly route-based not retail. AI lever = card payment/remote monitoring (CSC ServiceWorks) — already deployed.

812320.title: Drycleaning and Laundry Services (except Coin-Operated)
812320.rationale: 16,394 firms in secular decline (-30% since 2005 per DLI) — casualization of office wear post-COVID killed volume. Tide Cleaners franchise PE-backed but limited growth. Route consolidation and POS automation marginal. Avoid.

812331.title: Linen Supply
812331.rationale: Only 839 firms avg 54.8 emp — already consolidated (Cintas, Aramark, Vestis ~60%+). Healthcare/hospitality linen B2B with route density moat. PE buys regionals at 6-8x EBITDA. AI lever in route optimization + RFID inventory (UNX-Christeyns). Limited remaining targets.

812332.title: Industrial Launderers
812332.rationale: 1,488 firms avg 45.5 emp — uniform rental dominated by Cintas (~40%), Vestis, UniFirst (~15% combined remaining). Stable recurring B2B contracts. Few mid-market targets left; PE plays mostly tuck-ins for strategics at 7-9x EBITDA. AI in route + garment lifecycle tracking.

812910.title: Pet Care (except Veterinary) Services
812910.rationale: 24,267 firms avg 6.5 emp — fragmented grooming/boarding/daycare. PE-active: Camp Bow Wow (VCA), Dogtopia, Hounds Town franchising; Mars/Petsmart buying premium daycare. Humanization trend = pricing power. AI lever in scheduling, demand forecasting, computer-vision health monitoring (Companion Labs). 5-7x EBITDA singles, 9-11x for 10+ unit platforms.

812921.title: Photofinishing Laboratories (except One-Hour)
812921.rationale: 357 firms — dying category; commercial/wedding/professional photo printing largely digitized. Shutterfly/Snapfish dominate consumer online. AI lever exists (auto-enhance, layout) but TAM shrinking. Not a roll-up thesis.

812922.title: One-Hour Photofinishing
812922.rationale: 98 firms total — essentially extinct category killed by smartphones. Walgreens/CVS kiosks captured residual. No investable opportunity.

812930.title: Parking Lots and Garages
812930.rationale: 11,944 firms; SP Plus (acquired by Metropolis 2024 for $1.5B), LAZ Parking, ABM dominate operating. AI computer-vision (Metropolis 'checkout-free' parking) is genuine disruption — replaces gate hardware + attendants. PE/strategic active; 8-12x EBITDA for tech-enabled operators. Real estate ownership separate (REITs).

812990.title: All Other Personal Services
812990.rationale: Catch-all (shoe shine, dating services, personal shoppers, psychics, etc.) — 22,597 firms avg 3.4 emp; heterogeneity precludes coherent thesis. Skip.

813110.title: Religious Organizations
813110.rationale: 501(c)(3) churches and houses of worship — NOT PE-investable (nonprofit, no equity, IRS prohibits private inurement). ~380K congregations per Faith Communities Today; donations/tithes not profits.

813211.title: Grantmaking Foundations
813211.rationale: Private/community foundations — 501(c)(3) nonprofits with no equity to acquire. Foundation Center tracks ~120K foundations distributing grants from endowments; PE roll-up structurally impossible.

813212.title: Voluntary Health Organizations
813212.rationale: Disease-focused charities (American Cancer Society, MS Society chapters) — 501(c)(3), donor-funded, no equity. Federated structures (e.g., United Way) already consolidated where viable.

813219.title: Other Grantmaking and Giving Services
813219.rationale: DAFs, federated giving, philanthropic intermediaries — predominantly 501(c)(3). DAF sponsors (Fidelity Charitable etc.) hold ~$230B AUM but are nonprofits, not acquirable.

813311.title: Human Rights Organizations
813311.rationale: ACLU, Amnesty chapters, civil rights groups — 501(c)(3)/(c)(4) advocacy nonprofits funded by donations and grants. No private equity ownership possible.

813312.title: Environment, Conservation and Wildlife Organizations
813312.rationale: Land trusts, conservancies, environmental advocacy — 501(c)(3) with restricted assets and conservation easements that cannot be transferred to for-profit owners.

813319.title: Other Social Advocacy Organizations
813319.rationale: Single-issue advocacy nonprofits (animal welfare, taxpayer groups, etc.) — 501(c)(3)/(c)(4) structure precludes PE acquisition. Donor-revenue model with no transferable equity.

813410.title: Civic and Social Organizations
813410.rationale: Rotary, Kiwanis, Elks, Masonic lodges, fraternities — 501(c)(7)/(c)(8)/(c)(10) member-owned mutual-benefit nonprofits. Membership dues fund operations; no equity to roll up.

813910.title: Business Associations
813910.rationale: Chambers of commerce, trade associations — 501(c)(6) member-funded nonprofits. Adjacent for-profit AMC (association management company) services are PE-investable but classified under NAICS 561 (Admin Services), not 813.

813920.title: Professional Organizations
813920.rationale: Bar associations, medical societies, AICPA — 501(c)(6) professional nonprofits. CE/certification revenue is real but flows to nonprofit; no equity transferable to PE.

813930.title: Labor Unions and Similar Labor Organizations
813930.rationale: Unions — 501(c)(5) tax-exempt, governed by LMRDA/DOL Form LM-2. Member-owned; PE acquisition legally impossible and politically unthinkable.

813940.title: Political Organizations
813940.rationale: 527 organizations, PACs, party committees — FEC-regulated, contribution-funded, prohibited from for-profit equity structure. Not investable.

813990.title: Other Similar Organizations (except Business, Professional, Labor, and Political Organizations)
813990.rationale: Condo/homeowner associations, parent-teacher orgs, athletic associations — mix of 501(c)(3)/(c)(4)/(c)(7) nonprofits and HOAs governed by state nonprofit law. Not PE-investable as a class.

814110.title: Private Households
814110.rationale: Private households employing domestic staff (nannies, cooks, gardeners, housekeepers). NOT INVESTABLE — households are not commercial enterprises, no acquisition possible. Census tracks for labor statistics only. Adjacent investable categories (placement agencies, home services) are in NAICS 561311/811/621610, not here.

921110.title: Executive Offices
921110.rationale: Public-sector executive offices (President, Governors, Mayors). Tax-funded government entities, not commercial firms. Zero private establishments per Census CBP (NAICS 92 entirely excluded from County Business Patterns). Not acquirable by PE under any structure. AI opportunity is GovTech vendor sales (separate NAICS 5415/5417), not roll-up of the offices themselves.

921120.title: Legislative Bodies
921120.rationale: Congress, state legislatures, city councils. Sovereign public bodies — cannot be acquired, consolidated, or operated for profit. Zero private firms. No PE roll-up thesis possible.

921130.title: Public Finance Activities
921130.rationale: Government Treasury, tax collection, public debt management (e.g., IRS, state treasurers, municipal finance offices). Sovereign function, not commercial. Private-sector adjacents (muni-advisory, bond underwriting) sit in NAICS 5239, not here. Not investable.

921140.title: Executive and Legislative Offices, Combined
921140.rationale: Combined exec/legislative bodies (typical at small-municipality level — town councils with mayor). Public entities, tax-funded, not acquirable. Zero PE relevance.

921150.title: American Indian and Alaska Native Tribal Governments
921150.rationale: Sovereign tribal government bodies. Tribal enterprises (casinos, energy, construction) are classified under their operating NAICS, not 921150. The governments themselves are sovereign — not acquirable. Note: even tribal enterprises have sovereign-immunity complications that deter PE.

921190.title: Other General Government Support
921190.rationale: Catch-all for government support functions (personnel, procurement, general services within govt). Public-sector establishments only. Private GovCon contractors providing similar services classify under 5416/5614/etc., not 921190. Zero roll-up viability here.

922110.title: Courts
922110.rationale: Government-operated judicial branch (federal/state/local courts). Not for-profit, not acquirable. BJS Court Statistics Project documents ~100% public ownership. Zero PE roll-up viability.

922120.title: Police Protection
922120.rationale: Government police departments (municipal, state, federal). Not acquirable. Private security guards classified separately to 561612 (Security Guards & Patrol). Zero PE viability in 922120.
sources: BJS Law Enforcement Management & Administrative Statistics (LEMAS)

922130.title: Legal Counsel and Prosecution
922130.rationale: Government prosecutors, public defenders, attorneys general. Public-sector only. Private law firms classified to 5411 (Legal Services). Zero PE viability.
sources: BJS Census of State Court Prosecutors

922140.title: Correctional Institutions
922140.rationale: Public prisons/jails operated by federal/state/local government. PRIVATE prison operators (GEO Group, CoreCivic) are classified to 561210/561612, NOT 922140. 922140 itself has zero PE roll-up viability.

922150.title: Parole Offices and Probation Offices
922150.rationale: Government parole/probation agencies. Electronic monitoring vendors (e.g., BI Inc, Sentinel) sell into these but are classified to 561621/334290. Zero PE viability in 922150 itself.

922160.title: Fire Protection
922160.rationale: Municipal/government fire departments and volunteer fire districts. Not acquirable. Private fire suppression contractors and inspection firms are in 561621/238220. Zero PE viability.

922190.title: Other Justice, Public Order, and Safety Activities
922190.rationale: Government emergency management, coroner offices, civil defense. Public-sector only. Private emergency services/911 dispatch tech vendors classified elsewhere (5415, 561621). Zero PE viability.

923110.title: Administration of Education Programs
923110.rationale: Government agency (state/local education departments). Not PE-acquirable.

923120.title: Administration of Public Health Programs
923120.rationale: Government public health departments. Not PE-acquirable.

923130.title: Administration of Human Resource Programs (except Education, Public Health, and Veterans' Affairs Programs)
923130.rationale: Government HR/welfare admin agencies. Not PE-acquirable.

924110.title: Administration of Air and Water Resource and Solid Waste Management Programs
924110.rationale: Public-sector government function (EPA, state DEQ agencies). Not investable; PE roll-up impossible. Adjacent private environmental consulting/remediation services sit under NAICS 5416/5629, not here.

924120.title: Administration of Conservation Programs
924120.rationale: Public-sector government function (USFWS, NRCS, state conservation depts, parks admin). Not investable; PE roll-up impossible. Private land-management or forestry services sit under NAICS 113/115, not here.

925120.title: Administration of Urban Planning and Community and Rural Development

926110.title: Administration of General Economic Programs
926110.rationale: Public-sector government agency (Commerce/Treasury/state econ-dev). Not investable; no private firms. PE/AI roll-up N/A.

926120.title: Regulation and Administration of Transportation Programs
926120.rationale: Government regulators (DOT, FAA, state DMV/PUC). Public sector only; no private ownership possible.

926130.title: Regulation and Administration of Communications, Electric, Gas, and Other Utilities
926130.rationale: Government utility regulators (FCC, FERC, state PUCs). Public sector; not roll-up eligible.

926140.title: Regulation of Agricultural Marketing and Commodities
926140.rationale: Government ag regulators (USDA AMS, CFTC, state ag boards). Public sector; no investable firms.

926150.title: Regulation, Licensing, and Inspection of Miscellaneous Commercial Sectors
926150.rationale: Government licensing/inspection bodies (state professional boards, ABC). Public sector; not investable.

927110.title: Space Research and Technology
927110.rationale: NASA and federal space research agencies only. NOT INVESTABLE — public sector entity. Private space industry (SpaceX, Rocket Lab, Planet Labs, etc.) classified under NAICS 336414 (Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Mfg) and 517410 (Satellite Telecom), NOT here. This code is exclusively government civilian space programs.

928110.title: National Security
928110.rationale: Federal government (DoD, intelligence agencies). Not investable — public sector, no private firms to roll up.

928120.title: International Affairs
928120.rationale: Federal government (State Dept, embassies, foreign aid). Not investable — public sector, zero private rollup surface.
